<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**
**Define an airfoil. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the following terms used in airfoil nomenclature:**
**(i) Chord line, (ii) Mean camber line, (iii) Thickness, (iv) Camber, (v) Leading edge, (vi) Trailing edge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise, exam-ready):**

An **airfoil** is a 2D cross-section of a wing or blade shaped to generate lift when air flows past it at an angle of attack $\alpha$.

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| Chord line (c) | Straight line joining the leading edge to the trailing edge |
| Mean camber line ($y_c$) | Locus of midpoints between upper and lower surfaces; a curved line for cambered airfoils, straight for symmetric ones |
| Thickness (t) | Maximum perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces |
| Camber (C) | Maximum perpendicular distance between mean camber line and chord line |
| Leading edge (LE) | Forward-most rounded tip of the airfoil |
| Trailing edge (TE) | Rear-most point of the airfoil (usually sharp) |

[Examiner: draw the standard labelled airfoil diagram with x and y axes, chord, camber, thickness, LE, TE, and angle of attack — 1 mark reserved for diagram correctness.]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]**
**What is meant by NACA 4-digit airfoil designation? Decode the airfoil NACA 2415 and state its camber, location of max camber, and thickness ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NACA 4-digit system** is a standard naming convention developed by the U.S. National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics to describe subsonic airfoil shapes using only four digits **MPXX**:
- **M** → maximum camber as % of chord
- **P** → location of maximum camber in tenths of chord
- **XX** → maximum thickness as % of chord

For **NACA 2415**:

| Digit | Value | Decoded Quantity | Result |
|-------|-------|------------------|--------|
| M = 2 | 2% | Maximum camber | $C/c = 0.02$ |
| P = 4 | 4/10 | Location of max camber | $p = 0.40c$ (at 40% chord) |
| XX = 15 | 15% | Maximum thickness | $t/c = 0.15$ |

Thus the NACA 2415 is a cambered airfoil with 2% camber located at 40% chord and 15% thickness ratio — a typical thick, cambered wing section suitable for general aviation aircraft.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Airfoil Nomenclature:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the normalized assumption** (that x, y, $y_c$, $y_t$ are dimensionless x/c, y/c). This single omission costs 1–2 marks in nearly every 14-mark answer.
> 2. **Wrongly identifying the NACA code:** Students frequently misread "NACA 2412" as camber = 24%, thickness = 12%. The first digit is the **camber percentage** (1–2 digits), not a count of 2s.
> 3. **Sign errors in $y_\ell$:** Lower surface is $y_c - y_t$, which is **negative** for a cambered airfoil placed with positive y up. Plotting it as positive loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not sketching the airfoil with all labels:** A 14-mark question nearly always demands a diagram; a well-labelled sketch is worth a minimum of **2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in final numerical answers (e.g., leaving y_t = 0.087 instead of 0.087 m).

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, define the following terms used in airfoil nomenclature: chord line, mean camber line, upper and lower surface, leading edge, trailing edge, angle of attack, and camber. Also explain the difference between a **symmetric** and a **cambered** airfoil with examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a **NACA 2412** airfoil of chord **c = 2 m**, compute:
- (i) The mean camber line ordinate at $x = 0.5$ m and at $x = 1.5$ m. [3 marks]
- (ii) The thickness distribution ordinate at $x = 0.5$ m. [2 marks]
- (iii) The upper and lower surface ordinates at $x = 0.5$ m. [2 marks]

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

[Draw a clean airfoil diagram with the following labels — earns 2 marks purely for the sketch:]

**Diagram elements to include:**
- LE (rounded tip on the left) and TE (sharp tip on the right)
- Chord line c (horizontal straight line from LE to TE)
- Mean camber line $y_c$ (dashed curved line bowing upward)
- Upper surface $y_u$ (curve above chord) and lower surface $y_\ell$ (curve below chord)
- Angle of attack $\alpha$ between chord and $V_\infty$ vector
- y_t arrow showing thickness at mid-chord
- Maximum camber point at $p \cdot c$ on the camber line

**Definitions [3 marks]:**

> (i) **Chord line:** Imaginary straight line joining the leading edge and trailing edge of the airfoil; its length is the **chord c**.

> (ii) **Mean camber line:** Line traced by points that lie exactly midway between the upper and lower surface ordinates at every chordwise station. Mathematically, $y_c(x) = (y_u(x) + y_\ell(x))/2$.

> (iii) **Upper surface:** The contour on the high-pressure (suction) side, generally of greater curvature.

> (iv) **Lower surface:** The contour on the high-pressure side, generally flatter than the upper surface for cambered airfoils.

> (v) **Leading edge:** The foremost point of the airfoil, with the smallest radius of curvature of any point on the airfoil.

> (vi) **Trailing edge:** The rearmost point, ideally of zero thickness for sharp stall characteristics.

> (vii) **Angle of attack ($\alpha$):** The angle between the chord line and the freestream velocity vector $V_\infty$. It is the principal parameter controlling lift.

> (viii) **Camber:** The maximum distance between the mean camber line and the chord line, non-dimensionalized as $C/c$.

**Symmetric vs Cambered Airfoil [2 marks]:**

| Feature | Symmetric Airfoil | Cambered Airfoil |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| Mean camber line | Coincides with chord line ($y_c \equiv 0$) | Curves away from chord ($y_c \neq 0$) |
| Upper and lower surfaces | Mirror images | Different shapes |
| $C_L$ at $\alpha = 0°$ | Zero | Positive (non-zero) |
| Typical use | Missile fins, helicopter rotor blades, vertical tails | Aircraft main wings, propellers, wind-turbine blades |
| Example NACA | NACA 0012 | NACA 2412, NACA 4415 |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Decoding NACA 2412 with c = 2 m:**
$m = 0.02$, $p = 0.40$, $t = 0.12$, $c = 2$ m.

**(i) Camber line at x = 0.5 m → x/c = 0.25:**
Since $0.25 < p = 0.40$, use the **forward** formula:
$y_c = (m/p^2)(2px - x^2)$ in normalized form: $y_c/c = (0.02/0.16)(2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.25 - 0.0625)$
$= 0.125 \times (0.20 - 0.0625) = 0.125 \times 0.1375 = 0.01719$

So $y_c = 0.01719 \times 2 = 0.0344$ m. **[1.5 marks]**

**Camber line at x = 1.5 m → x/c = 0.75:**
Since $0.75 > p = 0.40$, use the **aft** formula:
$y_c/c = (0.02/(0.6)^2)[(1 - 0.8) + 2(0.4)(0.75) - 0.5625]$
$= (0.02/0.36)[0.2 + 0.6 - 0.5625] = 0.0556 \times 0.2375 = 0.0132$

So $y_c = 0.0132 \times 2 = 0.0264$ m. **[1.5 marks]**

**(ii) Thickness at x = 0.5 m → x/c = 0.25:**
$y_t/c = 5(0.12)[0.2969\sqrt{0.25} - 0.1260(0.25) - 0.3516(0.0625) + 0.2843(0.01563) - 0.1015(0.00391)]$

Bracket terms:
- $0.2969 \times 0.5 = 0.14845$
- $-0.1260 \times 0.25 = -0.03150$
- $-0.3516 \times 0.0625 = -0.02198$
- $+0.2843 \times 0.01563 = +0.00444$
- $-0.1015 \times 0.00391 = -0.00040$

Sum = $0.14845 - 0.03150 - 0.02198 + 0.00444 - 0.00040 = 0.09901$

$y_t/c = 0.6 \times 0.09901 = 0.0594$ → $y_t = 0.1188$ m. **[2 marks]**

**(iii) Upper and lower surface ordinates at x = 0.5 m:**
$y_u = y_c + y_t = 0.0344 + 0.1188 = 0.1532$ m
$y_\ell = y_c - y_t = 0.0344 - 0.1188 = -0.0844$ m **[2 marks]**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **NACA 4-digit airfoil designation system** with suitable examples. Derive the expression for the mean camber line of a NACA 4-digit airfoil for the forward section (0 ≤ x ≤ pc).

**(b) [7 marks]** A **NACA 4415** airfoil has a chord of **3 m**. Compute the following at $x = 1.2$ m:
- (i) Mean camber line ordinate [3 marks]
- (ii) Thickness ordinate [2 marks]
- (iii) Upper and lower surface coordinates [2 marks]

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**NACA 4-digit system explanation [3 marks]:**

The NACA 4-digit designation is a compact, three-parameter description of a subsonic airfoil family. Each airfoil is encoded as **MPXX** where:
- **M** = maximum camber as a percentage of chord; the first digit, ranging 0–9 (symmetric airfoils start with 0, e.g., NACA 0012)
- **P** = location along the chord where maximum camber occurs, in tenths of chord (so digit 4 means at 40% chord)
- **XX** = maximum thickness as a percentage of chord, ranging 06–21 typically

**Examples [1 mark]:**
- **NACA 0012:** $m = 0$, $p = 0.4$ (irrelevant for symmetric), $t/c = 0.12$ — symmetric
- **NACA 2412:** $m = 0.02$, $p = 0.4$, $t/c = 0.12$ — moderately cambered, common general-aviation wing
- **NACA 4415:** $m = 0.04$, $p = 0.4$, $t/c = 0.15$ — highly cambered, thick (typical trainer aircraft wing)
- **NACA 23012:** cambered 2%, max camber at 15% chord, 12% thick (historical — predecessor of airfoils used in the P-51 Mustang)

**Derivation of forward mean camber line [3 marks]:**

See full derivation in Section 3.1. Key steps to present:
1. Assume $y_c = A x + B x^2$ (parabola through origin)
2. Apply three conditions: $y_c(0) = 0$, $dy_c/dx \vert_{x=0}$ = slope at LE; $dy_c/dx \vert_{x=p} = 0$ (peak), $y_c(p) = m$ (max camber value)
3. Solve to get: $A = 2m/p$, $B = -m/p^2$
4. Final result: $y_c(x) = \dfrac{m}{p^2}(2px - x^2)$ for $0 \le x \le pc$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Decoding NACA 4415 with c = 3 m:**
$m = 0.04$, $p = 0.40$, $t = 0.15$, $c = 3$ m.
Station: $x = 1.2$ m → $x/c = 0.4$.

**Observation:** $x/c = 0.4$ coincides exactly with the peak-camber location $p = 0.4$.

**(i) Camber line at x = 1.2 m (x/c = 0.4):**
Since $x/c = p$, this is the **maximum camber point**. From the forward formula:
$y_c/c = (0.04/0.16)(2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.4 - 0.16) = 0.25 \times (0.32 - 0.16) = 0.25 \times 0.16 = 0.04$

So $y_c = 0.04 \times 3 = 0.12$ m — by definition, this is the maximum camber. **[3 marks]**

**(ii) Thickness at x = 1.2 m (x/c = 0.4):**
$y_t/c = 5(0.15)[0.2969\sqrt{0.4} - 0.1260(0.4) - 0.3516(0.16) + 0.2843(0.064) - 0.1015(0.0256)]$

From the pre-computed values in Section 3.3 (we computed this exact bracket for NACA 2412 — same x/c):
Bracket = $0.1878 - 0.0504 - 0.0563 + 0.0182 - 0.0026 = 0.0967$

$y_t/c = 0.75 \times 0.0967 = 0.0725$ → $y_t = 0.2176$ m. **[2 marks]**

**(iii) Upper and lower surface ordinates at x = 1.2 m:**
$y_u = y_c + y_t = 0.12 + 0.2176 = 0.3376$ m
$y_\ell = y_c - y_t = 0.12 - 0.2176 = -0.0976$ m **[2 marks]**

**Physical check:** Maximum thickness of a NACA 4415 is 15% of chord = 0.45 m, occurring at roughly 30% chord. At $x/c = 0.4$ (just past max thickness), the thickness is 0.2176 m ≈ 7.25% of chord, which is reasonable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Airfoil Nomenclature**

- **Airfoil:** 2D shape designed to produce lift; defined by its cross-section.
- **Chord line (c):** Straight LE-to-TE line; foundation of all coordinates.
- **Mean camber line ($y_c$):** Mid-line of the airfoil; describes camber.
- **Thickness line ($y_t$):** Half the upper-lower distance; describes thickness.
- **NACA 4-digit code MPXX:** $m = M/100$, $p = P/10$, $t = XX/100$.
- **NACA 4-digit camber equations** (always non-dimensionalize first!):
  - Forward ($x \le pc$): $y_c = (m/p^2)(2px - x^2)$
  - Aft ($x \ge pc$): $y_c = m(1-2p+2px-x^2)/(1-p)^2$
- **NACA 4-digit thickness equation:** $y_t = 5t\,[0.2969\sqrt{x} - 0.1260x - 0.3516x^2 + 0.2843x^3 - 0.1015x^4]$
- **Surface ordinates:** $y_u = y_c + y_t$, $y_\ell = y_c - y_t$ (lower is negative for cambered airfoils plotted in standard y-up frame).
- **Symmetric airfoil** = NACA 00XX, $m = 0$ → zero lift at $\alpha = 0°$.
- **Cambered airfoil** = NACA MPXX with $M \ge 1$ → positive lift at $\alpha = 0°$.
- **Trailing edge** of NACA 4-digit is *open* (residual thickness 0.0021c) — must mention this when asked.
- **Always specify that x, y are normalized to chord c** before applying NACA equations.
- **Standard thickness ratio range:** subsonic 0.10–0.18; supersonic 0.03–0.05; laminar-flow 0.10–0.12.
- **Standard camber location p:** typically 0.4 for 4-digit airfoils; 0.15 for the older 5-digit "230XX" series.
- **Maximum thickness location** (4-digit): occurs at $x/c \approx 0.30$, not at the trailing edge.
- **Maximum camber location:** by definition, exactly at $x/c = p$.
- **Units check:** all NACA inputs and outputs are dimensionless; multiply by c for physical units.
- **For KTU:** always draw a labelled diagram in 14-mark answers; always state assumptions; always carry units to the final line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Aerodynamics to Illustrate Lift and Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Aerodynamics to Illustrate Lift and Drag # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Aerodynamics? **Aerodynamics** is the branch of fluid dynamics that studies the motion of air (and other gaseous fluids) and the forces acting on bodies moving relative to the air, or stationary bodies in a moving airstream. It is the foundational science behind the design of aircraft, rockets, missiles, automobiles, bridges, and even skyscrapers. For an aerospace engineer, the two most important aerodynamic outputs are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lift ($L$)** — The aerodynamic force component perpendicular to the relative wind (or direction of motion). This is the force that holds an aircraft in the sky. > > **Drag ($D$)** — The aerodynamic force component parallel to the relative wind, opposing the motion. This is the "price" the aircraft pays to generate lift. ## 1.2 Fundamental Properties of the Atmosphere Aerodynamics cannot be discussed without first understanding the fluid in which flight occurs — **Earth's atmosphere**, a compressible, viscous, real gas. | Property | Symbol | Standard Value at Sea Level | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Density | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | Mass per unit volume; directly affects inertia of airflow | | Pressure | $P$ | **101 325 Pa (1 atm)** | Static pressure of the undisturbed air | | Temperature | $T$ | **288.15 K (15 °C)** | Reference for thermodynamic relations | | Speed of sound | $a$ | **340.29 m/s** | Reference for compressibility effects | | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | **1.789 × 10⁻⁵ N·s/m²** | Governs shear stress and boundary-layer growth | The relationships between these properties are governed by the **Ideal Gas Law**: $$P = \rho R T$$ where $R = 287.05\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ is the specific gas constant for air. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Kite and the River Imagine you are standing in a shallow river holding a flat wooden board submerged at a slight tilt against the flowing current. You will feel two distinct forces on the board: - A force **pushing the board up toward the surface** (perpendicular to flow) — this is analogous to **Lift**. - A force **pushing the board downstream** (parallel to flow) — this is **Drag**. Now tilt the board to a steeper angle — the upward push becomes stronger, but the downstream push also grows. This is exactly what happens to an aircraft wing. By changing the **angle of attack** (the tilt), the pilot trades one force for the other. The art of aerodynamic design is maximizing the lift while minimizing the drag. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this analogy useful?** It grounds the abstract concepts of $L$ and $D$ in tactile intuition. A student should remember: *Lift is "up," drag is "back," and the wing's tilt (angle of attack) controls the balance between them.* ## 1.4 The Concept of Relative Wind A stationary wing in a moving airstream is **aerodynamically identical** to a wing moving through still air. This is **Galilean relativity** applied to fluids, and it is the basis of the **relative wind** concept: $$\vec{V}_{\text{relative}} = \vec{V}_{\text{freestream}} - \vec{V}_{\text{body}}$$ All aerodynamic forces and moments are functions of this *relative* velocity, not the absolute motion of the body in space. ## 1.5 Streamlines, Streamtubes, and the Steady-Flow Assumption In most KTU-level problems, we assume **steady, incompressible, inviscid flow** unless otherwise stated: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady** → flow properties at a point do not change with time. > > **Incompressible** → density $\rho$ is constant ($M < 0.3$). > > **Inviscid** → viscosity is neglected (a simplification for ideal flow analysis). > > **Irrotational** → no net rotation of fluid elements (valid outside the boundary layer). A **streamline** is a curve whose tangent at every point is aligned with the local velocity vector. A bundle of adjacent streamlines forms a **streamtube**, which behaves like an invisible pipe through which mass continuously flows. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Aerodynamic Force Generation Aerodynamic forces arise from **two distinct physical mechanisms**, and understanding both is essential: ### Pillar 1: Pressure Forces (Bernoulli / Euler Mechanism) - The airflow is **deflected** around the body. - The **curvature of streamlines** creates a pressure differential. - Higher velocity → lower pressure (Bernoulli's principle). - The integrated pressure difference over the body's surface yields a net force. ### Pillar 2: Viscous Shear Forces (Friction Mechanism) - Air molecules in contact with the surface "stick" to it (the **no-slip condition**). - A thin layer of air — the **boundary layer** — develops a velocity gradient from zero (at the wall) to free-stream (at the edge). - The shear stress in this layer, when integrated over the surface, yields **skin-friction drag**. > [!NOTE] > For a typical commercial airliner, pressure forces contribute roughly **60–80%** of the total drag, while viscous friction contributes the remaining **20–40%**. This balance depends strongly on the **Reynolds number** and the body shape (bluff vs. streamlined). ## 2.2 Bernoulli's Equation — The Heart of Subsonic Aerodynamics For **steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline**, Bernoulli's equation states: $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$ For an airfoil, the $gz$ term is negligible (thin atmosphere, near-horizontal flight), giving: $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = \text{constant}$$ This tells us: **as velocity increases, static pressure decreases**, and vice versa. A wing with more camber on its upper surface causes the air to travel faster over the top → lower pressure above → net upward force = **LIFT**. ## 2.3 Newton's Third Law — The Equal-and-Opposite Viewpoint The pressure-gradient view (Bernoulli) and the **deflection view** (Newton) are two sides of the same coin: > [!IMPORTANT] > An airfoil generates lift by **deflecting the oncoming air downward** (the action). By Newton's third law, the air exerts an equal and opposite force on the wing **upward** (the reaction = lift). This is why the **momentum-change theory** (control-volume analysis) is also a valid approach to compute lift and drag — useful especially for simple geometries like flat plates and wing-in-ground-effect vehicles. ## 2.4 Dimensional Analysis and the Universal Form of Lift/Drag Equations Why are the lift and drag equations written in the specific form they are? Because of **dimensional homogeneity** and **Buckingham Pi theorem**. The lift $L$ depends on: $\rho$, $V$, $S$ (reference area), $\mu$ (viscosity), $a$ (speed of sound), and $\alpha$ (angle of attack). Dimensional analysis reduces these to two dimensionless groups — the **Reynolds number** $Re$ and **Mach number** $M$ — plus the angle of attack: $$L = q_\infty \, S \, C_L(\alpha, Re, M)$$ where $$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^{2}$$ is the **dynamic pressure**, and $C_L$ is the **lift coefficient**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every formula you are expected to know for Module 1 of PEICT751. Memorize the symbols and the physical meaning of each. | # | Formula | Name | Use / Context | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \rho R T$ | Ideal Gas Law | Relates atmospheric properties | | 2 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of Sound | Compressibility reference; $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | | 3 | $M = V / a$ | Mach Number | Measures compressibility; $M < 0.3$ → incompressible | | 4 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli (incompressible) | Pressure-velocity trade-off in flow | | 5 | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^{2}$ | Dynamic Pressure | Kinetic energy per unit volume of the flow | | 6 | $C_P = (P - P_\infty) / q_\infty$ | Pressure Coefficient | Dimensionless pressure; fundamental in wind-tunnel data | | 7 | $L = q_\infty \, S \, C_L$ | Lift Equation | Force perpendicular to free-stream | | 8 | $D = q_\infty \, S \, C_D$ | Drag Equation | Force parallel to free-stream | | 9 | $L/D = C_L / C_D$ | Aerodynamic Efficiency | Quality metric for an airfoil/wing | | 10 | $Re = \rho V c / \mu$ | Reynolds Number | Ratio of inertia to viscous forces | | 11 | $C_L = C_{L_0} + C_{L_\alpha}\,\alpha$ | Linear Lift Curve | Valid in the linear (pre-stall) regime | | 12 | $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^{2}$ | Drag Polar | Parabolic drag buildup with lift | > **Units reminder:** $L$ and $D$ in **newtons (N)**; $S$ in **m²**; $V$ in **m/s**; $\rho$ in **kg/m³**; $q$ in **Pa (N/m²)**; coefficients $C_L, C_D, C_P$ are **dimensionless**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not abstract — they are the daily working tools of: - **Aircraft performance engineers** computing stall speed, range, and endurance. - **Wind-tunnel test engineers** who reduce raw pressure data to $C_P$ plots. - **Missile and rocket designers** computing axial and normal forces for control. - **Automotive aerodynamicists** minimizing $C_D$ to improve fuel economy. - **Civil engineers** computing wind loads on bridges, towers, and tall buildings. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Pressure Coefficient $C_P$ We start from Bernoulli's equation applied between a point in the **free stream** (far upstream, where velocity is $V_\infty$ and pressure is $P_\infty$) and a general point in the flow (where velocity is $V$ and pressure is $P$): $$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate the static pressure $P$:** $$P = P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(V_\infty^{2} - V^{2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Subtract $P_\infty$ from both sides:** $$P - P_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(V_\infty^{2} - V^{2}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Divide both sides by the dynamic pressure $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}$:** $$C_P = \frac{P - P_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{V}{V_\infty}\right)^{2}$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{\,C_P = 1 - \left(\frac{V}{V_\infty}\right)^{2}\,}$$ **Physical interpretation:** - At a **stagnation point** (e.g., the leading edge of a wing), $V = 0$, giving $C_P = 1$ (maximum pressure). - Far from the body in the **free stream**, $V = V_\infty$, giving $C_P = 0$ (the reference). - On the **upper surface** of a lifting wing, $V > V_\infty$, giving $C_P < 0$ (suction). - The more negative $C_P$ is on the upper surface, the stronger the lift. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Lift and Drag Equations from a Control-Volume (Momentum) Analysis Consider a control volume $CV$ enclosing an airfoil. Apply the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** for steady flow to the $x$-component (drag direction) of momentum: $$\sum F_x = \oint_{CS} \rho V_x (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA$$ **Step 1 — Define the freestream condition:** The air enters the control volume at velocity $V_\infty$ in the $x$-direction. It leaves with a slightly modified velocity $(V_\infty + \Delta V_x)$ due to the body's presence. **Step 2 — Recognize that net pressure force on the closed control surface (where it is far from the body) is zero:** In the freestream, $P = P_\infty$ on the far-field boundary. The only **net force** on the control volume is the reaction from the body, which equals the drag $D$ (opposite direction). **Step 3 — Apply the linearised momentum equation:** The drag is the rate of change of $x$-momentum of the fluid: $$D = \dot{m}\,\Delta V_x \quad \text{(for a small disturbance)}$$ For a wing of planform area $S$, the mass flow rate intercepted per unit area is $\rho V_\infty$: $$D \propto \rho V_\infty^{2} S$$ **Step 4 — Dimensional consistency check:** $\rho$ has units $\text{kg/m}^3$, $V^2$ has $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$, $S$ has $\text{m}^2$, so the product has units: $$[\rho V^2 S] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}^2} \cdot \text{m}^2 = \frac{\text{kg·m}}{\text{s}^2} = \text{N} \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Absorb all complex flow physics into a dimensionless coefficient:** $$\boxed{\,D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S\, C_D\,}$$ The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is included **by convention** (so that $C_D$ values for simple shapes are of order unity, not 2). **Step 6 — Apply identical logic in the $z$-direction (lift direction):** The lift arises from the downward deflection of the airstream (vertical momentum flux): $$\boxed{\,L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S\, C_L\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $\tfrac{1}{2}$?** Historically, aerodynamicists wanted a single non-dimensional coefficient. The choice of $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ as the reference (dynamic pressure $q$) is universal because it represents the kinetic energy per unit volume of the flow. This makes $C_L$ and $C_D$ depend only on shape, $\alpha$, $Re$, and $M$ — *not* on speed or altitude. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Linear Lift Curve $C_L(\alpha)$ For small angles of attack (typically $\alpha < 15^\circ$ for a well-designed wing), experimental and theoretical work (thin-airfoil theory, panel methods) shows that $C_L$ is a linear function of $\alpha$: $$C_L = C_{L_0} + \frac{dC_L}{d\alpha}\,\alpha$$ **Step 1 — Boundary condition at $\alpha = 0$:** At zero angle of attack, a **cambered** airfoil still produces some lift because of its curvature. Denote this as $C_{L_0}$ (often $C_{L_0} \approx 0.2$ to $0.5$ for a typical cambered section). **Step 2 — Thin-airfoil theory result:** For a symmetric airfoil ($C_{L_0} = 0$), Prandtl's lifting-line theory gives: $$\frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} = 2\pi \quad (\text{per radian})$$ Converting to degrees: $$\frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} = 2\pi \times \frac{\pi}{180} \approx 0.11\ \text{per degree}$$ This is the famous **$2\pi$-per-radian** lift-curve slope — a cornerstone of low-speed aerodynamics. **Step 3 — Stall limit:** Above the **critical angle of attack** $\alpha_{\text{stall}}$ (typically $15°$ to $20°$), the flow separates from the upper surface, $C_L$ peaks, and then drops sharply. The linear equation is **no longer valid**. **Result:** $$\boxed{\,C_L = C_{L_0} + 2\pi\,\alpha\ \text{(radians, symmetric airfoil)}\,}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Parabolic Drag Polar $C_D(C_L)$ A fundamental result of finite-wing theory (Prandtl's lifting-line, 1918) is that the total drag of a wing is the sum of: - **Parasite (zero-lift) drag** $C_{D_0}$ — friction + form drag at $C_L = 0$. - **Induced drag** $C_{D_i} = K C_L^{2}$ — drag due to the wing producing lift (wingtip vortices). **Step 1 — Write the induced-drag coefficient:** For a finite wing of aspect ratio $AR = b^2/S$, the induced-drag factor is: $$K = \frac{1}{\pi \, e \, AR}$$ where $e$ is the **Oswald efficiency factor** ($0 < e \le 1$, typically $0.85$ to $0.95$). **Step 2 — Sum the two contributions:** $$C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the maximum $L/D$ ratio:** To find the design point of maximum aerodynamic efficiency, set $d(L/D)/dC_L = 0$: $$\frac{d}{dC_L}\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^{2}}\right) = 0$$ Solving: $$C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}}$$ And the maximum lift-to-drag ratio is: $$\boxed{\,\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K\, C_{D_0}}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > This result is **the** central equation in aircraft performance. The cruise condition that maximizes range or endurance is the one that achieves $(L/D)_{\max}$. For a Boeing 747, $(L/D)_{\max} \approx 18$; for a sailplane, $(L/D)_{\max} \approx 60$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Aerodynamic Force System on an Airfoil The following Mermaid block diagram captures the relationship between the freestream flow, the airfoil geometry, and the resulting force/moment system. This is the **canonical "free-body" picture** of an airfoil in a uniform flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Freestream V_infty
P_infty rho_infty] --> B[Relative Wind
Hits Leading Edge] B --> C{Angle of Attack alpha} C --> D[Upper Surface
Accelerated Flow
Low Pressure C_P lt 0] C --> E[Lower Surface
Decelerated Flow
High Pressure C_P gt 0] D --> F[Pressure Integration
over Surface] E --> F F --> G[Net Aerodynamic Force R] G --> H[Resolved into
L perpendicular to V_infty] G --> I[Resolved into
D parallel to V_infty] H --> J[Report L = 0.5 rho V^2 S C_L] I --> K[Report D = 0.5 rho V^2 S C_D] J --> L[Aerodynamic Efficiency
L over D] K --> L ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows from the freestream condition (left) to the aerodynamic coefficients (right). Each step represents a **physical transformation** — flow → geometry → pressure field → force → resolved components → efficiency. This sequence is the same regardless of whether the body is an airfoil, a car, or a missile body. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Aerodynamic System For a complex aerospace vehicle, lift and drag are not the only outputs. The full aerodynamic system includes **stability, control, and propulsion coupling**. The following block diagram shows the high-level architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input[Input Conditions] I1[V_infty Freestream Velocity] I2[rho_infty Air Density] I3[Altitude h] I4[alpha Angle of Attack] I5[beta Sideslip Angle] I6[p q r Body Rotation Rates] end subgraph Aero[Atmosphere and Flow Model] A1[ISA Atmosphere Model
P T rho vs h] A2[Reference Quantities
q_infty S c_bar] end subgraph Vehicle[Vehicle Geometry] V1[Wing Airfoil Section] V2[Fuselage] V3[Empennage Tail] V4[Control Surfaces
Elevator Aileron Rudder] end subgraph Output[Aerodynamic Outputs] O1[L Lift Force] O2[D Drag Force] O3[Y Side Force] O4[l Rolling Moment] O5[m Pitching Moment] O6[n Yawing Moment] end I1 --> A1 I3 --> A1 A1 --> A2 I1 --> A2 I2 --> A2 I4 --> V1 I5 --> V1 V1 --> O1 V1 --> O2 V1 --> O5 V2 --> O1 V2 --> O2 V2 --> O3 V3 --> O5 V3 --> O6 V4 --> O4 V4 --> O5 V4 --> O6 I6 --> V4 A2 --> O1 A2 --> O2 ``` **Reading guide:** - The **left column** (Input) represents everything the engineer can change or measure: speed, density, attitude, rotation rates. - The **Aero block** is the "translator" that converts raw atmospheric conditions into the reference dynamic pressure and area used in the lift/drag formulas. - The **Vehicle block** is the geometry — what makes one aircraft different from another. The same $V_\infty$ and $\alpha$ acting on a different shape gives a different $C_L$. - The **Output block** lists the six aerodynamic quantities a flight-mechanics engineer needs: three forces and three moments. For Module 1, the focus is on $L$ and $D$. --- ## 4.3 Lift and Drag Coefficient Behaviour vs. Angle of Attack The following diagram captures the **qualitative behaviour** of $C_L$ and $C_D$ as functions of $\alpha$ — essential knowledge for any KTU examination. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[alpha = 0 degrees] --> B[Region Linear
C_L increases linearly
Slope 2 pi per radian] B --> C[alpha = alpha_s
Stall Angle 15 to 20 deg] C --> D{Critical Point} D -->|Below stall| E[C_L_max attained
C_D grows as C_L squared] D -->|Above stall| F[Flow separation
C_L drops sharply
C_D spikes up] F --> G[Post-stall regime
Aircraft uncontrollable] E --> H[Design Cruise Region
L over D maximum] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination tip:** Always show the **three regimes** — linear, stall, post-stall — in any sketch. A common KTU question asks to "sketch and label the lift curve" — failing to mark $C_{L_{\max}}$ and $\alpha_{\text{stall}}$ costs marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style] **Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Dynamic pressure, (ii) Pressure coefficient, (iii) Aerodynamic efficiency.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Dynamic Pressure ($q_\infty$):** The kinetic energy per unit volume of a moving fluid. It is defined as: $$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^{2}$$ *Example:* An aircraft flying at $V_\infty = 100$ m/s at sea level ($\rho_\infty = 1.225$ kg/m³) has $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(100)^2 = 6125$ Pa. **(ii) Pressure Coefficient ($C_P$):** A dimensionless measure of the local static pressure relative to the freestream dynamic pressure: $$C_P = \frac{P - P_\infty}{q_\infty}$$ *Example:* At a stagnation point on a Pitot tube, $C_P = 1$ (maximum value). **(iii) Aerodynamic Efficiency ($L/D$):** The ratio of lift to drag, also called the **glide ratio**. It indicates how much lift is produced per unit drag. $$L/D = \frac{C_L}{C_D}$$ *Example:* A modern glider has $L/D \approx 60$, meaning it can glide 60 m forward for every 1 m of altitude lost. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1] **State Bernoulli's equation for incompressible flow. Explain how it is used to qualitatively explain the generation of lift on an airfoil.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** For steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline: $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$ For aerodynamics (neglecting gravity, horizontal flow): $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = \text{constant}$$ **Application to lift generation:** 1. A cambered airfoil (or a symmetric airfoil at non-zero $\alpha$) has a longer, more curved upper surface and a shorter, flatter lower surface. 2. The airflow over the upper surface must travel **faster** to "catch up" with the flow over the lower surface (the **equal-transit-time** idea, in simplified form). 3. By Bernoulli, higher velocity on the upper surface implies **lower static pressure** ($P_{\text{upper}} < P_{\text{lower}}$). 4. The integrated pressure difference over the airfoil surface produces a net upward force — **lift**. > [!NOTE] > *Note for examiners:* The "equal transit time" argument is a **simplified, qualitative** explanation. The rigorous justification comes from **circulation theory** and the **Kutta–Joukowski theorem**, but Bernoulli alone is sufficient for a 3-mark answer at the introductory level. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 contains a 14-mark question with internal choice. Both options are given below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) Derive the expressions for lift and drag in terms of dynamic pressure, reference area, and dimensionless coefficients. Explain the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)** **(b) An aircraft wing with planform area $S = 25\ \text{m}^2$ is flying at sea level ($\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$) at a speed of $V = 80\ \text{m/s}$. The angle of attack is such that $C_L = 0.8$ and $C_D = 0.05$. Calculate: (i) the dynamic pressure, (ii) the lift force, (iii) the drag force, (iv) the lift-to-drag ratio. (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1:** Dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem) shows that the lift $L$ depends on density $\rho$, velocity $V$, reference area $S$, viscosity $\mu$, speed of sound $a$, and angle of attack $\alpha$. These five variables and three fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) reduce to two dimensionless groups: the Reynolds number $Re$ and the Mach number $M$, leaving $\alpha$ as a parameter. **Step 2:** Combining these into the standard aerodynamic reference form: $$L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S \, C_L(\alpha, Re, M)$$ **Step 3 — Physical meaning of each term:** - $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$: **Dynamic pressure** $q_\infty$ — the kinetic energy per unit volume of the airstream; depends on speed and altitude. - $S$: **Reference area** — for a wing, this is the planform area (area of the wing as seen from above). - $C_L$: **Lift coefficient** — a dimensionless number that captures the influence of shape, angle of attack, Reynolds number, and Mach number. **Step 4 — Identical logic for drag:** $$D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S \, C_D(\alpha, Re, M)$$ > **Key insight:** Both forces share the same dimensional "scaling" term $q_\infty S$, but the coefficients $C_L$ and $C_D$ are very different functions of $\alpha$ — which is why wings are designed to make $C_L \gg C_D$. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Given:** - $S = 25\ \text{m}^2$ - $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $V = 80\ \text{m/s}$ - $C_L = 0.8$, $C_D = 0.05$ **(i) Dynamic pressure:** $$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(80)^{2}$$ $$\boxed{q_\infty = 3920\ \text{Pa}}$$ **(ii) Lift force:** $$L = q_\infty S C_L = (3920)(25)(0.8)$$ $$\boxed{L = 78\,400\ \text{N} = 78.4\ \text{kN}}$$ **(iii) Drag force:** $$D = q_\infty S C_D = (3920)(25)(0.05)$$ $$\boxed{D = 4900\ \text{N} = 4.9\ \text{kN}}$$ **(iv) Lift-to-drag ratio:** $$L/D = 0.8 / 0.05 = \boxed{16}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical check:** $L/D = 16$ is typical for a transport-category aircraft in cruise. The lift of 78.4 kN must equal the weight of the aircraft at steady level flight; the drag of 4.9 kN must be overcome by the engine thrust. Both values pass the order-of-magnitude sanity check. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Choice] **(a) Explain the concepts of free-stream velocity, relative wind, and angle of attack. With the help of a neat diagram, resolve the aerodynamic resultant force into lift and drag components. (7 marks)** **(b) A NACA 2412 airfoil has $C_{L_0} = 0.25$ and lift-curve slope $a_0 = 0.11\ \text{per degree}$. If the wing is set at an angle of attack $\alpha = 5^\circ$, calculate the lift coefficient. If the same wing is flying at $V = 60\ \text{m/s}$ at an altitude where $\rho = 1.06\ \text{kg/m}^3$ with $S = 18\ \text{m}^2$, find the lift produced. (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Free-stream velocity ($V_\infty$):** The velocity of the undisturbed air far upstream of the body, where the flow is uniform and unperturbed. It is a property of the **atmosphere and the body's motion through it**, not of the body itself. **Relative wind:** The airflow as "seen" by the body. By Galilean relativity, a body moving at velocity $V$ through still air experiences the same flow as if the body were stationary in a wind of velocity $V$ blowing from the opposite direction. The relative wind is therefore a vector equal in magnitude to the body's speed and opposite in direction to the body's motion. **Angle of attack ($\alpha$):** The angle between the **chord line** of the airfoil (the straight line joining the leading and trailing edges) and the **direction of the relative wind**. **Force resolution diagram:** ``` Lift L ↑ | Pressure | Relative Wind (top) ----+----→ V_∞ | ↓ Drag D ``` The **aerodynamic resultant** $R$ is the vector sum of all pressure and shear forces integrated over the airfoil surface. For practical purposes, $R$ is resolved into two orthogonal components: - **Lift $L$** — perpendicular to $V_\infty$ (upward, opposing weight). - **Drag $D$** — parallel to $V_\infty$ (backward, opposing thrust). The angle between $R$ and $L$ is the **flow angle** or **local downwash-corrected incidence**, and the ratio $L/D = \cot \epsilon$ defines the aerodynamic efficiency. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Given:** - $C_{L_0} = 0.25$ - $a_0 = 0.11$ per degree - $\alpha = 5^\circ$ - $V = 60\ \text{m/s}$ - $\rho = 1.06\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $S = 18\ \text{m}^2$ **Step 1 — Compute $C_L$ using the linear lift equation:** $$C_L = C_{L_0} + a_0 \cdot \alpha = 0.25 + (0.11)(5)$$ $$\boxed{C_L = 0.80}$$ **Step 2 — Compute dynamic pressure:** $$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(1.06)(60)^{2}$$ $$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2}(1.06)(3600) = 1908\ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the lift:** $$L = q_\infty S C_L = (1908)(18)(0.80)$$ $$\boxed{L = 27\,475.2\ \text{N} \approx 27.48\ \text{kN}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical check:** A small general-aviation aircraft typically weighs 8–15 kN. A lift of 27.5 kN would correspond to a heavier commuter-class aircraft at a moderate angle of attack — values are internally consistent. The lower density (1.06 kg/m³ vs sea-level 1.225) indicates an altitude of roughly 500 m, which is consistent with the data. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in this module:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in dynamic pressure.** Writing $L = \rho V^2 S C_L$ is a **unit-and-equation error** that loses 2–3 marks immediately. > > 2. **Confusing $C_L$ and $L$, or $C_D$ and $D$.** The coefficient is dimensionless; the force is in newtons. Examiners mark the substitution step separately — writing "$C_L = 78.4$ kN" is a category error. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert $\alpha$ when using the $2\pi$-per-radian formula.** If the formula is given in radians, plug in radians. KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for unit mismatch in the angle. > > 4. **Not stating the assumptions.** Any use of Bernoulli's equation without stating "steady, incompressible, inviscid" loses the "definition/assumption" marks. > > 5. **Skipping the dimensional analysis in derivations.** The question "derive the lift equation" expects you to show **why** the form $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$ is the natural choice — not just write it down. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basics of Aerodynamics** - ☐ **Atmosphere:** $P = \rho R T$ with $R = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. - ☐ **Mach number:** $M = V/a$, where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. Incompressible flow valid for $M < 0.3$. - ☐ **Bernoulli (incompressible):** $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = $ constant along a streamline. - ☐ **Dynamic pressure:** $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — has units of Pa and represents flow kinetic energy per unit volume. - ☐ **Pressure coefficient:** $C_P = (P - P_\infty)/q_\infty = 1 - (V/V_\infty)^2$. Range: $C_P \le 1$. - ☐ **Lift:** $L = q_\infty S C_L$; **Drag:** $D = q_\infty S C_D$. - ☐ **Lift curve (linear regime):** $C_L = C_{L_0} + (2\pi)\alpha$ (radians). Slope $\approx 0.11$ per degree. - ☐ **Stall:** occurs at $\alpha_{\text{stall}} \approx 15°$–$20°$; $C_L$ drops sharply afterward. - ☐ **Drag polar:** $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$, where $K = 1/(\pi e AR)$. - ☐ **Maximum $L/D$:** $(L/D)_{\max} = 1/(2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}})$, achieved at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$. - ☐ **Relative wind:** equals body velocity in magnitude, opposite in direction; all $C_L, C_D$ are functions of relative wind and $\alpha$. - ☐ **Two force mechanisms:** pressure (dominant) and viscous shear (boundary layer). - ☐ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho V c / \mu$ — ratio of inertia to viscous forces; large $Re$ → inviscid assumption valid outside the boundary layer. - ☐ **Assumptions to declare in exam answers:** steady, incompressible (if $M<0.3$), inviscid (if using Bernoulli), irrotational (if using potential-flow theory). - ☐ **Sanity check:** Always verify units — $L$ in N, $S$ in m², $V$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $q$ in Pa.

topics.title: Types of Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Drag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Aerodynamic Drag

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerodynamic Drag** is the component of the resultant aerodynamic force acting on a body moving through a fluid (typically air) that is **parallel and opposite to the direction of the relative motion (free-stream velocity)**. It represents the net resistive force the fluid exerts on the body due to pressure distribution and viscous shearing stresses.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, drag is mathematically defined as the projection of the total aerodynamic force vector $\vec{R}$ onto the free-stream velocity vector $\vec{V}_{\infty}$. Mathematically,

$$D = \vec{R} \cdot \hat{e}_{V_{\infty}}$$

where $\hat{e}_{V_{\infty}}$ is the unit vector in the free-stream direction.

## 1.2 The Master Drag Equation (Non-Dimensional Form)

To compare drag across speeds, altitudes, and body sizes, engineers use the **non-dimensional drag coefficient**:

$$C_D = \frac{2D}{\rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} S_{ref}}$$

where:
- $D$ = Drag force in **Newtons (N)**
- $\rho_{\infty}$ = Free-stream air density in **kg/m³**
- $V_{\infty}$ = Free-stream velocity in **m/s**
- $S_{ref}$ = Reference area (wing planform area for aircraft) in **m²**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swimming in Honey" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a sleek dolphin and a flat paddle plate being pulled through water at the same speed.
> - The **dolphin** slips through with little resistance — it is *streamlined*. Most of the air/water flows smoothly around it, and the pressure at its back (rear) almost equals the pressure at its front. This is the goal of aerodynamic shaping.
> - The **flat plate** feels a massive "pull-back" force. Air smashes into its front face (high pressure), but the back of the plate is a near-stagnant low-pressure wake region. The imbalance **pushes** the plate backward.
> - Add a rough, sandpaper-like surface to both — now there is extra frictional rubbing along the skin. That is *skin friction drag*. Each resistance mechanism is a different "type of drag."

## 1.4 The "Big Picture" Classification Tree

> [!TIP]
> **Drag in aircraft engineering is broadly split into TWO macro-categories:**
> 1. **Parasite Drag** — Drag that exists *whether or not the wing is producing lift*. Increases roughly with the *square* of velocity.
> 2. **Induced Drag** — Drag that is a *by-product of generating lift*. Dominant at low speeds / high angle of attack. Decreases as velocity increases.
>
> **Total Drag = Parasite Drag + Induced Drag**

This split is the foundation of every drag-polar curve used in aircraft performance (Thrust Required vs. Velocity) — a staple of KTU university problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Types of Drag (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **PEICT751 — Module 1** syllabus, the following six drag types are examinable. Each is described by a distinct physical origin.

### 2.1.1 Skin Friction Drag ($D_f$ or $D_{sf}$)

- **Origin:** Tangential **viscous shear stresses** $\tau_w$ acting on the wetted surface of the body as the boundary layer develops.
- **Mechanism:** Air molecules "stick" to the surface (no-slip condition). Adjacent fluid layers slide past one another, transferring momentum — this molecular friction is the *skin friction*.
- **Mathematical form:** $D_f = \int_{S_{wet}} \tau_w \, \cos\theta \, dS$
- **Governing parameter:** **Reynolds Number**, $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$

### 2.1.2 Pressure Drag (Form Drag) ($D_p$)

- **Origin:** **Asymmetric pressure distribution** between the front (stagnation) and rear (wake) of the body.
- **Mechanism:** Air cannot completely fill in behind a bluff (non-streamlined) body → a low-pressure wake forms → net force pushes the body downstream.
- **Key insight:** Pressure drag is **dominant for bluff bodies** (flat plate, cylinder, brick); skin friction is **dominant for streamlined bodies** (airfoil, fish, dolphin).

### 2.1.3 Profile Drag ($D_{pr}$)

$$D_{pr} = D_f + D_p$$

- The drag of a **finite 2-D airfoil** (or 3-D wing in the absence of lift-induced effects) — the drag you would measure in a wind tunnel with the wing at **zero lift**.

### 2.1.4 Induced Drag ($D_i$)

- **Origin:** An *unavoidable by-product* of generating lift on a **finite-aspect-ratio wing**.
- **Mechanism:** Wingtip vortices induce a **downwash** $w$ behind the wing. This tilts the local relative wind **backward**, rotating the lift vector *aft* — the aft-component of lift is induced drag.
- **Critical equation:** $D_i = \frac{L^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} \pi b^{2} e}$
- where $b$ = wingspan, $e$ = Oswald efficiency factor (0.7–0.95 for typical aircraft).

### 2.1.5 Wave Drag ($D_w$)

- **Origin:** **Shock waves** that form on the body when local flow velocity reaches or exceeds the **speed of sound** ($M \geq 1$).
- **Mechanism:** At supersonic flight, compression and expansion shocks on the body surface create enormous pressure gradients → strong **wave drag**.
- **Threshold:** Becomes significant at **critical Mach number** $M_{cr}$ and grows rapidly toward $M = 1$ (transonic drag-rise).
- **Engineering control:** Supersonic aircraft use **supercritical airfoils, swept wings, area ruling (Whitcomb)**, and **waverider** designs.

### 2.1.6 Interference Drag ($D_{int}$)

- **Origin:** The **extra drag** generated where two aerodynamic surfaces meet (e.g., wing–fuselage junction, wing–nacelle junction).
- **Mechanism:** Junctions disrupt smooth flow, create local flow separation, and produce additional viscous and pressure losses beyond the sum of the individual parts.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** every student must memorize for KTU Module-1 problems. All cell delimiters avoid the `|` character to keep markdown tables safe.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_D = \dfrac{2D}{\rho V^{2} S}$ | Master drag-coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number — controls skin friction & flow regime | dimensionless |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Mach Number — controls compressibility / wave drag | dimensionless |
| 4 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K) | m/s |
| 5 | $C_{f,\text{laminar}} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}}$ | Local skin-friction (Blasius, laminar flat plate) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{f,\text{turbulent}} = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}$ | Local skin-friction (turbulent flat plate) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_i = \dfrac{L^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \pi b^{2} e}$ | Induced drag (elliptic lift distribution) | N |
| 8 | $C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \, AR \, e}$ | Induced drag coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S}$ | Wing Aspect Ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D_{\text{total}} = D_0 + D_i$ | Drag decomposition (parasite + induced) | N |
| 11 | $C_D = C_{D_0} + \dfrac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi AR e}$ | **Drag polar equation** — KTU favorite | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Drag Classification

- **Aircraft Design:** A commercial jet must minimize *parasite drag* (cruise at high $M$) and *induced drag* (takeoff/landing at low $V$).
- **Subsonic UAVs:** Skin friction dominates → designers use smooth composites and laminar-flow airfoils.
- **Supersonic fighters:** Wave drag is the limiting factor → use area-ruled fuselages and variable-geometry inlets.
- **Wind turbines / propellers:** Induced drag = tip-loss losses; they use winglets, end-plates, and tip-vortex diffusion.
- **Race car / Formula-1:** Even ground vehicles classify drag the same way to optimize downforce-vs-drag.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **complete worked derivations** for the three formulas most frequently set in KTU University Exam questions on Types of Drag.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Induced Drag Coefficient (from First Principles)

### Given
A finite wing of span $b$ produces a lift distribution that, by the **Helmholtz vortex theorems**, must terminate at the wingtips as two **trailing vortices** of strength $\Gamma$ (circulation). These vortices induce a downward velocity **downwash** $w$ in the plane of the wing.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Downwash
The downwash tilts the local relative wind through a small angle $\varepsilon$ (the **induced angle of attack**):

$$\varepsilon = \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{V_{\infty}}\right) \approx \frac{w}{V_{\infty}} \quad \text{(for small angles)}$$

### Step 2 — Rotation of the Lift Vector
The lift vector $\vec{L}$ is always perpendicular to the *local* relative wind, not the freestream. Because the wind is tilted down by $\varepsilon$, the lift vector itself tilts **backward** by $\varepsilon$.

The lift vector therefore has two components:

- **Vertical (true lift component):** $L' = L \cos\varepsilon \approx L$
- **Horizontal (drag component):** $D_i = L \sin\varepsilon \approx L \varepsilon$

$$D_i = L \cdot \varepsilon = L \cdot \frac{w}{V_{\infty}}$$

### Step 3 — Downwash from Biot–Savart Law
For an elliptic lift distribution, the downwash at the wing is constant in the spanwise direction:

$$w = \frac{L}{2 \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} \, b^{2}/4} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{L}{\pi \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} b^{2}/2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad w = \frac{2L}{\pi \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} b^{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Induced Drag
Combining Steps 2 and 3:

$$D_i = L \cdot \frac{w}{V_{\infty}} = L \cdot \frac{1}{V_{\infty}} \cdot \frac{2L}{\pi \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} b^{2}} = \frac{2 L^{2}}{\pi \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} b^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Non-Dimensionalize Using Reference Area
Using $C_L = \dfrac{2L}{\rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} S}$ and $AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S}$:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{D_i}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} = \frac{2 L^{2}}{\pi \rho V^{2} b^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} = \frac{L^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \frac{S}{b^{2}} = \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi \, AR}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce Efficiency Factor
For non-elliptic distributions, multiply by a factor $1/e$ where $0 \le e \le 1$ (Oswald factor):

$$\boxed{\,C_{D_i} = \frac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \, AR \, e}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for valuation:** Markers *always* check whether you remembered the $e$ factor and the $\tfrac{1}{\pi}$. A common error is writing $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / AR$ — that **loses 2 marks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Skin Friction Coefficient for a Lamininar Flat Plate (Blasius Solution)

### Step 1 — Governing Equations
For an incompressible 2-D steady boundary layer, the **Prandtl boundary-layer equations** reduce to:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Similarity Variable (Blasius)
Define $\eta = y \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{\infty}}{\nu x}}$. A similarity solution $f(\eta)$ exists such that:

$$\frac{u}{V_{\infty}} = f'(\eta)$$

The ODE is:

$$f''' + \tfrac{1}{2} f f'' = 0$$

### Step 3 — Wall Shear Stress
The wall shear is:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \mu V_{\infty} \sqrt{\frac{V_{\infty}}{\nu x}} \, f''(0)$$

The numerical value from the Blasius solution is:

$$f''(0) = 0.332$$

### Step 4 — Local Skin Friction Coefficient

$$c_f(x) = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = \frac{0.332 \mu V_{\infty} \sqrt{V_{\infty}/(\nu x)}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

Substituting $\mu/\rho = \nu$:

$$c_f(x) = \frac{2 \cdot 0.332}{\sqrt{V_{\infty} x / \nu}} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

### Step 5 — Average over the Plate
Averaging from $x=0$ to $x=L$:

$$\boxed{\,C_{f,\text{lam}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 The Drag Polar — Derivation of $C_D$ vs $C_L$

Starting from the total drag:

$$C_D = C_{D_0} + C_{D_i}$$

where $C_{D_0}$ is the **parasite drag coefficient at zero lift** (constant for a given configuration, weakly dependent on $M$ and $Re$). Substituting the induced-drag result from §3.1:

$$\boxed{\,C_D = C_{D_0} + \frac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \, AR \, e}\,}$$

This is the **parabolic drag polar** — a parabola in the $C_D$–$C_L$ plane. It is the single most-asked equation in the KTU Module-1 question bank.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite twist:** "Find the speed for minimum drag" → set $\dfrac{dC_D}{dC_L} = 0 \Rightarrow C_{L,MD} = \sqrt{C_{D_0} \pi AR e}$. Then use $C_{L,MD} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho V_{MD}^{2} S}$ to find $V_{MD}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drag Type vs. Flow Regime

| Drag Type | Subsonic ($M<0.7$) | Transonic ($0.7 \le M \le 1.3$) | Supersonic ($M>1.3$) | Dominant Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Skin Friction | High | High | High | Reynolds Number $Re$ |
| Pressure (Form) | Moderate–High (bluff bodies) | High | Moderate (slender bodies) | Body shape |
| Induced | High (low speed) | Low | Low | Aspect Ratio $AR$ |
| Wave | Negligible | **Dominant** (drag-divergence) | High | Mach Number $M$ |
| Interference | Moderate | High | High | Junctions / fillets |
| Profile | High (laminar) | High | Moderate | Boundary-layer state |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often give a question like *"Why does wave drag suddenly appear near $M=0.85$?"* — the answer is the **drag-divergence Mach number**, where local flow over the upper surface of the wing first reaches sonic conditions, forming a **normal shock** that spills the boundary layer → massive wave drag onset.

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — All Types of Drag

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL DRAG D] --> B[PARASITE DRAG DP]
    A --> C[INDUCED DRAG DI]
    A --> W[WAVE DRAG DW supersonic only]

    B --> D1[SKIN FRICTION DRAG Df]
    B --> D2[PRESSURE FORM DRAG Dp]
    B --> D3[INTERFERENCE DRAG Dint]
    B --> D4[PROFILE DRAG Dpr Df plus Dp]

    C --> E1[Origin Wingtip Vortices]
    C --> E2[Parameter Aspect Ratio AR]
    C --> E3[Parameter Oswald factor e]

    W --> F1[Threshold Critical Mach Mcr]
    W --> F2[Control Sweep Area Rule Waverider]

    D1 --> G1[Source Viscous Shear Tau w]
    D1 --> G2[Governs Reynolds number Re]
    D2 --> H1[Source Pressure Imbalance]
    D2 --> H2[High for Bluff Bodies]
```

## 4.2 Drag Polar Schematic — $C_D$ vs $C_L$ Parabola

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parasite Drag Line constant CD0] --> SUM[Total Drag Polar Parabola]
    P2[Induced Drag Curve parabolic in CL] --> SUM
    SUM --> MINP[Minimum Drag Point CLMD]
    MINP --> VMD[Speed for Minimum Drag VMD]
    MINP --> E[Best Glide Ratio LD max at VMD]
    SUM --> LDMIN[Point L over D max used for range endurance]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — How Drag is Computed in an Aircraft Performance Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Flight Inputs V h W S b] --> M1[Compute Mach M V over a]
    IN1 --> M2[Compute Reynolds Re rho V c over mu]
    IN1 --> M3[Compute CL 2W over rho V squared S]
    M1 --> SW{Wave drag regime M greater than 0 7}
    SW -- Yes --> WD[Add Wave Drag Coefficient CDW from tables]
    SW -- No --> ND[Set CDW to 0]
    M2 --> CD0[Compute Parasite CD0 from components fuselage wing tail]
    CD0 --> SUM2[Drag Polar CD equals CD0 plus CL squared over pi AR e]
    M3 --> SUM2
    ND --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> CD[Coefficient CD]
    CD --> DRAG[Drag Force D equals 0 5 rho V squared S CD]
    DRAG --> OUT[Output Drag in Newtons]
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Boundary-Layer State → Skin Friction Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Compute Re rho V L over mu] --> Q2{Re less than Recrit}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Laminar BL]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Turbulent BL]
    Q3 --> Q5[Cf laminar 1 328 over sqrt Re]
    Q4 --> Q6[Cf turbulent 0 074 over Re to power 0 2]
    Q5 --> Q7[Skin Friction Drag Df Cf times 0 5 rho V squared S]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> QOUT[Df reported to aircraft drag database]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Diagram — Bluff vs Streamlined Body Pressure Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLUFF[BLUFF BODY cylinder brick] --> PB1[High P on front face stagnation]
    BLUFF --> PB2[Low P in separated wake]
    PB1 --> SUM1[Large Pressure Imbalance]
    PB2 --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> DRP1[DOMINANT Pressure Drag]

    SLIM[STREAMLINED BODY airfoil dolphin] --> PS1[High P small stagnation region]
    SLIM --> PS2[Low P wake much smaller]
    PS1 --> SUM2[Small Pressure Imbalance]
    PS2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> DRP2[Pressure Drag minimized]
    SLIM --> DRP3[Skin Friction becomes the major contributor]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are aligned to the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern: **Part A = 3-mark short answers (2 × 3 = 6 marks)**, **Part B = 14-mark long answers (1 × 14 = 14 marks with internal choice)**, and KTU Board valuation conventions (1 mark reserved for neatness/clarity in long answers, 7 + 7 sub-part split).

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define induced drag. Why is it called a "by-product" drag?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Induced drag is the component of total drag that arises as an **inevitable consequence of lift generation** on a finite-aspect-ratio wing. The pressure difference between the upper and lower wing surfaces causes air to leak around the wingtips, generating **trailing wingtip vortices**. These vortices induce a **downwash** $w$ in the plane behind the wing, which tilts the local relative wind backward by a small angle $\varepsilon = w/V_{\infty}$. The lift vector $\vec{L}$, always perpendicular to the local relative wind, therefore tilts aft by $\varepsilon$, and its horizontal projection is the induced drag.

$$D_i = L \cdot \sin\varepsilon \approx L \cdot \frac{w}{V_{\infty}}$$

It is called a "by-product" drag because **it does not exist in 2-D infinite-span flow**; it appears *only* because the wing has a finite span and must support lift. (3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark equation)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between skin-friction drag and pressure (form) drag with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Skin-Friction Drag | Pressure (Form) Drag |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Tangential viscous shear $\tau_w$ on the wetted surface | Net pressure imbalance between front and rear |
| Direction of stress | Parallel to surface | Normal to surface |
| Dominates for | **Streamlined bodies** (airfoil, fish) | **Bluff bodies** (flat plate, cylinder, brick) |
| Governing parameter | Reynolds number $Re$ | Body shape / frontal area |
| Example | Laminar flow over a wing | Drag on a flat plate normal to the flow |

(3 marks — 1 mark per row × 3, with example marks bundled in)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this course carry **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. The examiner must provide an internal choice (OR option). Two complete independent question sets are given below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Pressure Drag + Skin Friction

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEICT751]`

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the physical origin of **pressure drag** and **skin-friction drag**. How does the relative importance of the two change as the body changes from a flat plate held normal to the flow to a fully streamlined airfoil?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Pressure Drag (3 marks):** When a body is placed in a flow, fluid strikes the upstream face, creating a **high-pressure stagnation region**. On the downstream side, flow cannot completely fill the wake — it separates, leaving a **low-pressure region**. The resultant pressure imbalance produces a net force in the direction of flow → **pressure drag**. For a flat plate held normal to the flow, this drag is the dominant component.

2. **Skin-Friction Drag (2 marks):** Due to the **no-slip condition**, fluid in direct contact with the body has zero velocity. Each successive layer slides over the previous one, generating **tangential viscous shear stresses** $\tau_w$. The integral of these shears over the wetted surface yields the skin-friction drag.

3. **Body-shape effect (2 marks):** As the body is progressively streamlined (flat plate → cylinder → ellipse → airfoil), the wake shrinks, the rear pressure recovers toward the front stagnation pressure, and **pressure drag reduces dramatically**. For a highly streamlined airfoil, **skin friction is the dominant component** (often 80–90% of profile drag). This is why airliners, dolphins, and bumblebees share smooth, tapered shapes.

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** For a laminar flat plate of length $L = 2$ m, free-stream velocity $V_{\infty} = 30$ m/s, and air at standard sea-level conditions ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m·s), compute the **average skin-friction coefficient** and the **total skin-friction drag** per unit span.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re_L = \frac{\rho V_{\infty} L}{\mu} = \frac{1.225 \times 30 \times 2}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{73.5}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.108 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Verify laminar assumption:**
For a flat plate, the critical Reynolds number is typically $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$. Since $Re_L = 4.108 \times 10^{6} \gg Re_{crit}$, **the boundary layer is turbulent, not laminar.**

However, the problem asks for the *laminar* Blasius result (textbook), so we proceed:

**Step 3 — Average laminar skin-friction coefficient (Blasius):**

$$C_{f,\text{lam}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{4.108 \times 10^{6}}} = \frac{1.328}{2026.84} = 6.55 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Skin-friction drag per unit span:**
For a flat plate, $S_{ref} = L \times 1$ (per unit span). So:

$$D_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S \, C_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 30^{2} \times 2 \times 6.55 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$D_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 2 \times 6.55 \times 10^{-4} = 551.25 \times 1.31 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\,D_f \approx 0.722 \; \text{N per meter of span}\,}$$

(7 marks — 2 for Re, 1 for critical-Re check, 2 for $C_f$, 2 for $D_f$)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Induced Drag + Drag Polar

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEICT751]`

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** With a clear sketch, derive the **induced-drag coefficient** for a finite wing with elliptic lift distribution. State clearly the role of **aspect ratio** and the **Oswald efficiency factor**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Sketch (1 mark):** A wing producing lift with two trailing vortices at the tips; the slipstream tilts downward by the induced angle $\varepsilon$ behind the wing.

2. **Geometry (2 marks):** Trailing vortices induce a downwash $w$. This tilts the local relative wind by $\varepsilon = w/V_{\infty}$ and rotates the lift vector aft. The horizontal projection is the induced drag:

$$D_i = L \sin\varepsilon \approx L \, \varepsilon = \dfrac{L \, w}{V_{\infty}}$$

3. **Downwash from elliptic distribution (2 marks):** Applying the Biot–Savart law to an elliptic vortex-sheet gives constant downwash across the span:

$$w = \dfrac{2L}{\pi \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} b^{2}}$$

4. **Final result (2 marks):** Combining and non-dimensionalizing:

$$\boxed{\,C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \, AR}\,}$$

with the more general form

$$C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \, AR \, e}$$

where $e$ is the **Oswald efficiency factor** ($0.7 \le e \le 0.95$ for typical aircraft). Higher $e$ means the wing's lift distribution is closer to elliptic, reducing induced drag. **Aspect ratio** $AR = b^{2}/S$ is the dominant geometric parameter — long-span wings (high $AR$) have low induced drag but high structural weight.

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** A light aircraft has the following data: $W = 9000$ N, $S = 18$ m², $b = 12$ m, $e = 0.85$, $C_{D_0} = 0.025$. At sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³), find (i) the **speed for minimum drag** $V_{MD}$, and (ii) the **minimum drag value** $D_{min}$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \frac{b^{2}}{S} = \frac{12^{2}}{18} = \frac{144}{18} = 8$$

**Step 2 — $C_L$ for minimum drag:**
For the parabolic drag polar, $C_D = C_{D_0} + \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi \, AR \, e}$. Setting $\dfrac{dC_D}{dC_L} = 0$:

$$C_{L,MD} = \sqrt{C_{D_0} \, \pi \, AR \, e} = \sqrt{0.025 \times \pi \times 8 \times 0.85}$$

$$C_{L,MD} = \sqrt{0.025 \times 21.36} = \sqrt{0.534} = 0.731$$

**Step 3 — Minimum drag coefficient:**

$$C_{D,min} = 2 C_{D_0} = 2 \times 0.025 = 0.050$$

**Step 4 — Speed for minimum drag:**
Using $C_L = \dfrac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S} \Rightarrow V_{MD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,MD}}}$:

$$V_{MD} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9000}{1.225 \times 18 \times 0.731}} = \sqrt{\frac{18000}{16.118}}$$

$$V_{MD} = \sqrt{1116.76} \approx 33.4 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum drag value:**

$$D_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{MD}^{2} S \, C_{D,min} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 33.4^{2} \times 18 \times 0.05$$

$$D_{min} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1115.56 \times 18 \times 0.05 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1004.0 = 614.96 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{MD} \approx 33.4 \;\text{m/s}, \quad D_{min} \approx 615 \;\text{N}\,}$$

(7 marks — 1 for $AR$, 1 for $C_{L,MD}$, 1 for $C_{D,min}$, 2 for $V_{MD}$, 2 for $D_{min}$)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Types-of-Drag questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $e$** in $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \, AR \, e)$ — costs **2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Using profile drag and parasite drag interchangeably** — they are *not* the same. Profile = skin friction + pressure (2-D airfoil). Parasite = profile + interference + cooling + leak + ... (whole aircraft minus induced).
> 3. **Confusing $C_f$ (skin-friction coefficient) with $C_D$ (drag coefficient of a body)** — they live in different domains. $C_f$ is on a flat plate; $C_D$ is on a 3-D body.
> 4. **Skipping the critical-Reynolds-number check** when applying Blasius — examiners *test* this. If $Re > 5 \times 10^{5}$, the laminar formula is invalid.
> 5. **Not drawing a sketch in long answers.** The KTU marking scheme allocates **1 mark for neatness + clear sketch** in every 14-mark question. Always draw a labeled figure of the wing + trailing vortices.
> 6. **Mixing up $M_{cr}$ (critical Mach) with $M=1$.** Wave drag does not start exactly at $M=1$ — it starts at the **drag-divergence Mach number** $M_{DD}$, which is just above $M_{cr}$, often around $M = 0.85$ for thick wings.
> 7. **Sign errors in induced drag** — remember $D_i$ is *parallel and opposite* to $V_{\infty}$, so it is always **positive** when $L$ is positive.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (must memorize before the KTU exam):**
>
> - [x] **Six drag types** and their physical origins: Skin friction, Pressure/Form, Profile, Induced, Wave, Interference.
> - [x] **Total drag = Parasite drag + Induced drag** + (Wave drag, if supersonic).
> - [x] Master non-dimensional coefficient: $C_D = 2D/(\rho V^2 S)$.
> - [x] **Reynolds number** governs skin friction; **Mach number** governs wave drag; **Aspect ratio** governs induced drag.
> - [x] **Blasius laminar:** $C_{f,\text{lam}} = 1.328 / \sqrt{Re_L}$.
> - [x] **Turbulent plate:** $C_{f,\text{turb}} = 0.074 / Re_L^{1/5}$.
> - [x] **Induced drag:** $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \, AR \, e)$.
> - [x] **Drag polar:** $C_D = C_{D_0} + C_L^2 / (\pi \, AR \, e)$.
> - [x] **Minimum drag speed** corresponds to $C_{L,MD} = \sqrt{C_{D_0} \pi AR e}$ and **minimum drag coefficient** is $C_{D,min} = 2 C_{D_0}$.
> - [x] **Wave drag** appears near $M_{cr} \approx 0.7$–$0.85$ and peaks at $M=1$.
> - [x] **Streamlined bodies** → skin-friction-dominated; **bluff bodies** → pressure-dominated.
> - [x] Always include the **Oswald efficiency factor $e$**; always verify the **laminar/turbulent regime** before applying Blasius.
> - [x] One neatly labeled diagram is **worth 1 free mark** in every KTU long answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Finite Wing?

In classical airfoil (2D) theory, we assumed the wing had an **infinite span**, meaning air could not flow around the wingtips. In reality, **every aircraft wing is finite** — it has a beginning (root) and an end (tip). This finiteness fundamentally changes the aerodynamic behaviour of the wing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A **Finite Wing** is a three-dimensional lifting surface of finite span (*b*) and chord (*c*), where the pressure differential between the upper (low-pressure) and lower (high-pressure) surfaces causes air to leak around the wingtips, generating **wing-tip vortices**, **downwash**, and **induced drag (CDi)**. These 3D effects do not exist in 2D infinite wing analysis.

### 1.2 Key Geometric Parameters

For a finite wing, the geometry is characterized by:

- **Span (*b*)** → Wingtip-to-wingtip distance, measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Chord (*c*)** → Distance from leading edge to trailing edge at any section.
- **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (*MAC / $\bar{c}$*)** → The weighted average chord of the wing.
- **Wing Area (*S*)** → Planform area of the wing in **m²**.
- **Aspect Ratio (*AR*)** → Ratio of span-squared to wing area:

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$$

- **Taper Ratio (*λ*)** → Ratio of tip chord to root chord.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aspect Ratio is the single most important geometric parameter for finite wing aerodynamics.** A high AR wing (like a glider) produces very low induced drag, while a low AR wing (like a fighter) produces higher induced drag but better manoeuvrability.

### 1.3 Why Finite Wings Behave Differently — The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine pushing a long rectangular barge through water (this is like an **infinite wing** — water can only go over and under, never around the ends).

Now imagine pushing a short, finite paddle through the same water (this is like a **finite wing**). Water that builds up in front of the paddle can now leak around the edges (the tips), creating swirling whirlpools behind it. These whirlpools **rob the paddle of some of its thrust-producing efficiency**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Hold two sheets of paper — one rolled into a long tube (high AR), one flat and short (low AR). Blow across each. The long tube "pulls" much more strongly because air cannot escape around its ends. The flat sheet allows air to leak around the edges, reducing the suction effect. **This leakage = downwash = induced drag.**

### 1.4 The Three Critical 3D Effects Unique to Finite Wings

| # | Phenomenon | Root Cause | Aerodynamic Consequence |
|---|------------|------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Wing-Tip Vortices** | High-pressure air below leaks around tip to low-pressure region above | Swirling vortex sheet trails behind wing |
| 2 | **Downwash (*w*)** | Vortex sheet induces a downward velocity component behind the wing | Effective angle of attack reduced |
| 3 | **Induced Drag (*CDi*)** | Downwash tilts the lift vector backward | Lift vector tilted → drag component created |
