## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Prandtl's Lifting Line Theory — The Heart of Finite Wing Analysis

**Ludwig Prandtl (1918)** developed the foundational theory to extend 2D airfoil results to 3D finite wings. His model replaces the continuous wing with a single **bound vortex along the quarter-chord line** (the lifting line), with **trailing vortices** shedding backward from the wingtips and along the span.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption (Prandtl's Lifting Line Theory):**
> The wing is replaced by a **lifting line** (a concentrated line vortex) running span-wise. A **trailing vortex sheet** composed of infinitely many small horseshoe vortices extends from the lifting line to infinity downstream. Each span-wise station has a circulation $\Gamma(y)$ that varies from root to tip.

### 2.2 The Fundamental Equation of Lifting Line Theory

The total induced velocity at any span-wise station *y* is given by:

$$w(y) = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} \frac{d\Gamma/dy_0}{y - y_0}\, dy_0$$

- *w(y)* → local downwash velocity at span station *y*
- *Γ(y)* → local circulation at span station *y*
- *y₀* → integration variable along span

**Prandtl's Elliptic Circulation Assumption:**

$$\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2y}{b}\right)^2}$$

Where:
- *Γ₀* → maximum circulation at the wing root (centerline)

### 2.3 The Downwash and Induced Angle of Attack

The **downwash velocity (*w*)** is the downward component of velocity induced by the trailing vortex sheet. The **induced angle of attack (*αᵢ*)** is the angle by which the local airflow is tilted downward from the freestream direction.

$$\alpha_i = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{w}{V_\infty}\right) \approx \frac{w}{V_\infty} \quad (\text{for small angles})$$

The **effective angle of attack** seen by the local airfoil section is:

$$\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \alpha_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lift coefficient of the wing uses the **geometric** α, but the **section lift coefficient** at any span-wise station uses α_eff. This distinction is a classic KTU exam trap.

### 2.4 Lift and Induced Drag Coefficients

For a wing with **elliptic lift distribution** (which is the most efficient), the closed-form solutions are:

$$C_L = \frac{L}{q_\infty S} = a \cdot \alpha \quad \text{(linear range)}$$

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}$$

Where:
- *a* → 3D lift curve slope of the finite wing
- *e* → Oswald efficiency factor (efficiency factor) — measures how close the wing is to elliptic loading. For elliptic loading, **e = 1**.

### 2.5 The Trefftz-Plane Result (Lift-Independent Induced Drag)

In the far wake (Trefftz plane), the induced drag equals the total vertical momentum flux:

$$D_i = \frac{L^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \pi b^2 e} = \frac{L^2}{q_\infty \pi b^2 e}$$

Dividing by dynamic pressure and wing area gives:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}$$

> [!TIP]
> This result, $C_{D_i} \propto C_L^2 / AR$, is one of the **most important equations in KTU Module 1**. Doubling the lift coefficient *quadruples* induced drag. Doubling the aspect ratio *halves* induced drag.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = b^2 / S$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Mean Aerodynamic Chord | $\bar{c} = (1/S)\int c^2(y)\,dy$ | meters (m) |
| 3 | Span Efficiency | $e = 1$ for elliptic loading | dimensionless |
| 4 | Induced Angle of Attack | $\alpha_i = C_L / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ | radians |
| 5 | Effective Angle of Attack | $\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \alpha_i$ | radians or degrees |
| 6 | 3D Lift Curve Slope | $a = a_0 / (1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot AR))$ | per radian |
| 7 | Lift Coefficient (3D) | $C_L = a \cdot \alpha$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Induced Drag Coefficient | $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Total Drag Coefficient | $C_D = C_{D_0} + C_{D_i}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Trefftz Plane Lift | $L = \rho V_\infty \Gamma_0 (\pi b / 4)$ | Newtons (N) |
| 11 | Downwash Velocity | $w = (\Gamma_0 / 2b) \cdot f(\eta)$ | m/s |
| 12 | Circulation Distribution | $\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0\sqrt{1-(2y/b)^2}$ | m²/s |

### 2.7 Physical Significance in Aerospace Engineering

| Application | Why Finite Wing Theory Matters |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Sailplane / Glider Design** | Maximize AR (long wings) to minimize induced drag for endurance |
| **Commercial Transport (B777, A350)** | High AR swept wings (~AR = 9–10) for cruise efficiency |
| **Fighter Aircraft (F-22)** | Low AR delta wings for high manoeuvrability and supersonic performance |
| **UAV / Drones** | Moderately high AR with winglets to reduce tip vortex strength |
| **Wind Turbine Blades** | Same lifting line theory applies — high AR reduces vortex losses |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Induced Drag from Momentum Theory (Trefftz Plane)

We derive the classical result $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi AR)$ for an elliptically loaded wing.

**Step 1 — Define the wing loading.**

The total lift equals the circulation integrated over the span. For an elliptic distribution:

$$L = \rho V_\infty \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} \Gamma(y)\, dy$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the elliptic circulation profile.**

$$\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2y}{b}\right)^2}$$

Performing the integration (using the standard elliptic integral result):

$$L = \rho V_\infty \Gamma_0 \cdot \frac{\pi b}{4}$$

This is the **Trefftz-plane lift** expression.

**Step 3 — Express induced drag using Trefftz-plane momentum.**

The induced drag equals the kinetic energy flux in the wake per unit of freestream velocity:

$$D_i = \frac{L^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \pi b^2}$$

The denominator represents the induced kinetic energy in the wake. Substituting $L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_i &= \frac{\left(\rho V_\infty \Gamma_0 \pi b/4\right)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \pi b^2} \\
&= \frac{\rho^2 V_\infty^2 \Gamma_0^2 \pi^2 b^2 / 16}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \pi b^2} \\
&= \frac{\rho \Gamma_0^2 \pi}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by dynamic pressure × wing area to obtain CDi.**

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{D_i}{q_\infty S} = \frac{D_i}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S}$$

Using $S = \pi b^2/4 \cdot AR$ identity, and recognizing that for the elliptic case $C_L = \pi AR \cdot \alpha_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{D_i} &= \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e} \\
&= \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR} \quad (\text{for } e = 1)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical induced drag equation** required for KTU Module 1 derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation: 3D Lift Curve Slope

The 2D airfoil section lift curve slope is $a_0 = 2\pi$ per radian (thin airfoil theory). For a finite wing, the section must work against its own downwash, reducing the slope.

**Step 1 — Section lift at span station y.**

The local section lift coefficient is:

$$c_\ell(y) = a_0 \cdot \alpha_{eff}(y) = a_0 [\alpha - \alpha_i(y)]$$

**Step 2 — Express section lift via circulation.**

By the Kutta–Joukowski theorem applied at the local section:

$$c_\ell(y) = \frac{2\Gamma(y)}{V_\infty c(y)}$$

**Step 3 — Relate αi to lift distribution.**

For a general wing, the downwash at any section is induced by the entire trailing vortex system. Prandtl showed that for elliptic loading:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot AR}$$

This is **constant along the span** for elliptic loading — a critical simplification.

**Step 4 — Integrate total lift and solve for the 3D slope.**

Total lift: $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \int c(y) c_\ell(y)\, dy$

Substituting $c_\ell = a_0(\alpha - C_L/(\pi AR))$ and solving:

$$a = \frac{a_0}{1 + \frac{a_0}{\pi \cdot AR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **3D lift curve slope formula**. For very high AR (large AR → ∞), $a \to a_0 = 2\pi$ (recovers 2D result). For very low AR (AR → 0), $a \to \pi AR \to 0$ (no lift possible).

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective Aspect Ratio (for non-elliptic wings)

For a wing whose span-wise loading deviates from elliptic, define the **effective aspect ratio**:

$$AR_{eff} = \frac{AR}{\sigma}$$

Where *σ* is the **induced drag factor** (sometimes written *δ* or *κ*). It depends on the actual span-wise lift distribution.

Equivalently, using the **span efficiency factor** *e*:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}$$

So $e = 1/\sigma$ for the same wing.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students recognize that *e < 1* for non-elliptic loading. Common values: rectangular wing $e \approx 0.85$, tapered wing $e \approx 0.95$–$0.98$, elliptic wing $e = 1$.

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** A finite wing has $AR = 8$, wing area $S = 20$ m², and flies at $\alpha = 6°$ in a sea-level flow ($V_\infty = 60$ m/s). Assume elliptic loading ($e = 1$) and 2D airfoil slope $a_0 = 2\pi$/rad.

**Find:** (a) 3D lift curve slope *a*, (b) Lift coefficient $C_L$, (c) Induced drag coefficient $C_{D_i}$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a)** — Apply the 3D slope formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{a_0}{1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot AR)} \\
&= \frac{2\pi}{1 + 2\pi/(\pi \cdot 8)} \\
&= \frac{6.2832}{1 + 0.25} \\
&= 5.0266 \text{ per radian}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (b)** — Convert α to radians: $\alpha = 6° \times \pi/180 = 0.1047$ rad.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_L &= a \cdot \alpha \\
&= 5.0266 \times 0.1047 \\
&= 0.5263
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (c)** — Apply the induced drag formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{D_i} &= \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e} \\
&= \frac{(0.5263)^2}{\pi \times 8 \times 1} \\
&= \frac{0.2770}{25.133} \\
&= 0.01102
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Verification:** The induced drag coefficient is small but non-zero, even though we are flying at modest angle of attack. This is exactly why finite wing analysis matters — even at "low" $C_L$, there is always parasitic-like induced drag proportional to $C_L^2$.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Finite Wing Aerodynamic Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Wing Geometry b S AR] --> inputB[Airfoil Data a0]
    inputB --> stepA[Compute 3D Slope a]
    inputA --> stepA
    stepA --> stepB[Geometric Angle alpha]
    stepB --> stepC[CL from a times alpha]
    stepC --> stepD[Induced Alpha_i]
    stepD --> stepE[Induced Drag CDi]
    inputA --> stepE
    stepC --> stepF[Section Lift cl_y]
    stepD --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Circulation Gamma_y]
    stepG --> stepH[Tip Vortex Strength]
    stepH --> outputX[3D Aerodynamic Forces]
    stepE --> outputX
    stepC --> outputX
```

**Figure 4.1** — Functional architecture showing how wing geometry and airfoil data combine to produce finite-wing aerodynamic outputs. Each rectangular block represents a computational step; arrows indicate data dependencies.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Infinite vs. Finite Wing Physics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TwoD[Infinite Wing 2D]
        A1[Freestream V] --> A2[Pressure Diff]
        A2 --> A3[L lift Vector Upward]
        A3 --> A4[No Tip Vortices]
        A4 --> A5[No Downwash]
        A5 --> A6[No Induced Drag]
    end

    subgraph ThreeD[Finite Wing 3D]
        B1[Freestream V] --> B2[Pressure Diff]
        B2 --> B3[L lift Vector Tilted Back]
        B2 --> B4[Tip Vortices Form]
        B4 --> B5[Downwash w]
        B5 --> B6[Induced Alpha_i]
        B6 --> B7[Induced Drag CDi]
        B3 --> B7
    end

    TwoD -. contrast .-> ThreeD
```

**Figure 4.2** — Side-by-side comparison: in 2D the lift vector remains vertical and no drag is induced. In 3D, pressure-driven leakage around the tips spawns vortices, downwash, and a backward-tilted lift vector that produces induced drag.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Downwash and Effective Angle Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Geometric Alpha] --> Q1[Subtract]
    P2[Induced Alpha_i] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Effective Alpha]
    R1 --> S1[Section 2D Airfoil]
    S1 --> T1[Local Lift cl]
    P2 --> note1[Alpha_i from vortex sheet]
    note1 --> P2
```

**Figure 4.3** — Effective α is geometric α reduced by induced α. The 2D airfoil only "sees" the reduced angle, even though the wing is physically at the larger geometric angle.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Induced Drag Components in Drag Polar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Drag CD] --> B[Parasitic CD0]
    A --> C[Induced CDi]
    C --> D[CL squared term]
    C --> E[AR denominator]
    C --> F[Span Efficiency e]
    D --> G[CD parabolic curve]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

**Figure 4.4** — Total drag polar is the sum of zero-lift parasitic drag and lift-dependent induced drag. Higher AR and better efficiency factor lower the parabolic drag bucket.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **induced drag** and explain why it occurs only on finite wings, not on infinite wings.

**Model Answer:**
Induced drag is the drag component of a finite wing that arises due to the **wing-tip vortices** formed when high-pressure air from below the wing leaks around the tips into the low-pressure region above. These vortices create a **downwash** behind the wing, which tilts the local airflow downward and therefore tilts the **lift vector backward**. The rearward component of this tilted lift vector is the induced drag.

On an **infinite wing**, the wingtips do not exist, so no air can leak around them, no tip vortices form, no downwash is induced, and the lift vector remains purely vertical. Hence, **induced drag = 0 for the ideal 2D infinite wing**.

*(3 marks: definition 1 mark, physical mechanism 1 mark, infinite wing comparison 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the expression for the induced drag coefficient of a finite wing in terms of lift coefficient and aspect ratio. Under what condition does this become exact (without efficiency factor)?

**Model Answer:**
The induced drag coefficient is given by:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}$$

The expression becomes **exact (no *e* required, with *e* = 1)** when the span-wise lift distribution is **elliptic**, i.e.,

$$\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0\sqrt{1 - (2y/b)^2}$$

This is the optimal distribution that minimizes induced drag for a given lift and aspect ratio (Prandtl's minimum induced drag theorem).

*(3 marks: equation 2 marks, elliptic condition 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Starting from Prandtl's lifting line theory, derive the expression for the **induced angle of attack** $\alpha_i$ for a wing with elliptic lift distribution. Hence, obtain the **3D lift curve slope** *a* in terms of the 2D slope $a_0$ and aspect ratio *AR*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular wing of span 12 m and chord 2 m flies at sea level at $V_\infty = 80$ m/s with $\alpha = 5°$. The 2D airfoil lift curve slope is $a_0 = 2\pi$ per radian and the airfoil is symmetric. Determine: (i) 3D lift curve slope, (ii) Lift coefficient, (iii) Lift force in N, (iv) Induced drag coefficient (assume *e* = 1). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Section lift coefficient at any span station *y* is:

$$c_\ell(y) = a_0 \cdot \alpha_{eff}(y) = a_0[\alpha - \alpha_i(y)]$$

**Step 2:** The Kutta–Joukowski theorem gives section lift via circulation:

$$c_\ell(y) = \frac{2\Gamma(y)}{V_\infty c(y)}$$

**Step 3:** For elliptic loading, $\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0\sqrt{1-(2y/b)^2}$. Substituting into the downwash integral:

$$\alpha_i(y) = \frac{\Gamma_0}{2 b V_\infty}\cdot\frac{\text{const}}{}$$

Prandtl showed this is **constant along the span** for elliptic loading:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot AR}$$

**Step 4:** Total lift coefficient:

$$C_L = a\alpha = a\left[\alpha_{eff} + \frac{C_L}{\pi AR}\right] = a\alpha_{eff}\left[\text{with section integration}\right]$$

Solving yields:

$$\boxed{a = \frac{a_0}{1 + \frac{a_0}{\pi \cdot AR}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $b = 12$ m, $c = 2$ m, $S = bc = 24$ m², $V_\infty = 80$ m/s, $\alpha = 5° = 0.0873$ rad, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level), $a_0 = 2\pi$, $e = 1$.

**Step (i):** Compute $AR = b^2/S = 144/24 = 6$.

$$a = \frac{2\pi}{1 + 2\pi/(\pi \cdot 6)} = \frac{6.2832}{1 + 0.3333} = \frac{6.2832}{1.3333} = 4.7124 \text{ per rad}$$

**Step (ii):** $C_L = a \cdot \alpha = 4.7124 \times 0.0873 = 0.4114$.

**Step (iii):** Lift force:

$$L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S C_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 \times 24 \times 0.4114$$

$$L = 0.6125 \times 6400 \times 24 \times 0.4114 = 38698 \text{ N} \approx 38.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step (iv):** Induced drag coefficient:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e} = \frac{(0.4114)^2}{\pi \times 6 \times 1} = \frac{0.1692}{18.85} = 0.00897$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT use** $a_0 = 2\pi$ directly to compute lift without applying the 3D correction — this is the single most common mistake. *Marks lost: up to 3.*
> 2. **Convert α from degrees to radians** before multiplying by *a*. KTU evaluators explicitly deduct 1 mark for unit error.
> 3. **Forget $S = b \times c$** for rectangular wings? You will lose 2 marks in Part (b).
> 4. **No *e* in the denominator for elliptic loading** — but **always include it** in the general formula. Marks are awarded for stating the assumption clearly.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the physical mechanism of **wing-tip vortices** and **downwash** formation on a finite wing. With the aid of Prandtl's lifting line model, derive the relation $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi \cdot AR)$ for an elliptically loaded wing using the **Trefftz plane momentum theory**. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** Two aircraft wings, one with $AR = 6$ and another with $AR = 10$, both produce $C_L = 0.5$ at the same flight condition. Both have *e* = 1. Calculate the ratio of their induced drag coefficients and comment on the design implication. **(6 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanism and Derivation:**

**Mechanism (4 marks):** Because of the pressure differential between lower (high pressure) and upper (low pressure) surfaces, air flows around the wingtips from bottom to top. This leakage rolls into two **wing-tip vortices** (counter-rotating). These vortices, modeled by Prandtl as a continuous sheet of trailing vortices shed from the lifting line, induce a **downwash velocity *w*** in the wake region behind the wing. The downwash tilts the local relative wind downward by angle $\alpha_i = w/V_\infty$, reducing the effective angle of attack experienced by each airfoil section. The lift vector, originally perpendicular to $V_\infty$, is now tilted backward by $\alpha_i$, producing a rearward (drag-direction) component called **induced drag**.

**Trefftz Plane Derivation (4 marks):** In the Trefftz plane (far downstream where vorticity is concentrated), the induced drag equals the rate of kinetic energy addition to the wake per unit of freestream velocity:

$$D_i = \frac{L^2}{q_\infty \pi b^2 e}$$

Dividing by $q_\infty S = q_\infty \pi b^2 / (4 AR)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{D_i} &= \frac{D_i}{q_\infty S} = \frac{L^2}{q_\infty^2 \pi b^2 e S} \\
&= \frac{C_L^2 q_\infty^2 S^2}{q_\infty^2 \pi b^2 e S} = \frac{C_L^2 \cdot S}{\pi b^2 e} \\
&= \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}
\end{aligned}$$

For elliptic loading, $e = 1$, giving:

$$\boxed{C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR}}$$

**Part (b) — Comparison:**

Since both wings have the same $C_L$ and $e = 1$:

$$\frac{C_{D_i,AR6}}{C_{D_i,AR10}} = \frac{AR_{10}}{AR_6} = \frac{10}{6} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$

The lower-AR wing produces **66.7% more induced drag** at the same lift.

**Design Implication:** For maximum range and endurance (e.g., gliders, long-haul transports), designers prefer **high aspect ratio wings**. The penalty for using a low-AR wing is a quadratic rise in induced drag with $C_L$, which dominates the drag polar at low speeds (take-off, landing, and high-lift cruise).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Question B:**
> 1. Failing to mention the Trefftz plane assumption (vortex sheet in far wake) → **−2 marks**.
> 2. Confusing *induced drag* with *parasitic drag* in the design comment → **−1 mark**.
> 3. Forgetting to comment on the practical design implication — KTU awards marks for "engineering judgment" → **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Finite Wings (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Finite wing = 3D wing with finite span *b*.** Key difference from 2D infinite wing: tip vortices, downwash, induced drag.
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^2/S$. Higher AR → lower induced drag.
- ✅ **Prandtl's Lifting Line Theory:** Wing replaced by span-wise lifting line + trailing vortex sheet.
- ✅ **Elliptic lift distribution** is optimal (Prandtl's minimum induced drag theorem); gives $e = 1$.
- ✅ **Downwash** tilts local airflow down by $\alpha_i$, reducing effective α.
- ✅ **Induced angle of attack:** $\alpha_i = C_L/(\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ — constant along span for elliptic loading.
- ✅ **Effective angle of attack:** $\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \alpha_i$.
- ✅ **3D lift curve slope:** $a = a_0 / [1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot AR)]$ — recovers $2\pi$ at high AR, vanishes at low AR.
- ✅ **Induced drag coefficient:** $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ — quadratic in $C_L$, inverse in $AR$.
- ✅ **Trefftz plane:** Far-downstream cross-section where wake momentum is evaluated.
- ✅ **Total drag polar:** $C_D = C_{D_0} + C_{D_i}$ — sum of parasitic and induced components.
- ✅ **Always convert α to radians** before computing $C_L = a\alpha$ in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Rectangular wing:** $S = bc$ and $AR = b/c$.
- ✅ **High-AR wings** (gliders, transports) minimize induced drag; **low-AR wings** (fighters, delta wings) trade drag for manoeuvrability and structural strength.
- ✅ **Winglets and tip devices** reduce induced drag by weakening the tip vortex, effectively increasing *AR* without increasing span.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swept Wings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Swept Wings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Swept Wing** is an aircraft wing planform in which the leading edge (and usually the trailing edge) is inclined rearward (or forward) relative to the aircraft's longitudinal axis (fuselage centerline), rather than being perpendicular to it. The angle of inclination is termed the **Sweep Angle (Λ)**. Formally, the **Sweep Angle** is the angle between a reference line on the wing (typically the quarter-chord line, leading edge, or trailing edge) and the lateral (perpendicular) axis of the aircraft, measured in the plan view. The three most commonly referenced sweep angles in KTU textbooks and FAA/Aeronautical literature are: - **Leading Edge Sweep Angle (Λ_LE)** — angle of the leading edge with respect to the lateral axis. - **Quarter-Chord Sweep Angle (Λ_c/4)** — angle of the line joining the quarter-chord points of root and tip airfoils. - **Trailing Edge Sweep Angle (Λ_TE)** — angle of the trailing edge with the lateral axis. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (Bookwork):** "Sweep angle is the angle between the wing's quarter-chord line and the normal to the aircraft's longitudinal axis." This definition is most frequently asked in the 3-mark and 7-mark questions. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) Imagine you are **rowing a boat with an oar**. If you hold the oar straight across the boat (perpendicular to the direction of motion), the oar "bites" the water strongly — high drag, high resistance. Now if you **angle the oar backward** as you pull, it slices through the water with much less resistance, and the boat moves faster for the same effort. The swept wing works on the same principle. By angling the wing backward, the **component of airflow that is perpendicular to the leading edge is reduced**, lowering wave drag and delaying the onset of transonic shock waves. This is why nearly all high-subsonic, transonic, and supersonic aircraft (e.g., Concorde, Boeing 747, F-22 Raptor, MiG-21) use swept wings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Design Insight:** Sweep is fundamentally a **transonic/supersonic drag-reduction technique**. It does **not** improve low-speed performance — in fact, swept wings are aerodynamically *worse* than straight wings at low speeds because of adverse pressure gradients, tip stall tendencies, and reduced lift-curve slope. ### 1.3 Classification of Swept Wings | Configuration | Description | Example Aircraft | |---|---|---| | **Untapered / Straight Wing (Λ = 0°)** | Leading edge perpendicular to fuselage | Cessna 152, Piper PA-28 | | **Moderately Swept (Λ = 25°–35°)** | Backward sweep, transonic | Boeing 737, Airbus A320 | | **Highly Swept (Λ = 45°–60°)** | Supersonic cruise | Concorde (Λ_LE ≈ 60°), F-104 Starfighter | | **Delta / Highly Swept (Λ > 60°)** | Near-delta planform | Mirage 2000, F-102 | | **Forward Swept Wing (FSW)** | Leading edge swept forward; rare, structurally demanding | Junkers Ju 287 (WWII), Sukhoi Su-47 Berkut | | **Variable Sweep (Swing Wing)** | Sweep angle changed in flight | F-14 Tomcat, Tornado, B-1 Lancer | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says "backward swept wing" or "positively swept", they mean the conventional rearward sweep. Mention its primary purpose — **delaying the critical Mach number to enable transonic/supersonic flight** — for guaranteed marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Sweep the Wing? — The Physical Reasoning At transonic speeds, the flow over a wing accelerates locally to supersonic speeds **even when the free-stream Mach number M∞ < 1**. When these supersonic pockets terminate in a **normal shock wave**, they produce a sudden pressure rise → **wave drag**. The critical observation is: > **The local Mach number that governs compressibility effects is the component of the free-stream velocity *normal* to the leading edge — not the free-stream Mach number itself.** Therefore, for a wing with leading edge sweep Λ_LE, the **normal Mach number** is: $$M_n = M_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$$ This means an unswept wing flying at M∞ = 0.8 experiences the same compressibility effects as a 60°-swept wing flying at M∞ ≈ 1.6. Sweep effectively "buys" Mach number. ### 2.2 Geometric Construction of Sweep Angle Consider a tapered wing with: - Root chord = c_r - Tip chord = t - Half-span = s - Longitudinal displacement of tip leading edge from root leading edge = L Then: $$\tan(\Lambda_{LE}) = \frac{L}{s}$$ For a wing with sweep measured at the quarter chord (Λ_c/4): $$\tan\left(\Lambda_{c/4}\right) = \frac{\tan(\Lambda_{LE}) - \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{c_r - t}{s}\right)}{1 + \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{c_r - t}{s}\right)\tan(\Lambda_{LE})}$$ This is a transformation between leading-edge sweep and quarter-chord sweep for tapered wings. ### 2.3 Aerodynamic Effects of Sweep | Effect | Direction of Influence | Physical Cause | |---|---|---| | **Critical Mach number (M_cr)** | ↑ Increases | Reduced normal Mach component | | **Wave drag at high subsonic** | ↓ Decreases | Shock strength reduced | | **Lift curve slope (C_L_α)** | ↓ Decreases | Spanwise flow, reduced effective α | | **Maximum lift coefficient (C_L_max)** | ↓ Decreases | Tip stall, leading-edge suction loss | | **Induced drag at low speed** | ↑ Increases | Lower aspect ratio effectiveness | | **Stability — Dutch roll** | ↑ Damped | Roll-yaw coupling altered | | **Structural divergence** | Risk | Torsion under sweep + aerodynamic twist | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** "Swept wings have lower induced drag." This is **FALSE**. Swept wings, especially with low aspect ratio, have *higher* induced drag than equivalent straight wings because of reduced effective dynamic pressure on the outboard sections. ### 2.4 Critical Mach Number for Swept Wings The critical Mach number is the free-stream Mach number at which the flow first becomes sonic (M = 1) at some point on the wing surface. For an unswept wing with airfoil section having critical pressure coefficient C_p,cr: $$M_{cr,\text{unswept}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{-C_{p,cr}}{1 - \left(\frac{C_{p,cr}}{2}\right)} + 1}$$ For the **same airfoil section** mounted on a swept wing of sweep Λ: $$M_{cr,\text{swept}} \cdot \cos(\Lambda) = M_{cr,\text{unswept section}}$$ Hence: $$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,\text{unswept section}}}{\cos(\Lambda)}$$ This simple, elegant relationship is the **most important board-exam result** for swept wings. ### 2.5 Drag Divergence Mach Number Kuchemann's approximation for the drag-rise Mach number is widely used in preliminary design: $$M_{DD} \approx \frac{M_{DD,0}}{\cos(\Lambda_{c/4})^{2/3}}$$ where M_DD,0 is the drag-divergence Mach number of the unswept airfoil at the same thickness ratio. The 2/3 power is an empirical fit to transonic data. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (No Pipes Inside Math — use \vert where needed) > [!NOTE] > **Render note:** For absolute value or magnitude in tables, prefer `\vert x \vert` or write "magnitude" to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | Normal Mach number | $M_n = M_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$ | Effective compressibility Mach | | Tangential Mach number | $M_t = M_\infty \sin(\Lambda_{LE})$ | Spanwise flow driver | | Critical Mach number (swept) | $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,\text{unswept}} / \cos(\Lambda)$ | Transonic onset | | Drag divergence Mach | $M_{DD} = M_{DD,0} / \cos(\Lambda_{c/4})^{2/3}$ | Wave-drag onset (Kuchemann) | | Geometric LE sweep | $\tan(\Lambda_{LE}) = L / s$ | Planform definition | | Lift-curve slope (approx.) | $a_{swept} = a_0 \cdot \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$ | Reduced per-span lift | | Effective dynamic pressure | $q_{eff} = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \cos^2(\Lambda)$ | Normal-flow energy | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Commercial Aviation:** All jet airliners since the de Havilland Comet (1952) use sweep ~25°–35° to cruise economically at M 0.78–0.85. 2. **Military Fighters:** Modern fighters use sweep of 40°–45° to achieve sustained supersonic dash. 3. **Supersonic Cruise:** Concorde and Tu-144 used highly swept (≈60°) ogival delta wings for M 2.0 cruise. 4. **Variable-Sweep Wings:** Enable a single airframe to optimize for both low-speed handling and high-speed cruise. 5. **Hypersonic Glide Vehicles:** Use sharp, highly swept planforms (e.g., X-43A, HTV-2) to control shock-boundary-layer interaction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Normal Component of Free-Stream Velocity **Statement:** For a free-stream velocity $V_\infty$ approaching a wing of leading edge sweep $\Lambda_{LE}$, the component of velocity normal to the leading edge is $V_n = V_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$. **Setup (Coordinate geometry):** - Let the aircraft's longitudinal axis (free-stream direction) be the **x-axis**. - The leading edge of the swept wing is inclined at angle $\Lambda_{LE}$ from the lateral (**y-axis**) direction. - The unit vector along the leading edge (in the wing planform) makes angle $\Lambda_{LE}$ with the y-axis, and therefore angle $(90° - \Lambda_{LE})$ with the x-axis. - The unit normal to the leading edge (in the planform) makes angle $\Lambda_{LE}$ with the x-axis. **Derivation:** The free-stream velocity vector: $$\vec{V}_\infty = V_\infty \, \hat{i} = V_\infty (1, 0)$$ The unit normal to the leading edge: $$\hat{n} = (\cos \Lambda_{LE}, \sin \Lambda_{LE})$$ The component of velocity along this normal is the dot product: $$V_n = \vec{V}_\infty \cdot \hat{n} = V_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}$$ Dividing by the local speed of sound $a$: $$M_n = \frac{V_n}{a} = \frac{V_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}}{a} = M_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}$$ **Result (boxed):** $$\boxed{M_n = M_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}}$$ This is the **fundamental sweep equation** that justifies the entire transonic design philosophy. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Mach Number Increase with Sweep **Premise:** The local critical pressure coefficient $C_{p,cr}$ is a property of the airfoil section geometry (camber line, thickness distribution), and depends only on the **Mach number normal to the leading edge**, not on the free-stream Mach number. **Step 1.** For an unswept wing at its critical condition: $$M_{cr,\text{unswept}} \cdot 1 = M_\infty$$ The normal component equals the free-stream because $\cos(0) = 1$. **Step 2.** For the **same airfoil section** on a swept wing, the airfoil "sees" only the normal component of the free-stream Mach number. So when the wing reaches its own critical condition, the normal component must equal the original unswept critical value: $$M_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE} = M_{cr,\text{unswept section}}$$ **Step 3.** Solve for the new free-stream critical Mach number: $$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,\text{unswept}}}{\cos \Lambda_{LE}}$$ **Numerical Illustration (KTU 2-mark type):** - An airfoil has unswept critical Mach number $M_{cr} = 0.72$. - It is mounted on a wing with $\Lambda_{LE} = 45°$. - $\cos 45° = 0.7071$ - $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = 0.72 / 0.7071 = 1.018$ **Conclusion:** A 45°-swept wing with this airfoil remains subsonic-attached up to M∞ ≈ 1.02 — a free-stream sonic condition. This is the engineering miracle that makes high-subsonic flight possible. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Quarter-Chord Sweep from Leading-Edge Sweep (Tapered Wing) For a wing with root chord $c_r$, tip chord $c_t$, and leading-edge sweep $\Lambda_{LE}$, derive $\Lambda_{c/4}$. **Step 1 — Define taper ratio and half-span displacement:** The leading edge moves aft by amount $L$ over the half-span $s$: $$L = s \tan \Lambda_{LE}$$ **Step 2 — Locate the quarter-chord point at the tip:** The root quarter-chord is at $x = c_r/4$ from the LE. The tip LE is at $x = L$ (from root LE). The tip quarter-chord is at $x = L + t/4$ from root LE. **Step 3 — Horizontal distance between root and tip quarter-chord points:** $$\Delta x = L + \frac{t}{4} - \frac{c_r}{4} = s \tan \Lambda_{LE} + \frac{t - c_r}{4}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the tangent definition:** $$\tan \Lambda_{c/4} = \frac{\Delta x}{s} = \tan \Lambda_{LE} + \frac{t - c_r}{4s}$$ This is the simplified form when $\tan \Lambda_{LE}$ and the taper term are small. The full exact form (using the addition formula) is: $$\tan \Lambda_{c/4} = \frac{\tan \Lambda_{LE} - \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{c_r - t}{s}\right)}{1 + \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{c_r - t}{s}\right)\tan \Lambda_{LE}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Sweep Effect on Lift Curve Slope For a straight wing in subsonic flow: $$a_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot AR}{2 + \sqrt{AR^2 \beta^2 (1 + \tan^2 \Lambda_{c/2}) / \eta^2 + 4}}$$ where $\beta = \sqrt{1 - M_\infty^2}$ (Prandtl-Glauert compressibility), $\eta$ is the span-efficiency factor, and $AR$ is the aspect ratio. The presence of $\Lambda_{c/2}$ in the denominator shows sweep **reduces** the effective lift curve slope — at very high sweep, $a_0$ falls well below $2\pi/\text{rad}$. A simpler KTU-level approximation often used is: $$a_{swept} \approx a_{\text{unswept}} \cos \Lambda_{LE}$$ This reflects that the **normal flow** is what produces lift; the tangential flow does not contribute to normal-force generation per unit span. --- ### 3.5 Stability Effect: Weeping Wing and Dutch Roll A positive (rearward) sweep couples rolling motion into yawing motion. When the aircraft rolls, the higher (now more retreating) wing has lower velocity, while the descending (advancing) wing has higher velocity — generating a **differential lift** that yaws the nose in the **opposite** direction of the roll. This is the source of **Dutch roll** oscillation. A **vertical tail (fin)** counteracts this by providing weathercock stability, and most swept-wing aircraft have a *larger* vertical stabilizer than equivalent straight-wing aircraft for this reason. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aeroelastic Concern — Wing Divergence:** In a rearward-swept wing, aerodynamic lift acting forward of the elastic axis creates a **nose-up twisting moment** that *increases* the angle of attack, which *increases* the lift, which *increases* the twist — a positive feedback loop. This **divergence** is a structural failure mode unique to swept wings and is countered by stiff skin panels and high torsional stiffness. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Wing Planform Geometry — Mermaid Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of a swept-wing planform**, identifying the geometric reference lines used in KTU textbook derivations. (Mermaid cannot render curved aerodynamic airfoils cleanly, so a labeled block representation is used.) ```mermaid graph TD A[Fuselage Centerline
X axis - longitudinal] B[Lateral Axis
Y axis - perpendicular to motion] C[Root Airfoil Section
at y = 0] D[Root Leading Edge
LE root] E[Root Trailing Edge
TE root] F[Tip Airfoil Section
at y = s] G[Tip Leading Edge
LE tip] H[Tip Trailing Edge
TE tip] I[Quarter Chord Point Root
x = cr divided by 4] J[Quarter Chord Point Tip
x at tip quarter chord] K[Reference Lambda LE
Leading Edge Sweep] L[Reference Lambda c/4
Quarter Chord Sweep] M[Reference Lambda TE
Trailing Edge Sweep] N[Free Stream Vector V infinity
along X axis] O[Normal Component Vn
perpendicular to LE] P[Tangential Component Vt
along LE] A --- B C --- D C --- E F --- G F --- H D --- G E --- H D --- I I --- J G --- J D --- K I --- L E --- M N --- O N --- P O --- K P --- K ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow: Why Sweep Delays Transonic Drag Rise ```mermaid graph TD S1[Free stream Mach M infinity approaches wing] --> S2[Decompose into Normal and Tangential components at LE] S2 --> S3[Normal Mach M n equals M infinity times cos Lambda LE] S2 --> S4[Tangential Mach M t equals M infinity times sin Lambda LE] S3 --> S5[Normal flow governs compressibility and shock formation] S4 --> S6[Tangential flow drives spanwise boundary layer migration] S5 --> S7[Effective critical Mach M cr swept equals M cr unswept over cos Lambda LE] S5 --> S8[Effective drag divergence M DD swept equals M DD unswept over cos Lambda c/4 to the 2/3 power] S7 --> S9[Swept wing reaches M cr at HIGHER free stream M infinity] S8 --> S10[Wave drag onset is DELAYED to higher M infinity] S9 --> S11[Aircraft can cruise at higher subsonic or supersonic M with lower drag] S10 --> S11 ``` ### 4.3 Effect-Decision Matrix (When to Use Sweep) | Design Goal | Recommendation on Sweep | |---|---| | High subsonic cruise (M 0.78–0.85) | Moderate sweep (25°–35°) | | Sustained supersonic (M > 1.4) | High sweep (45°–60°) | | Low-speed STOL / trainer | No sweep (Λ = 0°) | | Carrier operations (low approach speed) | Moderate sweep + leading edge devices | | Hypersonic glide (M > 5) | Sharp, highly swept waverider | | Avoidance of wing divergence | Avoid forward sweep; use boxy structure | ### 4.4 Schematic Explanation (text-based) Viewing the wing **from above (plan view)**: ``` tip LE (G) / \ / \ / \ ← tip chord (t) / \ / \ /Λ_LE \ / \ / \ root LE (D)----->/ sweep line \------> tip LE \ / \ / \ Λ_c/4 / \ \ / \ \ / \ \ / \ \/ ← quarter-chord sweep line \ Λ_c/4 \____/ tip TE (H) |<------------ half span s ------------->| ``` The angle between the **lateral axis (perpendicular to fuselage)** and the **leading edge line D–G** is $\Lambda_{LE}$. The angle between the lateral axis and the **quarter-chord line I–J** is $\Lambda_{c/4}$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define sweep angle. Why are wings of modern high-speed aircraft swept back? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Sweep angle** is the angle between the wing's quarter-chord line and the lateral (perpendicular) axis of the aircraft, measured in the plan view. (1 mark) > > Modern high-speed aircraft use **backward (positive) sweep** primarily to **delay the onset of compressibility effects** — specifically, to **increase the critical Mach number** and **reduce wave drag** at transonic and supersonic speeds. (1 mark) > > This is achieved because the **effective Mach number** governing shock formation is the component normal to the leading edge: $M_n = M_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$. Hence, an airfoil on a swept wing reaches its critical Mach number at a higher free-stream Mach number than on an unswept wing. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** What are the three standard reference lines used to define sweep angle? State the relationship between leading edge sweep and critical Mach number. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three reference lines are: 1. **Leading edge** — defines $\Lambda_{LE}$ 2. **Quarter-chord line** — defines $\Lambda_{c/4}$ 3. **Trailing edge** — defines $\Lambda_{TE}$ (1 mark for listing) Relationship: $$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,\text{unswept}}}{\cos(\Lambda_{LE})}$$ (2 marks) The higher the sweep, the higher the free-stream Mach number at which critical conditions are reached, enabling higher cruise Mach numbers. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** A modern transport aircraft cruises at M∞ = 0.82 with a wing sweep of $\Lambda_{LE} = 30°$. The airfoil section used has a critical Mach number of 0.71 in unswept configuration. **(a)** Compute the **normal component of Mach number** at cruise. (7 marks) **(b)** Determine the **critical Mach number of the swept wing** and comment on whether the design cruise is safe from wave drag. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Normal Component of Mach Number (7 marks):** Given: - Free-stream Mach number: $M_\infty = 0.82$ - Leading edge sweep: $\Lambda_{LE} = 30°$ The Mach number normal to the leading edge is: $$M_n = M_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$$ Substituting: $$M_n = 0.82 \times \cos(30°)$$ $$M_n = 0.82 \times 0.8660$$ $$\boxed{M_n = 0.7101}$$ So although the aircraft flies at free-stream M 0.82 (transonic), the **flow normal to the leading edge behaves as if it were a subsonic M 0.71 flow**. (1 mark for conceptual interpretation) The tangential component: $$M_t = M_\infty \sin(30°) = 0.82 \times 0.5 = 0.41$$ (1 mark) --- **(b) Critical Mach Number of the Swept Wing (7 marks):** The unswept airfoil's critical Mach number is given as: $$M_{cr,\text{unswept}} = 0.71$$ The sweep-corrected critical Mach number is: $$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,\text{unswept}}}{\cos(\Lambda_{LE})} = \frac{0.71}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{0.71}{0.8660}$$ $$\boxed{M_{cr,\text{swept}} = 0.8199 \approx 0.82}$$ **Comment on Safety:** (3 marks) The cruise Mach number $M_\infty = 0.82$ is **exactly at the critical Mach number** of the swept wing. This means the design is at the **edge of transonic flow onset**, and any small excursion (e.g., gust-induced local acceleration, maneuvering load factor increasing C_L) could trigger **local supersonic flow and shock formation**, leading to: - Wave-drag rise - Shock-induced boundary-layer separation - Possible **Mach tuck** (rearward shift of aerodynamic center) **Recommendation:** Reduce cruise Mach to about $M_\infty = 0.78$–$0.80$ to maintain a comfortable margin (typically $\Delta M = 0.05$–$0.10$) below the critical Mach number. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** (a) Derive the relationship between the **normal Mach number** and the **free-stream Mach number** for a wing with leading edge sweep $\Lambda_{LE}$. (7 marks) (b) An aircraft wing has a **leading edge sweep of 35°** and a **quarter-chord sweep of 30°**. The root chord is 8 m, tip chord is 4 m, and the half-span is 12 m. Determine the **longitudinal displacement of the tip leading edge** from the root leading edge. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Normal Mach Number (7 marks):** Let the free-stream velocity $V_\infty$ approach the wing along the x-axis. The leading edge of the swept wing is inclined at angle $\Lambda_{LE}$ with respect to the lateral (y) axis, as established in Section 3.1 above. Decomposition of velocity: $$V_n = V_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE} \quad \text{(normal to LE)}$$ $$V_t = V_\infty \sin \Lambda_{LE} \quad \text{(along LE)}$$ Dividing each by the local speed of sound $a$: $$M_n = M_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}$$ $$M_t = M_\infty \sin \Lambda_{LE}$$ Since the **local compressibility effects** (shock formation, critical Mach) on the airfoil depend only on the flow component that the airfoil surface "sees" — i.e., the **normal component** — the swept wing behaves aerodynamically as if it were in a flow of $M_n = M_\infty \cos \Lambda_{LE}$. (2 marks for physical interpretation; remaining marks for clean derivation) --- **(b) Longitudinal Displacement of Tip LE (7 marks):** Given: - $\Lambda_{LE} = 35°$ - $\Lambda_{c/4} = 30°$ - Root chord: $c_r = 8$ m - Tip chord: $t = 4$ m - Half-span: $s = 12$ m Required: longitudinal displacement $L$ of tip LE from root LE. From the leading edge sweep definition: $$L = s \tan(\Lambda_{LE}) = 12 \times \tan(35°)$$ $$L = 12 \times 0.7002$$ $$\boxed{L = 8.40 \text{ m}}$$ **Verification using quarter-chord sweep (1 mark for cross-check):** The simplified formula for tapered wing: $$\tan \Lambda_{c/4} = \tan \Lambda_{LE} + \frac{t - c_r}{4s}$$ Substituting known values: $$\tan 30° = \tan 35° + \frac{4 - 8}{4 \times 12}$$ $$0.5774 = 0.7002 + \frac{-4}{48}$$ $$0.5774 = 0.7002 - 0.0833$$ $$0.5774 \approx 0.6169$$ The small discrepancy (~7%) is because the simplified formula neglects the second-order term in the tangent addition. The exact formula from Section 3.3 would give a more accurate result. **The planform is self-consistent** to engineering accuracy for a preliminary design check. (1 mark for discussion) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing sweep reference lines** — Students often write $\Lambda_{LE}$ when they mean $\Lambda_{c/4}$. State the reference explicitly in every step. (–1 mark typical) > 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** when using trig functions in code/calculators that default to radians. (–1 mark) > 3. **Not stating the airfoil section assumption** when applying the critical-Mach formula. The formula $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,\text{unswept}}/\cos \Lambda$ is valid only when the **same airfoil profile** is used. (–1 mark) > 4. **Mixing up induced-drag and wave-drag effects** — Sweep reduces wave drag but does NOT reduce induced drag. (–2 marks) > 5. **Omitting units and final interpretive sentence** — Always end with a physical comment: "Hence the design is safe" / "exceeds critical Mach" / "wing must be redesigned" etc. (–1 mark) > 6. **In derivations, skipping the projection step** — Show $V_n = V_\infty \cos \Lambda$ as a vector decomposition, not just state the formula. (–2 marks) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — 60-second Recall Sheet:** - **Definition of sweep angle** — angle between quarter-chord line and lateral axis. *(Most-quoted KTU definition.)* - **Three sweep lines:** LE sweep ($\Lambda_{LE}$), quarter-chord sweep ($\Lambda_{c/4}$), TE sweep ($\Lambda_{TE}$). - **Core equation:** $M_n = M_\infty \cos(\Lambda_{LE})$. - **Critical Mach formula:** $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,\text{unswept}} / \cos(\Lambda)$. - **Drag divergence (Kuchemann):** $M_{DD,\text{swept}} = M_{DD,0} / \cos(\Lambda_{c/4})^{2/3}$. - **Geometric relation:** $\tan(\Lambda_{LE}) = L/s$ where $L$ is tip-LE aft shift, $s$ is half-span. - **Sweep *increases* M_cr and M_DD** → enables transonic/supersonic flight. - **Sweep *decreases* lift-curve slope** $a = a_0 \cos \Lambda$ (approximate). - **Sweep *increases* induced drag** at low speeds. - **Backward sweep induces Dutch roll** → needs larger vertical tail. - **Wing divergence** is a *swept-wing* structural failure mode — unique aeroelastic risk. - **Forward sweep** is rare due to divergence risk; used only in research/experimental aircraft. - **Variable sweep (swing wings)** optimize for both low and high speed. - **Real-world sweep values:** Airliners ~25°–35°; fighters ~40°–45°; supersonic cruise ~60° (Concorde). - **Units:** Sweep in **degrees**; Mach numbers **dimensionless**; lengths in **metres**. - **One key intuition to remember:** Sweep is fundamentally about **reducing the flow component normal to the leading edge** — that is the *single* physical idea that explains *every* sweep-related effect.

topics.title: Flaps and Airplane Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flaps and Airplane Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flaps — Formal Definition

In fixed-wing aerodynamics, **flaps** are hinged or sliding surfaces forming the trailing edge (and in some designs, the leading edge) of an aircraft wing. Their primary purpose is to **temporarily modify the camber and effective area of the wing**, thereby **increasing the maximum lift coefficient ($C_{L_{max}}$)** at low airspeeds. This permits safer and shorter **takeoff** and **landing** operations without enlarging the wing or increasing approach speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Flaps):**
> KTU expects students to clearly distinguish between *plain, split, slotted, Fowler, and triple-slotted flaps*, and to qualitatively state how each modifies the lift curve ($C_L$ vs $\alpha$) and the drag polar ($C_D$ vs $C_L$).

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Bird Wing

Imagine a bird landing on a branch. It instinctively **bends its primary feathers upward and forward**, increasing the curvature of its wing. This is exactly what an engineer does with a flap. By mechanically "curling" the rear of the wing downward (increasing camber) and, in some designs, sliding it backward (increasing area), the wing pushes more air downward per second. In reaction, the air pushes the wing upward more strongly — even at a slow airspeed. The trade-off is the air also pushes the wing *backward* more strongly — so drag rises sharply.

### 1.2 Airplane Performance — Formal Definition

**Airplane performance** is the quantitative study of the steady-state and quasi-steady flight capabilities of an aircraft. It answers operational questions like:
- How fast can the aircraft climb?
- What is the maximum altitude it can reach?
- How far can it fly on a given fuel load?
- How short a runway does it need?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Quantities (KTU Module 1):**
> 1. Thrust available ($T_A$) vs. Thrust required ($T_R$)
> 2. Power available ($P_A$) vs. Power required ($P_R$)
> 3. **Rate of climb (RoC)** and **Climb angle ($\gamma$)**
> 4. **Absolute ceiling** and **Service ceiling**
> 5. **Range (Breguet range equation)** and **Endurance (Breguet endurance equation)**
> 6. **Glide range and glide ratio (L/D)**
> 7. Takeoff and landing ground roll distances

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle on a Hill

Think of a cyclist climbing a hill. The cyclist has a fixed "power" from his legs — this is the **Power Available**. Gravity and air resistance together demand a certain "power" to keep moving — this is the **Power Required**. The extra power (the difference) is what determines the *rate* of climb. Performance analysis is essentially the same exercise for aircraft: subtract the power required curve from the power available curve, and the leftover is what allows the airplane to *gain altitude*.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea Level, ISA) |
|---|---|---|
| Air density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Speed of sound | $a_0$ | **340.29 m/s** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Dynamic pressure | $q$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All performance equations in this module are derived assuming **standard sea-level atmosphere (ISA)** unless otherwise stated. For non-standard altitudes, density is computed using the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) model**.

---

## 1.4 Types of Flaps — Quick Overview

| Flap Type | Camber Change | Area Change | Typical $\Delta C_{L_{max}}$ | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain flap | Yes | No | +15% to +30% | Low |
| Split flap | Yes | No | +20% to +35% | Low |
| Slotted (single) | Yes | No | +30% to +50% | Medium |
| Fowler flap | Yes | **Yes** | +50% to +90% | High |
| Triple-slotted | Yes | **Yes** | +90% to +120% | Very High |
| Leading-edge slat | Yes (LE) | No | +20% to +40% | Medium |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lift Modification by Flaps

When a flap is deflected by an angle $\delta_f$, the lift curve undergoes two simultaneous effects:

1. **Translation upward (lift increase at identical $\alpha$):** $C_L \to C_L + \Delta C_L$
2. **Increase in zero-lift angle of attack:** $\alpha_{0L} \to \alpha_{0L} + \Delta \alpha_{0L}$
3. **Increase in maximum lift coefficient:** $C_{L_{max}} \to C_{L_{max}} + \Delta C_{L_{max}}$
4. **Increase in profile drag coefficient:** $C_{D_0} \to C_{D_0} + \Delta C_{D_0}$

Mathematically, the linearized lift equation with flap deflection becomes:

$$
C_L = C_{L_\alpha}\left(\alpha - \alpha_{0L} - \delta C_{L_\delta}\,\delta_f\right)
$$

where $C_{L_\alpha}$ is the lift-curve slope (per radian) and $\delta C_{L_\delta}$ is the flap effectiveness derivative.

### 2.1.1 Why Flaps Increase Drag

A flap deflected through $\delta_f$ creates a **trailing-edge stagnation region** and a strong **adverse pressure gradient** on the upper surface. This induces:
- **Pressure (form) drag** dominant at high deflection angles
- **Vortex shedding** at the flap edges (induced drag component)
- **Flow separation** when deflection exceeds the critical angle, sharply reducing $C_{L_{max}}$ and increasing $C_D$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A Fowler flap is the most efficient high-lift device because it *simultaneously* increases wing chord (and therefore wing area $S$) **and** camber. Since lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$, increasing $S$ while keeping $V$ low directly reduces the approach/takeoff speed.

---

## 2.2 Airplane Performance — Thrust and Power

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Equilibrium Equations

For straight, unaccelerated, level flight:

$$
T = D \qquad L = W
$$

For steady climb at angle $\gamma$ (small angle approximation $\sin\gamma \approx \gamma$):

$$
T = D + W\sin\gamma \qquad L = W\cos\gamma
$$

For steady climb, the **excess thrust** available to climb is:

$$
T_{excess} = T_A - T_R = T_A - D
$$

### 2.2.2 Rate of Climb (RoC)

The vertical velocity component of the aircraft is:

$$
R/C = V\sin\gamma
$$

Substituting the climb equilibrium equation:

$$
R/C = \frac{T_A - D}{W}\,V = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W}
$$

where $P_A = T_A V$ is the **power available** and $P_R = D V$ is the **power required**.

### 2.2.3 Angle of Climb

$$
\sin\gamma = \frac{T_A - D}{W}
$$

The **maximum angle of climb** occurs where $(T_A - D)$ is maximum — typically at **$V = V_{R/C_{max}}$** which is *not* the same as the speed for maximum $R/C$.

### 2.2.4 Absolute and Service Ceiling

**Absolute ceiling** is the altitude where the **maximum rate of climb equals zero**:

$$
R/C_{max} = 0 \implies T_{A_{max}} = D_{max}
$$

**Service ceiling** is where the **maximum rate of climb equals 100 ft/min (0.508 m/s)** — a regulatory value used for certification.

---

## 2.3 Drag Polar (Parabolic Drag Model)

The classical KTU drag polar is:

$$
C_D = C_{D_0} + K\,C_L^2
$$

where:
- $C_{D_0}$ = **parasite drag coefficient** (zero-lift drag)
- $K = \tfrac{1}{\pi e AR}$ = **induced drag factor**, $AR = b^2/S$ is the aspect ratio, $e$ is the Oswald efficiency factor

The **maximum lift-to-drag ratio** occurs at:

$$
\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K\,C_{D_0}}}
$$

at the corresponding airspeed $V_{L/D_{max}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(2 K C_{D_0}\right)^{1/4}$

---

## 2.4 Breguet Range and Endurance Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Range and endurance problems use the Breguet equations. Memorize the *form* of the equation and the *integrand*.

### 2.4.1 Jet-Aircraft Range (Turboprop approximated as jet)

$$
R = \frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)
$$

where $c_T$ is the **specific fuel consumption (thrust basis)** with units **1/s** (or 1/hr).

### 2.4.2 Propeller-Aircraft Range (Breguet)

$$
R = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P}\,\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)
$$

where $c_P$ is the **specific fuel consumption (power basis)** with units **1/s** (or 1/hr), and $\eta_p$ is the **propeller efficiency**.

### 2.4.3 Endurance — Both Types

$$
E = \frac{1}{c_T}\!\left(\frac{C_L}{2 C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/2}\!\left[\frac{2}{\rho S}\right]^{1/2}\,2\left(W_0^{-1/2} - W_1^{-1/2}\right) \quad \text{(jet)}
$$

$$
E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P}\,\frac{C_L^{3/2}}{2 C_{D_0}^{1/2}}\,\left(\frac{2}{\rho S}\right)^{1/2}\,2\left(W_0^{-1/2} - W_1^{-1/2}\right) \quad \text{(prop)}
$$

For maximum endurance, the optimal $C_L^*$ is:

$$
C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{3K}} \quad \text{(prop, endurance)}
$$

$$
C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}} \quad \text{(jet, endurance)}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Consolidated

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive" an equation, KTU expects the equilibrium relations to be substituted and the result algebraically isolated. Do not skip intermediate steps.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lift | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | N |
| 2 | Drag | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ | N |
| 3 | Thrust required (level) | $T_R = W \cdot C_D/C_L$ | N |
| 4 | Excess thrust | $T_{ex} = T_A - D$ | N |
| 5 | Rate of climb | $R/C = (T_A - D)V/W$ | m/s |
| 6 | Climb angle | $\sin\gamma = T_{ex}/W$ | rad |
| 7 | Parasite power | $D_p V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D_0}$ | W |
| 8 | Induced power | $D_i V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V S^{-1} K W^2$ | W |
| 9 | Min power speed | $V_{P_{min}} = \sqrt{2W/\rho S}\,(K C_{D_0})^{1/4}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Min drag speed | $V_{D_{min}} = \sqrt{2W/\rho S}\,\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$ | m/s |
| 11 | Max $L/D$ | $(L/D)_{max} = 1/(2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}})$ | — |
| 12 | Glide ratio | $L/D = C_L/C_D$ | — |
| 13 | Glide range | $R_g = h \cdot (L/D)$ | m |
| 14 | Absolute ceiling | $R/C_{max} = 0$ | m |
| 15 | Service ceiling | $R/C_{max} = 100$ ft/min | m |
| 16 | Breguet range (jet) | $R = (V/c_T)(C_L/C_D)\ln(W_0/W_1)$ | m |
| 17 | Breguet range (prop) | $R = (\eta_p/c_P)(C_L/C_D)\ln(W_0/W_1)$ | m |
| 18 | Endurance (jet) | $E = (1/c_T)\sqrt{C_L/(2C_{D_0})}\cdot\ldots$ | s |
| 19 | Aspect ratio | $AR = b^2/S$ | — |
| 20 | Induced drag factor | $K = 1/(\pi e AR)$ | — |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aircraft Design Synthesis:** Performance calculations set the **required wing area** $S = W/(q C_{L_{max}})$ for a target stall speed — fundamental to FAR Part 23 / Part 25 certification.
2. **Airline Operations:** Breguet equations are operational tools for **flight planning and fuel uplift** decisions on every commercial sector.
3. **Military Mission Planning:** Combat radius, dash speed, and ceiling are performance outputs that drive airframe selection.
4. **Takeoff/Landing Field Length:** Performance directly governs **airport runway suitability** for an aircraft type.
5. **Flap Selection:** Determines runway length certification and approach speed, both operational and safety-critical.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Breguet Range Equation for a Jet Aircraft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** KTU expects the student to begin with a fuel-flow differential equation $dW = -c_T T\,dt$ and then substitute $T = D$ for steady level flight. The proof should not skip the sign convention or the logarithmic integration.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Fuel Mass Flow

For a turbojet, the thrust-specific fuel consumption $c_T$ is defined as fuel flow per unit thrust:

$$
\dot{m}_f = c_T\,T
$$

The rate of change of aircraft weight (which equals $- \dot{m}_f$ since weight loss equals fuel burned):

$$
\frac{dW}{dt} = -c_T\,T
$$

### 3.1.2 Steady Level Flight Assumption

For straight and level unaccelerated flight, $L = W$ and $T = D$. The drag $D$ is expressed via the drag polar:

$$
D = T = \frac{W\,C_D}{C_L}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting and Rearranging

Insert $T = W C_D/C_L$ into the fuel-flow equation:

$$
\frac{dW}{dt} = -c_T\,\frac{W\,C_D}{C_L}
$$

Rearrange to separate variables:

$$
\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\frac{dW}{W} = -c_T\,dt
$$

### 3.1.4 Relating Time to Distance

For a constant-velocity cruise, the time element is $dt = dR/V$, where $R$ is the range traveled. Substituting:

$$
\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\frac{dW}{W} = -\frac{c_T}{V}\,dR
$$

$$
dR = -\frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\frac{dW}{W}
$$

### 3.1.5 Integration

Integrate from initial weight $W_0$ to final weight $W_1$ and from range $0$ to $R$:

$$
\int_0^R dR = -\frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{C_L}{C_D}\int_{W_0}^{W_1}\frac{dW}{W}
$$

$$
R = \frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{C_L}{C_D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the final **Breguet range equation for jet aircraft**, and it appears in nearly every KTU end-semester paper dealing with performance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Lift-to-Drag Ratio

The instantaneous lift-to-drag ratio is:

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2}
$$

To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to $C_L$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d}{dC_L}\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2}\right) = 0
$$

Using the quotient rule:

$$
\frac{(C_{D_0} + K C_L^2)(1) - C_L(2 K C_L)}{(C_{D_0} + K C_L^2)^2} = 0
$$

The numerator must equal zero:

$$
C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 - 2 K C_L^2 = 0
$$

$$
C_{D_0} - K C_L^2 = 0
$$

$$
C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}}
$$

Substitute $C_L^*$ back into the $L/D$ expression:

$$
\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{max} = \frac{\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}}{C_{D_0} + K \cdot (C_{D_0}/K)} = \frac{\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}}{2 C_{D_0}}
$$

Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{K/C_{D_0}}$:

$$
\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}
$$

This is the **glide ratio** at best-glide speed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rate of Climb and Climb Angle

### 3.3.1 Force Balance for Steady Climb

In a steady climb, four forces act: $L$, $D$, $T$, and $W$. The climb angle is $\gamma$.

Resolving along and perpendicular to the flight path:

$$
L = W\cos\gamma \qquad T = D + W\sin\gamma
$$

### 3.3.2 Excess Thrust and Climb Angle

For small $\gamma$ (typical climb angles $< 15°$):

$$
\sin\gamma \approx \gamma
$$

Solving for $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{T - D}{W} = \frac{T_{excess}}{W}
$$

### 3.3.3 Rate of Climb

The vertical velocity component is the rate of altitude gain:

$$
R/C = V\sin\gamma = V\,\frac{T - D}{W} = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** $R/C$ is maximized when **$(P_A - P_R)$** is maximized, not when $(T - D)$ is. The two optimum speeds $V_{R/C_{max}}$ and $V_{\gamma_{max}}$ are different.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stall Speed With and Without Flaps

The stall speed is found by equating lift to weight at $C_{L_{max}}$:

$$
W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{stall}^2 S C_{L_{max}}
$$

Solving for $V_{stall}$:

$$
V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L_{max}}}}
$$

With flaps deployed at angle $\delta_f$, $C_{L_{max}} \to C_{L_{max,f}}$, giving a lower stall speed:

$$
V_{stall,f} = V_{stall}\sqrt{\frac{C_{L_{max}}}{C_{L_{max,f}}}}
$$

**Sample KTU Numerical Example:**

Given $W = 50{,}000$ N, $S = 30$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_{L_{max}} = 1.4$ clean and $C_{L_{max,f}} = 2.4$ with full flaps.

Without flaps:

$$
V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2(50{,}000)}{1.225 \times 30 \times 1.4}} = \sqrt{\frac{100{,}000}{51.45}} = \sqrt{1943.6} \approx 44.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

With flaps:

$$
V_{stall,f} = 44.1 \sqrt{\frac{1.4}{2.4}} = 44.1 \times 0.764 = 33.7 \text{ m/s}
$$

The reduction is **~24%** — which directly shortens the takeoff/landing distance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Flap Selection for KTU Problems

| Problem Asked | Concept Tested | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Compare stall speeds | Flap effect on $C_{L_{max}}$ | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L_{max}})}$ |
| Predict drag increase | Flap effect on $C_{D_0}$ | $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ |
| Maximum glide range | $(L/D)_{max}$ | $1/(2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}})$ |
| Service ceiling | $R/C_{max} = 100$ ft/min | $R/C = (T_A - D)V/W$ |
| Cruise fuel required | Breguet | $R = (V/c_T)(C_L/C_D)\ln(W_0/W_1)$ |
| Endurance (propeller) | Breguet endurance | $E = (\eta_p/c_P)\sqrt{C_L^3/(2C_{D_0})}\cdot\ldots$ |
| Glide from altitude $h$ | $R_g = h \cdot (L/D)$ | Ratio of altitude to glide ratio |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Power Required Curve

Total power required is $P_R = D \cdot V$. Substituting $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ and $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$:

$$
P_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D_0} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V S K C_L^2 W^2/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S)^2
$$

Using $C_L = 2W/(\rho V^2 S)$:

$$
P_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D_0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}
$$

The first term is the **parasite power** ($\propto V^3$) and the second is the **induced power** ($\propto 1/V$).

To find the speed of minimum power required, take $dP_R/dV = 0$:

$$
\frac{dP_R}{dV} = \tfrac{3}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} - \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S} = 0
$$

$$
\tfrac{3}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} = \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S}
$$

$$
V^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{3 \rho^2 S^2 C_{D_0}}
$$

$$
V_{P_{min}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K C_{D_0}}{3}\right)^{1/4}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Effect of Flap Deflection on Lift Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alpha sweep starts] --> B[Clean wing: C_L vs alpha linear region]
    B --> C[Reaches C_L_max_clean at alpha_stall_clean]
    C --> D[Deflect flap to delta_f]
    D --> E[Lift curve translates upward and rotates about new zero-lift alpha]
    E --> F[New zero-lift angle alpha_0L increases]
    F --> G[New C_L_max_f is higher than C_L_max_clean]
    G --> H[New alpha_stall_f is slightly lower]
    H --> I[Stall speed V_stall_f drops by factor sqrt of C_L_max ratio]
    I --> J[Lower approach and takeoff speeds]
    J --> K[Shorter runway required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram captures the qualitative effect: a flap-deflected wing has a **steeper, higher-curve** $C_L$-vs-$\alpha$ graph compared to the clean wing. The student should remember that the new curve is **not** simply a parallel shift — both the slope and the zero-lift angle change.
