| 4 | Pressure (Troposphere) | $P = P_0 \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\tfrac{g}{R L}}$ | $0 \le h \le 11\,\text{km}$ | Exponent $= 5.2559$ |
| 5 | Density (Troposphere) | $\rho = \rho_0 \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\tfrac{g}{R L}-1}$ | $0 \le h \le 11\,\text{km}$ | Exponent $= 4.2559$ |
| 6 | Pressure (Isothermal Layer) | $P = P_1 \, \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{g(h-h_1)}{R T_1}\right]$ | $11 \le h \le 20\,\text{km}$ | $T_1 = 216.65\,\text{K}$ |
| 7 | Density (Isothermal Layer) | $\rho = \rho_1 \, \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{g(h-h_1)}{R T_1}\right]$ | $11 \le h \le 20\,\text{km}$ | $\rho_1 = 0.3639\,\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 8 | Speed of Sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | All | $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| 9 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | All | — |
| 10 | Geopotential Altitude | $H = \dfrac{r_0 \, h}{r_0 + h}$ | All | $r_0 = 6\,356\,766\,\text{m}$ |
| 11 | Pressure Altitude (ICAO) | $h_p = 44\,330 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{1013.25}\right)^{0.190263}\right]\,\text{ft}$ | Troposphere | — |
| 12 | Density Altitude | $h_\rho = h_p + \dfrac{T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{act}}}{L}$ | — | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** The troposphere exponent $g/(RL) \approx 5.256$ is a *constant you must memorize*. It comes from substituting $g = 9.80665$, $R = 287.05$, $L = 0.0065$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Barometric Formula in the Troposphere

> **Goal:** Derive an expression for $P(h)$ in the troposphere, given $T(h) = T_0 - L h$.

**Step 1 — Start with the hydrostatic + ideal-gas combined equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R \, T} \, dh$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linear temperature profile:**

$$T = T_0 - L \, h \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dT = -L \, dh \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dh = -\frac{dT}{L}$$

Substitute into the equation:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R \, T} \left( -\frac{dT}{L} \right) = \frac{g}{R \, L} \, \frac{dT}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from sea-level conditions $(P_0, T_0)$ to a generic altitude $h$ with $(P, T)$:**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{g}{R \, L} \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{g}{R \, L} \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides to remove the natural log:**

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R \, L}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P(h) = P_0 \left(\frac{T_0 - L h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R L}} = P_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the density expression using the ideal gas law:**

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R \, T} = \frac{P_0}{R T} \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R L}}$$

Divide by $\rho_0 = P_0 / (R T_0)$:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \frac{T_0}{T} \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R L}} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R L} - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\,\rho(h) = \rho_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{4.2559}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation B: Barometric Formula in the Lower Stratosphere (Isothermal, 11–20 km)

> **Goal:** Derive $P(h)$ when temperature is **constant** at $T_1 = 216.65 \, \text{K}$.

**Step 1 — With $T = \text{constant}$, the combined equation becomes:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R \, T_1} \, dh$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from the tropopause $(P_1, h_1 = 11\,000\,\text{m})$ to $(P, h)$:**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R \, T_1} \int_{h_1}^{h} dh$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{g}{R \, T_1} (h - h_1)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P$:**

$$\boxed{\,P(h) = P_1 \, \exp\!\left[-\frac{g(h - h_1)}{R \, T_1}\right]\,}$$

**Step 4 — Density in the isothermal layer (using $P = \rho R T_1$):**

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_1} = \frac{P}{P_1} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_1} = \frac{P}{P_1}$$

$$\boxed{\,\rho(h) = \rho_1 \, \exp\!\left[-\frac{g(h - h_1)}{R \, T_1}\right]\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]:** A commercial aircraft cruises at a geometric altitude of **8 km**. Using the standard atmosphere, calculate the **pressure, density, and speed of sound** at this altitude.

**Given:**
$h = 8\,000\,\text{m}$, $T_0 = 288.15\,\text{K}$, $P_0 = 101\,325\,\text{Pa}$, $\rho_0 = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $L = 0.0065\,\text{K/m}$, $R = 287.05\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 1 — Temperature at 8 km:**

$$T = T_0 - L h = 288.15 - (0.0065)(8000)$$

$$T = 288.15 - 52.0 = 236.15 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure using the tropospheric formula:**

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559} = \left(\frac{236.15}{288.15}\right)^{5.2559}$$

Compute the ratio first:

$$\frac{T}{T_0} = 0.81954$$

Raise to the 5.2559 power:

$$0.81954^{5.2559} = \exp\!\left(5.2559 \cdot \ln 0.81954\right) = \exp\!\left(5.2559 \cdot (-0.19912)\right) = \exp(-1.0466)$$

$$0.81954^{5.2559} \approx 0.3511$$

Therefore:

$$P = 0.3511 \times 101\,325 \approx 35\,573 \, \text{Pa} \approx 35.57 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Density using $\rho = P/(R T)$:**

$$\rho = \frac{35\,573}{287.05 \times 236.15} = \frac{35\,573}{67\,792} \approx 0.5247 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Speed of sound:**

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287.05 \times 236.15}$$

$$a = \sqrt{94\,905.6} \approx 308.07 \, \text{m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** At $h = 8\,\text{km}$: $T = 236.15\,\text{K}$, $P \approx 35.57\,\text{kPa}$, $\rho \approx 0.525\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $a \approx 308.1\,\text{m/s}$.

---

### 3.4 Geopotential vs Geometric Altitude — Quick Derivation

> **Why is geopotential altitude needed?** Because gravity $g$ decreases with altitude, the "effective" height for hydrostatic integration uses geopotential altitude.

$$H = \frac{r_0 \, h}{r_0 + h}, \qquad r_0 = 6\,356\,766 \, \text{m (effective Earth radius)}$$

Inverse form:

$$h = \frac{r_0 \, H}{r_0 - H}$$

For engineering accuracy, all ICAO tables are tabulated against **geopotential altitude $H$**, with conversion to geometric altitude $h$ applied afterward.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Standard Atmosphere Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Altitude h in km] --> B{h le 11 km?}
    B -- YES --> C[Troposphere Branch]
    B -- NO --> D{h le 20 km?}
    D -- YES --> E[Lower Stratosphere Branch]
    D -- NO --> F[Upper Stratosphere / Mesosphere]

    C --> C1[Compute T = T0 minus L times h]
    C1 --> C2[Compute P = P0 times T over T0 raised to 5.2559]
    C2 --> C3[Compute rho = P over R times T]
    C3 --> G[Derive a = sqrt of gamma R T]

    E --> E1[Set T1 = 216.65 K constant]
    E1 --> E2[Compute P = P1 times exp of minus g times h minus h1 over R times T1]
    E2 --> E3[Compute rho = P over R times T1]
    E3 --> G

    F --> F1[Use piecewise lapse rate for layer]
    F1 --> G

    G --> H[OUTPUT: P, rho, a, T, Mach M = V over a]
    H --> I[END]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Layered Architecture: Atmospheric Thermal Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L0[Troposphere 0 to 11 km]
        T0[Sea Level T0 equals 288.15 K]
        TROP[Lapse L equals 0.0065 K per m]
        T11[Tropopause T11 equals 216.65 K]
    end

    subgraph L1[Lower Stratosphere 11 to 20 km]
        ISO[Isothermal T equals 216.65 K]
    end

    subgraph L2[Upper Stratosphere 20 to 32 km]
        S20[Stratopause T equals 216.65 K]
        INV[Inversion L equals minus 1.0 K per km]
    end

    T0 --> TROP --> T11 --> ISO --> S20 --> INV

    style L0 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Altimetry & Air Data Computation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Static Pressure Sensor P_static] --> ALT[Altimeter Computation]
    SENS --> MACH[Mach Meter Computation]
    SENS --> ASI[Air Speed Indicator ASI]
    SENS --> VSI[Vertical Speed Indicator VSI]
    TAT[Total Air Temperature TAT] --> SAT[Static Air Temperature SAT]
    ALT --> H_P[Pressure Altitude h_P]
    MACH --> M[Mach Number M]
    ASI --> V_CAS[Calibrated Air Speed]
    VSI --> ROC[Rate of Climb]
    H_P --> DA[Density Altitude]
    H_P --> ISA_Dev[ISA Deviation]
    DA --> ENG[Engine Performance Adjustment]
    ISA_Dev --> ENG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** Board examiners often award marks for a clean **block-level flow** showing how static pressure is processed into various cockpit instruments. The architecture above is directly aligned with **Air Data Computer (ADC)** functionality tested in KTU 2024 scheme modules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA). State the standard sea-level values of temperature, pressure, and density.

**Model Answer:**

> The **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** is a static, mathematical model of the Earth's atmosphere that defines a fixed, average profile of temperature, pressure, and density versus altitude, used as a universal reference for aviation, aerospace, and meteorological calculations.
>
> - **Sea-level temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15 \, \text{K}$ (15 °C)
> - **Sea-level pressure:** $P_0 = 101\,325 \, \text{Pa}$ (1013.25 hPa)
> - **Sea-level density:** $\rho_0 = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is the significance of the **tropospheric lapse rate** in the standard atmosphere, and what is its numerical value?

**Model Answer:**

> The tropospheric lapse rate $L$ defines the **rate at which temperature decreases with altitude** in the troposphere (0–11 km). Its standard value is
> $$L = 6.5 \, \text{K/km} = 0.0065 \, \text{K/m}$$
> It is the key input to the tropospheric barometric formula
> $$T = T_0 - L h$$
> and is used in deriving the exponents $g/(RL) = 5.2559$ for pressure and $g/(RL) - 1 = 4.2559$ for density.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from the hydrostatic equation and the ideal gas law, derive the **barometric formula for pressure in the troposphere**. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A jet aircraft flies at a geometric altitude of **6 km**. Using the standard atmosphere, calculate the **pressure, density, and speed of sound** at this altitude. State the relationship between $P$, $\rho$, and $T$ as used. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Assumptions:
1. The air is a **perfect (ideal) gas**.
2. The atmosphere is **static** (no vertical motion).
3. Gravity $g$ is **constant** at 9.80665 m/s².
4. Temperature varies **linearly** in the troposphere: $T = T_0 - L h$.

From the hydrostatic equation:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho g$$

Using the ideal gas law: $\rho = P / (R T)$, substitute:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\frac{P g}{R T} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R T} \, dh$$

Since $T = T_0 - L h$, then $dh = -dT / L$:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{g}{R L} \cdot \frac{dT}{T}$$

Integrate from sea level to altitude $h$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{g}{R L} \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{g}{R L} \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

$$P = P_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{g/(R L)} = P_0 \left(\frac{T_0 - L h}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Temperature:**

$$T = T_0 - L h = 288.15 - (0.0065)(6000) = 288.15 - 39.0 = 249.15 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure:**

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{249.15}{288.15}\right)^{5.2559} = (0.8645)^{5.2559}$$

$$\ln(0.8645) = -0.14565 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5.2559 \times (-0.14565) = -0.7655$$

$$P = 101\,325 \times e^{-0.7655} = 101\,325 \times 0.4653 \approx 47\,143 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 47.14 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Density (using $\rho = P / (R T)$):**

$$\rho = \frac{47\,143}{287.05 \times 249.15} = \frac{47\,143}{71\,511} \approx 0.6592 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 0.659 \, \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Speed of sound:**

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287.05 \times 249.15}$$

$$a = \sqrt{100\,107.6} \approx 316.39 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 316.4 \, \text{m/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **vertical thermal structure** of the standard atmosphere up to 32 km. List the temperature gradient (lapse rate) in each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reconnaissance aircraft operates at **15 km** altitude. Using the **isothermal lower-stratospheric model**, compute the local **pressure, density, and temperature**. State the assumption regarding temperature in this layer. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Layered Structure:**

| Layer | Altitude Range | Lapse Rate | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Troposphere | 0 – 11 km | $-6.5 \, \text{K/km}$ | Linear decrease |
| Tropopause | 11 km | Boundary | $T = 216.65 \, \text{K}$ |
| Lower Stratosphere | 11 – 20 km | $0 \, \text{K/km}$ (isothermal) | Constant $T$ |
| Upper Stratosphere | 20 – 32 km | $+1.0 \, \text{K/km}$ (inversion) | Temperature rises |

A clean schematic (use the Mermaid block in Section 4) should be drawn showing the temperature–altitude profile with sharp bends at 11 km and 20 km.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution at 15 km:**

**Assumption:** In the lower stratosphere (11–20 km), temperature is **isothermal** at $T_1 = 216.65 \, \text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Required boundary values at 11 km:**

$$T_1 = 216.65 \, \text{K}, \qquad \rho_1 = 0.3639 \, \text{kg/m}^3, \qquad P_1 = 22\,632 \, \text{Pa}$$

(These are tabulated ICAO values; alternatively, derive $P_1$ from the tropospheric formula.)

**Step 2 — Exponent factor:**

$$\frac{g(h - h_1)}{R T_1} = \frac{9.80665 \times (15\,000 - 11\,000)}{287.05 \times 216.65}$$

$$= \frac{9.80665 \times 4000}{62\,173.5} = \frac{39\,226.6}{62\,173.5} = 0.6309$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at 15 km:**

$$P = P_1 \times e^{-0.6309} = 22\,632 \times 0.5322$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 12\,045 \, \text{Pa} \approx 12.05 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Density at 15 km:**

$$\rho = \rho_1 \times e^{-0.6309} = 0.3639 \times 0.5322$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 0.1937 \, \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 5 — Temperature at 15 km:**

$$\boxed{T = 216.65 \, \text{K} \quad (\text{isothermal assumption})}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on Standard Atmosphere questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent value** — Many students write the tropospheric pressure equation without the exponent $g/(RL) = 5.2559$. They write $P = P_0 (T/T_0)$, which is dimensionally wrong and earns **0 marks** for the formula.
> 2. **Mixing up geometric and geopotential altitude** — ICAO tables use **geopotential** altitude. Above 11 km, this is critical; using the wrong altitude changes the pressure by a few percent.
> 3. **Wrong lapse-rate sign** — In the upper stratosphere, the lapse rate is **+1.0 K/km** (inversion), not $-1.0$. Marks are cut for sign errors.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — Always state final answers with units (Pa or kPa for pressure, kg/m³ for density, m/s for speed of sound).
> 5. **Using $R = 8.314$** — This is the universal gas constant. The correct value for *dry air* is **$R = 287.05 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$**. Examiners specifically watch for this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standard Atmosphere**

- **Definition:** ISA = a fixed, mathematical model of the Earth’s atmosphere used as a universal engineering reference.
- **Sea-level reference values:** $T_0 = 288.15\,\text{K}$, $P_0 = 101\,325\,\text{Pa}$, $\rho_0 = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Specific gas constant for dry air:** $R = 287.05 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$ (memorize this; do not use 8.314).
- **Ratio of specific heats:** $\gamma = 1.4$.
- **Lapse rate in troposphere:** $L = 0.0065 \, \text{K/m} = 6.5 \, \text{K/km}$.
- **Two key laws:** Hydrostatic equation $dP = -\rho g\, dh$ and ideal gas law $P = \rho R T$.
- **Tropospheric pressure formula:** $P = P_0 (T/T_0)^{5.2559}$ — exponent $g/(RL)$.
- **Tropospheric density formula:** $\rho = \rho_0 (T/T_0)^{4.2559}$ — exponent $g/(RL) - 1$.
- **Isothermal (11–20 km) pressure formula:** $P = P_1 \exp[-g(h - h_1)/(R T_1)]$ with $T_1 = 216.65 \, \text{K}$.
- **Geopotential altitude:** $H = r_0 h / (r_0 + h)$, where $r_0 = 6\,356\,766 \, \text{m}$.
- **Speed of sound:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — must decrease with altitude in the troposphere.
- **Layers to remember:** Troposphere (0–11 km, $L = -6.5$), Lower Stratosphere (11–20 km, isothermal), Upper Stratosphere (20–32 km, $L = +1.0$).
- **Atmospheric mass concentration:** ~75 % of total atmospheric mass lies in the troposphere.
- **Real-world use:** Altimeter calibration, Mach number, dynamic pressure, density altitude, ISA deviation tables.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** Wrong exponent, wrong gas constant, sign error in lapse rate, missing units, mixing geometric and geopotential altitudes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Altimeters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Altimeters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Altimeter (Definition as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> An **altimeter** is a flight-critical onboard aerospace instrument designed to measure the **vertical distance of an aircraft above a chosen reference datum** (most commonly **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** or, in low-altitude operations, **Ground Level (GL)**). The measurement is derived indirectly by exploiting the **monotonic decrease of atmospheric pressure with altitude**, governed by the hydrostatic equation of the atmosphere.

The altimeter is one of the **three primary flight instruments** of an aircraft (alongside the **Airspeed Indicator (ASI)** and the **Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI)**), collectively referred to as the "**T-panel**" in the classical "**six-pack**" cockpit instrument cluster.

## 1.2 Classification of Altimeters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO2 – Aerospace Sensors & Measurement Systems):**
> Students must demonstrate competency in classifying, operating principles, error analysis, and limitations of the following altimeter families.

| S.No. | Altimeter Type | Physical Principle | Typical Use Altitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Pressure Altimeter (Aneroid)** | Barometric pressure differential vs. **ISA standard** | 0 – 50,000 ft |
| 2 | **Servo Altimeter** | Electromechanical servo-driven pressure capsule | 0 – 100,000 ft |
| 3 | **Encoding Altimeter** | Pressure altimeter + optical/electrical shaft encoder | Transponder interface |
| 4 | **Radio Altimeter (Radar Altimeter)** | FMCW/Pulse radar time-of-flight to terrain | 0 – 2,500 ft (AGL) |
| 5 | **GPS Altimeter** | Geometric trilateration using satellite ranging | En-route |
| 6 | **Cabin Altimeter** | Differential pressure across fuselage skin | Pressurised aircraft |
| 7 | **Density Altimeter** | Computed from pressure altitude + OAT | Performance charts |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Water" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy for Quick Learning:**
> Imagine you are **scuba diving in the ocean**. As you descend, you feel increasing pressure on your eardrums because of the weight of water above you. The deeper you go, the higher the pressure. Now imagine the **atmosphere as a giant invisible ocean of air** with you swimming upward. As you climb higher in the atmosphere, there is **less air above you pressing down**, so the **pressure decreases predictably**. The altimeter is essentially an **underwater wrist-watch equivalent for the air ocean** — it measures how much air is "above" the aircraft by sensing this pressure drop and converting it to a height reading.

The standard sea-level pressure is **1013.25 hPa (hectopascals)** or equivalently **29.92 inHg (inches of mercury)**. This corresponds to a height of **0 ft** in the standard atmosphere.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Pressure Altimeter

The pressure altimeter is, at its core, a **highly sensitive aneroid barometer** that has been re-calibrated in units of **feet (or metres)** instead of pressure units. The operational chain is:

1. **Static Pressure Sensing:** The aircraft's **static port (flush-mounted pitot-static probe)** samples the undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure ($P$).
2. **Sealed Reference Aneroid Capsule:** Inside the instrument, a stack of sealed, evacuated **aneroid capsules** are pre-set to the standard reference pressure ($P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa).
3. **Differential Mechanical Expansion:** As the aircraft climbs, $P < P_0$. The aneroid stack expands proportionally. The expansion is amplified through a **mechanical gear train** and displayed on the **drum-pointer indicator**.
4. **Altimeter Setting (Kollsman Window):** The pilot dials in a local barometric pressure (**QNH or QFE**) to make the instrument read **true MSL altitude** at that location.

## 2.2 The Four Pilots' Altitudes (KTU Examiner Favourite!)

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Yield Topic:** Distinguishing the *four altitudes* is a recurring 7-mark sub-part question in KTU ESE papers.

| Altitude | Definition | Formula / Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Indicated Altitude ($H_I$)** | Reading directly off the altimeter face | $H_I = f(P_{\text{static}})$ via Kollsman setting |
| **Pressure Altitude ($H_P$)** | Altitude above the **standard datum (1013.25 hPa)** | $H_P = f(P)$ only — no temperature correction |
| **Density Altitude ($H_D$)** | Pressure altitude corrected for **non-standard temperature** | $H_D = H_P + (T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}}) \times 120 \text{ ft/°C}$ (approx.) |
| **True Altitude ($H_T$)** | Actual vertical distance above MSL | Computed from local $P, T$ |

## 2.3 The Hypsometry Equation (Fundamental Physics)

The relation between pressure and altitude in the **troposphere (0 – 36,089 ft / 11 km)** is derived from the hydrostatic equation combined with the **ideal gas law**, yielding:

$$
P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g \cdot M}{R^* \cdot L}}
$$

Solving for height $h$:

$$
h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{R^* \cdot L}{g \cdot M}}\right]
$$

> **Notation & Standard Values (KTU Board Prescribed):**
> - $P_0$ = **1013.25 hPa** (standard sea-level pressure)
> - $T_0$ = **288.15 K** (standard sea-level temperature = 15 °C)
> - $L$ = **0.0065 K/m** (tropospheric lapse rate)
> - $g$ = **9.80665 m/s²** (standard gravity)
> - $M$ = **0.0289644 kg/mol** (molar mass of dry air)
> - $R^*$ = **8.31447 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Domain | Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}$ | Troposphere | Pressure from height |
| 2 | $h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026}\right]$ | Troposphere | Height from pressure |
| 3 | $P = P_0 \cdot e^{-h/H_s}$, $\;H_s = 8434.5$ m | Isothermal approx. | Stratosphere base |
| 4 | $H_P = \frac{T_0}{L}\left[1-\left(\frac{P}{1013.25}\right)^{0.19026}\right]$ | Any operation | Pressure altitude |
| 5 | $H_T \approx H_P + H_P \cdot \frac{T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}}}{T_0}$ | Cold weather | True altitude correction |
| 6 | $\Delta H_{\text{temp}} = H_P \cdot \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ISA}}}{T_0}$ | Error analysis | Altimeter temp error |
| 7 | $H_D = H_P + 120 \cdot (T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}})$ | EASY form | Density altitude (ft) |
| 8 | $P_{V} = \rho \cdot R^*_{\text{air}} \cdot T$ | Gas law | Air density relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** In **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE**, full credit is awarded only when the **units are explicitly stated** and **standard atmospheric constants** are cited in the answer sheet. Examiners will not award marks for bare numerical answers.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Aerospace

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Vertical separation of aircraft (minimum **1000 ft** above FL290, **500 ft** below).
- **Terrain Avoidance:** GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) uses radio altimeter data.
- **Aircraft Performance:** Density altitude directly determines **takeoff roll**, **climb rate**, and **engine power output**.
- **Autopilot Coupling:** Altitude hold and altitude select modes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: The Hypsometry Equation (From First Principles)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Skill (CO2 Apply):** The student is expected to derive the pressure-altitude relation starting from the hydrostatic equation. The derivation below is the *complete, board-grade* version.

### Step 1: Hydrostatic Equilibrium of the Atmosphere

Consider a thin vertical slice of air of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dh$ at altitude $h$. The pressure difference $dP$ between the top and bottom of the slice is due to the **weight of the air column** above:

$$
dP \cdot A = -\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot dh
$$

Dividing through by $A$:

$$
dP = -\rho \cdot g \cdot dh
$$

**Textual Logic:** The negative sign indicates that pressure *decreases* with increasing altitude (as we intuitively know — higher up, less air above pushing down).

### Step 2: Apply the Ideal Gas Law

For dry air behaving as an ideal gas, the density $\rho$ is given by:

$$
\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{R^* \cdot T}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The density is proportional to pressure and inversely proportional to absolute temperature. This is the **ideal gas law** in molar form, where $M$ is the molar mass of air.

### Step 3: Substitute into the Hydrostatic Equation

$$
dP = -\frac{P \cdot M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot T} \, dh
$$

Separating variables (pressure on the left, height on the right):

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot T} \, dh
$$

### Step 4: Define the Tropospheric Temperature Profile

In the **troposphere (0 – 36,089 ft)**, temperature decreases *linearly* with altitude according to the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** lapse rate $L$:

$$
T = T_0 - L \cdot h
$$

**Textual Logic:** At sea level, $T = T_0 = 288.15$ K. As $h$ increases by 1 metre, $T$ drops by $L = 0.0065$ K.

### Step 5: Substitute the Temperature Profile

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot (T_0 - L \cdot h)} \, dh
$$

### Step 6: Integrate Both Sides

Integrating from sea level $(P_0, 0)$ to altitude $(P, h)$:

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{M \cdot g}{R^*} \int_{0}^{h} \frac{dh}{T_0 - L \cdot h}
$$

The left-hand side is a simple natural logarithm:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{M \cdot g}{R^*} \left[ -\frac{1}{L} \ln(T_0 - L \cdot h) \Big|_0^h \right]
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot L} \left[\ln(T_0 - L \cdot h) - \ln(T_0)\right]
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot L} \ln\left(\frac{T_0 - L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)
$$

### Step 7: Exponentiate to Eliminate the Logarithm

$$
\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{T_0 - L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot L}}
$$

### Step 8: Final Form — The Hypsometry Equation

$$
P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot L}}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation of the Exponent:**

$$
\frac{M \cdot g}{R^* \cdot L} = \frac{(0.0289644)(9.80665)}{(8.31447)(0.0065)} = \frac{0.28404}{0.054044} \approx 5.2559
$$

Thus the **standard KTU-board form** is:

$$
P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}
$$

### Step 9: Inversion for Altitude

Raise both sides to the power $\frac{R^* \cdot L}{M \cdot g} = 0.19026$:

$$
\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026} = 1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026}\right]
$$

**Textual Logic:** This is the *working equation* a pressure altimeter uses — it converts the sensed static pressure $P$ into an altitude reading, assuming standard temperature profile.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Altimeter Temperature Error (Cold-Weather Correction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hot Question:** "Explain the altimeter error when the ambient temperature is *colder* than ISA."

### Step 1: Recognize the Physical Cause

The altimeter *assumes* the air column below is at the **ISA standard temperature profile**. If the actual air is **colder** than standard, it is also **denser** (per the ideal gas law). For the same pressure value, the aircraft is **lower** in a colder atmosphere than in a standard one. The altimeter will therefore **over-read** (indicate higher than actual) in cold weather.

### Step 2: Differential Height Between Two Atmospheres

Consider two atmospheres reaching the same pressure $P$ at the top:
- **Standard atmosphere:** pressure falls from $P_0$ to $P$ over height $h_{\text{std}}$.
- **Cold (non-standard) atmosphere:** pressure falls from $P_0$ to $P$ over a *smaller* height $h_{\text{act}}$.

The altimeter always displays $h_{\text{std}}$, but the aircraft is actually at $h_{\text{act}}$.

### Step 3: Quantify the Error Using Scale Height Analysis

For an isothermal layer, the fractional change in pressure is:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{dh}{H_s}, \quad H_s = \frac{R^* T}{M g}
$$

For a layer of finite thickness $\Delta h$, the equivalent pressure ratio is:

$$
\frac{P_0}{P} = e^{\Delta h / H_s}
$$

Taking logarithms and applying the small-perturbation assumption between standard ($T_0$) and actual ($T_{\text{act}}$) temperatures:

$$
\Delta h_{\text{error}} \approx h \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_0}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The fractional error in height equals the fractional error in absolute temperature, scaled by the altitude. The sign is *positive* if actual is colder than ISA (altimeter reads too high).

### Step 4: Final Working Formula

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta H_{\text{altimeter}} = h \cdot \frac{T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}}}{T_0}\;}
$$

> **Sign Convention:** Positive $\Delta H$ means the **altimeter over-reads** (i.e., actual altitude is *less* than indicated). Negative means it under-reads.

### Step 5: Worked Example for Intuition

Suppose an aircraft is flying at a **pressure altitude** of $h = 10{,}000$ ft, and the **OAT (Outside Air Temperature)** is $-30\;°\text{C}$ instead of the standard $-5\;°\text{C}$ for that altitude.

The temperature deficit is $\Delta T = -5 - (-30) = 25$ K. Substituting:

$$
\Delta H = 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{25}{288.15} \approx 868 \text{ ft}
$$

**Conclusion:** The altimeter reads approximately **868 ft too high**. The aircraft's *true* altitude is roughly **9,132 ft**. This is critical in **mountainous terrain operations**, where an over-reading altimeter can cause Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Sub-Part Coverage

| Sub-Question Type | Primary Concept Tested | Marks Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Define & classify altimeters | Block I (Basics) | 3 / 7 |
| Derive hypsometry equation | Block II (Theory) | 7 / 7 |
| Compute pressure altitude from $P$ | Block III (Numericals) | 3 / 7 |
| Altimeter temperature error correction | Block II + III | 4 / 7 |
| Compare pressure / radio / GPS altimeter | Block IV (Comparison) | 7 / 7 |
| Block diagram of servo altimeter | Block V (Schematic) | 7 / 7 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pressure Altimeter (Mechanical Aneroid Type)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below traces the **signal path** from ambient air to the cockpit display, showing the major functional blocks. This is the most commonly asked block-diagram question in KTU ESE Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Static Port on Fuselage] --> B1[Static Line Manifold]
    B1 --> C1[Aneroid Capsule Stack]
    C1 --> D1[Mechanical Linkage and Lever Amplifier]
    D1 --> E1[Pointer and Drum Indicator]
    F1[Kollsman Barometric Setting Knob] --> D1
    G1[Sealed Reference Aneroid] --> C1
    H1[Aircraft Static Pressure Input] --> A1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Static Port** samples ambient air.
> - **Aneroid Capsule Stack** expands as ambient pressure drops (climb).
> - **Mechanical Linkage** amplifies the small expansion (microns) into a usable pointer rotation.
> - **Kollsman Knob** lets the pilot input local QNH, which mechanically biases the linkage.
> - **Pointer & Drum** display the altitude in feet (analog) or via synchro to an EFIS display (digital).

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Radio (Radar) Altimeter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Radio altimeters use **FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave)** or **pulse radar** to measure **absolute height above terrain (AGL)** — not above MSL. They are critical for **autoland** and **GPWS** systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Transmitter FMCW Modulator] --> B2[Antenna Diplexer]
    B2 --> C2[Transmit Antenna on Belly]
    C2 --> D2[Ground Reflection]
    D2 --> E2[Receive Antenna on Belly]
    E2 --> F2[Receiver Mixer]
    B2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2[Beat Frequency Detector]
    G2 --> H2[Time to Altitude Converter]
    H2 --> I2[Cockpit Indicator and GPWS Coupling]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **FMCW Modulator** sweeps a carrier frequency (typically 4.3 GHz, C-band).
> - The **Antenna Diplexer** allows a single antenna pair to transmit and receive.
> - **Beat Frequency** between transmitted and reflected signals is proportional to **round-trip time delay** $\Delta t$, and hence to altitude $h = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Pressure vs Radio vs GPS Altimeter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A3[Pressure Altimeter]
        A3a[Static Port] --> A3b[Aneroid Capsule]
        A3b --> A3c[Mechanical Indicator]
    end
    subgraph B3[Radio Altimeter]
        B3a[FMCW Tx Rx] --> B3b[Beat Detector]
        B3b --> B3c[AGL Indicator]
    end
    subgraph C3[GPS Altimeter]
        C3a[Antenna Array] --> C3b[Satellite Ranging]
        C3b --> C3c[Geometric Height MSL]
    end
    A3c --> D3[Flight Display EFIS]
    B3c --> D3
    C3c --> D3
```

---

## 4.4 ISA Atmospheric Layer Model (Altimeter Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Troposphere 0 to 11 km Lapse Rate 0.0065 K per m] --> L2[Tropopause Isothermal 11 to 20 km]
    L2 --> L3[Stratosphere Inversion 20 to 32 km]
    L3 --> L4[Stratopause 32 to 47 km]
    L4 --> L5[Mesosphere Lapse 47 to 51 km]
    L5 --> L6[Mesopause 51 to 71 km]
    L6 --> L7[Thermosphere Above 71 km]
```

> **KTU Board Note:** The pressure altimeter uses the **Tropospheric + Tropopause** profile (up to **32,000 ft** standard) for its calibration. Outside this band, additional temperature segments are used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"Differentiate between pressure altitude and density altitude. Why is density altitude critical for aircraft takeoff performance?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Pressure Altitude (1 mark):** The altitude indicated when the altimeter is set to the **standard datum pressure of 1013.25 hPa**. It depends *only* on static pressure, not temperature.
- **Density Altitude (1 mark):** Pressure altitude corrected for **non-standard temperature**. It represents the altitude at which the aircraft *thinks* it is operating in terms of **air density**, which is the true performance parameter.
- **Criticality for Takeoff (1 mark):** Higher density altitude → thinner air → reduced **engine power output**, reduced **lift**, and increased **takeoff roll**. On a hot day at high-altitude airports (e.g., **La Paz, Bolivia at 13,325 ft**), density altitude can exceed 18,000 ft, drastically degrading performance.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"Define QNH and QFE in the context of pressure altimeter settings."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **QNH (1.5 marks):** The **local barometric pressure setting** that causes the altimeter to read **elevation above Mean Sea Level (MSL)** when the aircraft is on the ground at that location. Used for **transition altitude and below**.
- **QFE (1.5 marks):** The local barometric pressure setting that causes the altimeter to read **zero** when the aircraft is on the ground. Used by **military operations** and some general aviation airports for **low-level circuit flying**, since it gives **height above the runway directly**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are **two full alternative choices** to be offered in a single question paper slot.

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **aneroid-type pressure altimeter**. Discuss the role of the Kollsman window.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - **Static Port:** Flush-mounted on the aircraft fuselage, positioned to sample undisturbed free-stream static pressure.
   - **Aneroid Capsules:** A stack of 2–4 sealed, partially evacuated metal capsules (typically beryllium copper or nickel alloy) which expand/contract with pressure changes.
   - **Mechanical Linkage & Gears:** A lever-and-pinion arrangement that amplifies the small (order of microns) capsule expansion into a measurable pointer rotation.
   - **Kollsman Window:** A small rotary knob with a digital display showing the barometric pressure setting (in hPa or inHg) the pilot has dialled in.
   - **Pointer and Drum Display:** A multi-pointer face (100s, 1000s, 10,000s of feet) and a vertical drum counter for precise reading.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - Ambient static pressure enters via the static port.
   - The aneroid stack expands when the aircraft climbs ($P_{\text{ambient}} < P_{\text{capsule internal}}$).
   - The expansion is mechanically amplified and rotates the pointers/drum.
   - The Kollsman setting biases the mechanical zero to match local conditions, ensuring the reading corresponds to MSL altitude.

3. **Role of Kollsman Window (1 mark):**
   - Allows the pilot to input the **local QNH** so the altimeter indicates **MSL altitude** rather than **pressure altitude**.
   - Without the Kollsman setting, the altimeter would only display pressure altitude (relative to 1013.25 hPa).

---

**(b)** *An aircraft is flying at a pressure altitude of **12,000 ft**. The Outside Air Temperature (OAT) is **−35 °C** instead of the standard **−21 °C** for that altitude. Calculate the **altimeter temperature error** and the **true altitude** of the aircraft.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Pressure Altitude: $H_P = 12{,}000$ ft
- OAT (actual): $T_{\text{ACT}} = -35\;°\text{C} = 238.15$ K
- Standard temperature at 12,000 ft: $T_{\text{ISA}} = 15 - 2 \times 12 = -9\;°\text{C}$ (using the standard lapse rate of **2 °C per 1000 ft**)
- $T_{\text{ISA}} = -9 + 273.15 = 264.15$ K
- $T_0 = 288.15$ K

**Note:** In aviation practice, the **2 °C/1000 ft** lapse rate is used. The KTU examiner expects this aviation-specific lapse rate unless the question specifies otherwise.

**Step 1: Temperature Deficit Calculation (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}} = (-9) - (-35) = 26\;°\text{C} = 26 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Altimeter Error Formula (3 marks)**

$$
\Delta H = H_P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_0} = 12{,}000 \times \frac{26}{288.15}
$$

$$
\Delta H = 12{,}000 \times 0.09023 \approx 1082.8 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 3: True Altitude (2 marks)**

$$
H_T = H_P - \Delta H = 12{,}000 - 1082.8 \approx 10{,}917 \text{ ft}
$$

**Conclusion:** The altimeter reads **12,000 ft**, but the aircraft is actually flying at approximately **10,917 ft** — about **1,083 ft lower** than indicated. The altimeter has **over-read** due to the **cold-weather error**.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the principle of operation of a **Radio (Radar) Altimeter**. Compare its operating range, accuracy, and application with that of a **pressure altimeter**.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Principle of Radio Altimeter (3 marks):**
   - A **FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave)** signal is transmitted downward from a belly-mounted antenna.
   - The signal reflects off the terrain and is received back.
   - The **beat frequency** $f_b$ between transmitted and received signals is proportional to the **round-trip time delay** $\Delta t$.
   - Altitude: $h = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, where $c$ is the speed of light.
   - Typical operating band: **4.3 GHz (C-band)** allocated for radio altimeters.

2. **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pressure Altimeter | Radio Altimeter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference | MSL (sea level) | Ground / terrain (AGL) |
| Range | 0 to 50,000 ft | 0 to 2,500 ft AGL |
| Accuracy | ±50–100 ft (high alt) | ±0.5 ft (low alt) |
| Principle | Pressure | Time-of-flight radar |
| Application | En-route, cruise | Approach, autoland, GPWS |

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Both altimeters are complementary — pressure altimeter for en-route navigation, radio altimeter for **precision low-altitude operations**.

---

**(b)** *Derive the **hypsometry equation** relating pressure and altitude in the **troposphere**, starting from the hydrostatic equation. State all standard atmospheric constants used.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

> **Note:** This is a full re-derivation. Students are expected to reproduce the steps shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps expected by the KTU examiner are:

1. **Hydrostatic Equation** (1 mark):
   $$dP = -\rho g \, dh$$
2. **Ideal Gas Law** (1 mark):
   $$\rho = \frac{PM}{R^*T}$$
3. **Substitution and Separation** (1 mark):
   $$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{R^*T}\,dh$$
4. **ISA Temperature Profile** (1 mark):
   $$T = T_0 - Lh$$
5. **Integration and Final Form** (2 marks):
   $$P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}$$
6. **Standard Constants List** (1 mark): $P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa, $T_0 = 288.15$ K, $L = 0.0065$ K/m, $g = 9.80665$ m/s², $M = 0.0289644$ kg/mol, $R^* = 8.31447$ J/(mol·K).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** A formula without units is treated as incomplete. **Always append hPa, m, K, ft** as appropriate.
> 2. **Mixing lapse rates:** Using the **thermodynamic lapse rate 0.0065 K/m** in some places and the **aviation lapse rate 2 °C/1000 ft** in others — examiners will mark only the *consistent* derivation. State explicitly which one you are using.
> 3. **Missing exponent calculation:** Showing the derivation with the symbol $gM/(R^*L)$ but **not evaluating it to 5.2559** loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing QNH/QFE/QNE:** These are board favourites. The third one (QNE) — the standard 1013.25 hPa setting — is often missed by students.
> 5. **Radio vs Pressure:** Stating that a radio altimeter "measures altitude above sea level" — this is **wrong**; it measures AGL. This single error can lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist — Altimeters (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Altimeter = instrument to measure vertical distance above a reference (MSL or AGL).
- **Primary Type:** Pressure altimeter, based on aneroid capsule barometric principle.
- **Key Equation:** $P = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}$ — the **hypsometry equation**.
- **Inverted Form:** $h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026}\right]$.
- **Four Altitudes:** Indicated, Pressure, Density, True — know the **differences cold**.
- **QNH:** Local setting → altimeter reads MSL.
- **QFE:** Local setting → altimeter reads zero on runway.
- **QNE:** 1013.25 hPa → altimeter reads pressure altitude (used above transition altitude).
- **Temperature Error:** $\Delta H = h \cdot (T_{\text{ISA}} - T_{\text{ACT}}) / T_0$.
- **Cold-Weather Trap:** Altimeter **over-reads** in cold air — true altitude is *lower* than indicated.
- **Radio Altimeter:** FMCW, 4.3 GHz, 0–2,500 ft AGL, ±0.5 ft accuracy.
- **Block Diagram Must-Knows:** Static port → aneroid stack → mechanical amplifier → Kollsman bias → pointer.
- **Standard Constants:** Memorise $P_0, T_0, L, g, M, R^*$.
- **KTU Weighting:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) + 1 Part-B (14 marks) per module — prepare both derivations and numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aneroid and Radio Altimeters, Aircraft Compass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aneroid and Radio Altimeters, Aircraft Compass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Altimetry – The Need for Altitude Measurement

In aviation, **altitude** is the vertical distance of an aircraft above a chosen reference datum. Depending on the datum, altitude is classified as:

| Altitude Type | Reference Datum | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Indicated Altitude** | Sea level (via barometric pressure) | Cruising, ATC clearance |
| **Absolute Altitude** | Terrain directly below the aircraft | Landing, terrain avoidance |
| **True Altitude** | Mean sea level (actual) | Navigation charts |
| **Pressure Altitude** | Standard pressure datum (**1013.25 hPa**) | Flight levels (FL) |
| **Density Altitude** | Adjusted for non-standard air density | Aircraft performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** An altimeter is *not* a direct altitude measuring device — it is fundamentally a **highly refined barometer** (pressure transducer) that infers altitude from the atmospheric pressure lapse rate.

---

## 1.2 Aneroid Altimeter

### Formal Definition
The **Aneroid Altimeter** is a sensitive pressure-measuring instrument that uses one or more **evacuated metallic capsules (aneroid cells)** to detect local atmospheric static pressure and convert it mechanically into an indication of altitude above a reference plane, based on the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** pressure–height relationship.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Collapsing Can" Intuition
Imagine a sealed, air-removed metal can (aneroid capsule). As you climb a mountain, the air outside becomes "lighter" (lower pressure). The vacuum inside the can is then pushed inward by the higher outside pressure, causing the can to **deflect slightly inward**. Connect this tiny mechanical deflection to a gear train and a needle — and you have an altimeter! The needle rotates *more* the higher you climb, because the can collapses *more* as outside pressure drops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Atmospheric pressure decreases monotonically with altitude (approximately **1 hPa per 8.4 m** near sea level). The aneroid altimeter transduces this pressure into a calibrated altitude reading.

**Key Specifications (KTU Standard):**
- **Sea-level standard pressure:** $P_0 = 1013.25\ \text{hPa}$
- **Sea-level standard temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15\ \text{K}\ (15^\circ\text{C})$
- **Pressure lapse rate (troposphere):** $\beta = 0.0065\ \text{K/m}$
- **Operating range:** $-1000\ \text{ft}$ to **$50{,}000\ \text{ft}$** (typical transport altimeter)

---

## 1.3 Radio Altimeter (Radar Altimeter)

### Formal Definition
A **Radio Altimeter** is an airborne electronic instrument that determines the **absolute height of the aircraft above the terrain directly beneath it** by transmitting a radio-frequency signal toward the ground and measuring the **time delay** between transmission and reception of the reflected echo.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Shouting into a Well"
Stand at the top of a well and shout "Hello!". The time it takes for the echo to return tells you how deep the well is — **deeper well = longer echo time**. A radio altimeter does the *exact same thing* with electromagnetic waves instead of sound, but the waves travel at the speed of light ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$), so the time intervals are extremely short (nanoseconds for low altitudes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** $h = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$, where $t$ is the round-trip time of the radio signal. The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ accounts for the signal travelling *down* and *back up*.

**Operational Parameters:**
- **Carrier frequency band:** **$4.2$ to $4.4\ \text{GHz}$** (C-band, internationally allocated)
- **Maximum useful altitude:** Typically $0$ to **$2{,}500\ \text{ft}$** AGL (Above Ground Level)
- **Accuracy:** $\pm 2\ \text{ft}$ or $\pm 2\%$ of altitude, whichever is greater

---

## 1.4 Aircraft Compass Systems

### Formal Definition
An **Aircraft Compass** is a navigation instrument that indicates the aircraft's **heading** (the horizontal direction in which the aircraft is pointing, measured clockwise from Magnetic North). Modern aircraft use either a **Direct-Reading Magnetic Compass** or a **Remote-Indicating Gyro/Magnetic Compass System**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Leaf in a Stream"
A magnetic compass needle behaves like a small leaf floating on water: it aligns itself with the local flow of the Earth's magnetic field. The pilot simply reads which way the leaf points to know the direction of magnetic north relative to the aircraft. However, the aircraft itself is moving (like the stream), so we need to *subtract* the aircraft's own heading change to get the true compass reading — analogous to watching a leaf from a moving boat.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Three cardinal errors affect magnetic compasses — **Variation**, **Deviation**, and **Dip**. Mastery of these three is mandatory for Module 2.

**Key Earth Magnetic Field Values:**
- **Horizontal field component at equator:** $\approx 24\ \mu\text{T}$
- **Vertical field component at poles:** $\approx 60\ \mu\text{T}$
- **Magnetic declination (Variation):** Angle between True North and Magnetic North
- **Dip angle:** Angle the field lines make with the horizontal (0° at equator, 90° at poles)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aneroid Altimeter — Working Principle

### Operational Breakdown

1. **Static Pressure Sensing:** The aircraft's **static port** (flush-mounted on the fuselage) supplies ambient atmospheric pressure to the altimeter's sealed casing via a pitot-static system.
2. **Aneroid Capsule Stack:** A stack of 2–4 evacuated corrugated metal discs (typically **beryllium copper** or **Ni-Span C**) expand/contract as ambient pressure changes.
3. **Mechanical Linkage:** The minute capsule motion ($\sim 0.01\ \text{mm}$) is amplified by a **lever-and-gear train** to rotate the indicator needle.
4. **Calibration Disc (Kollsman Window):** A rotating sub-scale allows the pilot to set the local barometric pressure (QNH or QFE), shifting the entire altitude reading accordingly.
5. **Display:** The needle (or drum) displays altitude in **feet** on a multi-revolution dial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA Pressure–Height Relationship (Troposphere, $h \le 11{,}000\ \text{m}$):**
> $$P = P_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{\beta h}{T_0} \right)^{\dfrac{g}{\beta R}}$$
> where $R = 287\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ is the specific gas constant for dry air.

### Sources of Error in Aneroid Altimeters

| Error Type | Cause | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Instrument Error** | Mechanical hysteresis, friction | $\pm 50\ \text{ft}$ (compensated by manufacturer) |
| **Position Error (Installation)** | Airflow disturbance near static port | $\pm 50$ to $\pm 150\ \text{ft}$ |
| **Temperature Error** | Aneroid spring constant varies with temperature | $\sim 4\ \text{ft}$ per °C deviation from standard |
| **Barometric Setting Error** | Pilot enters wrong QNH | **Major — can be hundreds of feet** |

---

## 2.2 Radio Altimeter — Working Principle

### Two Principal Architectures

#### A. Pulsed Radio Altimeter
- Transmits short RF pulses (typically $20$–$100\ \text{ns}$ wide)
- Measures the round-trip time $t$ directly
- Altitude: $h = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$
- Simple, robust, but limited minimum altitude due to receiver recovery time

#### B. FM-CW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) Altimeter
- Transmitter frequency sweeps linearly (e.g., $100\ \text{MHz}$ deviation at $100\ \text{Hz}$ rate)
- The reflected signal has a frequency offset proportional to the round-trip delay
- A mixer produces a **beat frequency** $f_b$ proportional to altitude
- **Superior for low altitudes** (down to $0\ \text{ft}$), used in modern transport aircraft

### FM-CW Beat Frequency Derivation

The transmitted frequency varies linearly with time:
$$f_T(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot t$$

The received signal is delayed by $\tau = \dfrac{2h}{c}$:
$$f_R(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot (t - \tau)$$

The instantaneous frequency difference (beat frequency) is:
$$f_b = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot \tau = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot \dfrac{2h}{c}$$

Solving for altitude:
$$\boxed{h = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b \cdot T_m}{2 \cdot \Delta f}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The radio altimeter is the **primary sensor for GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System)** and **autoland** systems in commercial aircraft. Without it, Category III ILS landings are impossible.

### Limitations of Radio Altimeters

- **Sea/Land Reflection:** Over calm water, the low dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) and specular reflection can cause **signal loss** at shallow grazing angles.
- **Pitch & Roll Limitations:** Useful only for $\pm 30^\circ$ pitch and $\pm 10^\circ$ roll (beamwidth constraints).
- **Beam Footprint:** At $2{,}000\ \text{ft}$ AGL, the footprint is $\sim 60\ \text{ft}$ diameter — terrain may be non-uniform within the beam.

---

## 2.3 Aircraft Compass — Working Principles

### 2.3.1 Direct-Reading Magnetic Compass (Whisker Type)

#### Construction
- A pair of **magnetized needles** is mounted on a **pivot** inside a sealed bowl filled with **kerosene** (damping fluid).
- The bowl is attached to a **compass card** (graduated 0°–360° in 5° increments).
- A **vertical pivot** (whisker) allows the bowl to tilt freely in the vertical plane.
- A **lubber line** is fixed to the case at the aircraft's forward axis.

#### Reading Interpretation
The number under the lubber line is the **magnetic heading** of the aircraft.

#### Sources of Error

> [!WARNING]
> **The Three Cardinal Compass Errors — Frequently Examined!**

**1. Variation (Magnetic Declination):**
- The angle between **True North** and **Magnetic North** at the aircraft's location.
- Caused by non-coincidence of geographic and geomagnetic poles.
- East variation: Magnetic North is east of True North.
- West variation: Magnetic North is west of True North.
- Memory aid: **"Isogonic lines"** on aeronautical charts connect points of equal variation.

**2. Deviation:**
- The angle between **Magnetic North** and **Compass North** caused by **local magnetic disturbances** in the aircraft (steel parts, electrical wiring, avionics).
- Compensated by **adjustable magnets** (N-S and E-W pairs) placed near the compass.
- A **deviation card** lists residual deviation at $30^\circ$ heading intervals.

**3. Dip (Magnetic Inclination):**
- The vertical component of the Earth's field causes the compass needle to dip downward in the Northern Hemisphere.
- The needle is weighted to compensate for dip in the region of certification.
- During **acceleration on Easterly/Westerly headings** or **turns through North/South**, dip causes **northerly turning error** and **acceleration error**.

### Northerly Turning Error (Oscillation Error)

| Heading | Acceleration/Deceleration Effect | Turn Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **North** (0°) | Reads LOW | — |
| **South** (180°) | Reads HIGH | — |
| **East** (90°) | — | Reads HIGH in turn |
| **West** (270°) | — | Reads LOW in turn |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "ANDS" Rule:** **A**cceleration on **N**ortherly/Southerly headings causes **D**eviation towards **S**outh. **UNOS Rule:** **U**ndershoot **N**orth, **O**vershoot **S**outh when turning through these headings.

### 2.3.2 Remote-Indicating Compass System (Flux-Gate / Gyrocompass)

#### Flux Valve (Detector Unit)
- Three-spoke laminated core with three windings
- **Primary (excitation) winding** energized at $400\ \text{Hz}$
- The Earth's horizontal field component induces voltages in the two **secondary windings** oriented at $90^\circ$ to each other
- The amplitude ratio of these voltages gives the **heading angle**
- Signal is amplified and transmitted to a **repeater indicator** in the cockpit

#### Gyrocompass (Heading Reference)
- A **directional gyroscope** (high-speed rotor with two gimbals) maintains a fixed heading in space
- A **latitude nut** on the inner gimbal applies a controllable precession torque to counteract Earth rotation
- **Settling time:** $\sim 3$–$5$ minutes after alignment
- Drift rate: $\sim 3^\circ$ per hour (must be re-checked every 15 min in cruise)
- The gyroscope can be slaved to the flux valve for automatic correction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The flux valve is a **saturable inductor** that exploits the **non-linearity of magnetic materials** (B-H curve) to detect weak DC fields using AC excitation — a brilliant example of analogue signal processing.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = P_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{\beta h}{T_0} \right)^{\tfrac{g}{\beta R}}$ | ISA pressure–altitude (troposphere) | $P$ in Pa, $h$ in m |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Radio altimeter (pulsed) | $h$ in m, $t$ in s |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b \cdot T_m}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | Radio altimeter (FM-CW) | $h$ in m, $f_b$ in Hz |
| 4 | $\text{True Heading} = \text{Magnetic Heading} + \text{East Variation}$ | Compass correction | degrees |
| 5 | $\text{Magnetic Heading} = \text{Compass Heading} + \text{East Deviation}$ | Compass correction | degrees |
| 6 | $\tau_d = \dfrac{2h}{c}$ | Round-trip time of radio signal | seconds |
| 7 | $\dot{P} \approx -12\ \text{Pa/m}$ | Vertical pressure gradient near sea level | Pa/m |
| 8 | $V_{\text{sec}} = K \cdot B \cdot \omega \cdot \sin\theta$ | Flux valve secondary EMF (per phase) | V |
| 9 | $\omega_E = 15^\circ/\text{hr}$ | Earth rotation rate | deg/hr |
| 10 | $\omega_p = \omega_E \cdot \sin\phi$ | Gyrocompass precession rate (latitude-dependent) | deg/hr |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** Variations and deviations are labeled **East or West** based on whether the *reference* direction is east or west of the *true* direction. **East is positive (+)**, **West is negative (−)** — this is the **"East is least, West is best"** mnemonic (in terms of sign convention for addition/subtraction).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ISA Pressure–Altitude Equation

### Statement
Derive the relationship between atmospheric pressure $P$ and geometric altitude $h$ in the **troposphere** (0 – 11 km), assuming a constant temperature lapse rate $\beta = -\dfrac{dT}{dh}$.

### Step 1: Hydrostatic Equation
The fundamental hydrostatic equation for the atmosphere is:
$$\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\rho \cdot g$$

where $\rho$ is air density and $g$ is gravitational acceleration ($g \approx 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$).

### Step 2: Ideal Gas Law
For dry air treated as an ideal gas:
$$P = \rho \cdot R \cdot T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \rho = \dfrac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

where $R = 287\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ is the specific gas constant.

### Step 3: Substitute Density
Substituting $\rho$ into the hydrostatic equation:
$$\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\dfrac{P \cdot g}{R \cdot T}$$

### Step 4: Lapse Rate Relationship
By definition, $\beta = -\dfrac{dT}{dh}$, so:
$$T = T_0 - \beta \cdot h$$

where $T_0 = 288.15\ \text{K}$ at $h = 0$.

### Step 5: Variable Separation
The differential equation becomes:
$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{g}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dh}{T_0 - \beta h}$$

### Step 6: Integrate Both Sides
Integrating from sea level ($h = 0$, $P = P_0$) to altitude $h$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \dfrac{dP'}{P'} = -\dfrac{g}{R} \int_{0}^{h} \dfrac{dh'}{T_0 - \beta h'}$$

The right-hand side integral yields:
$$\int_{0}^{h} \dfrac{dh'}{T_0 - \beta h'} = -\dfrac{1}{\beta} \left[ \ln(T_0 - \beta h) - \ln(T_0) \right] = \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln\left( \dfrac{T_0}{T_0 - \beta h} \right)$$

### Step 7: Final Closed-Form Expression
$$\ln\left( \dfrac{P}{P_0} \right) = \dfrac{g}{\beta R} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{T_0}{T_0 - \beta h} \right)$$

Exponentiating:
$$\boxed{P = P_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{\beta h}{T_0} \right)^{\tfrac{g}{\beta R}}}$$

### Step 8: Numerical Evaluation of Exponent
With $\beta = 0.0065\ \text{K/m}$, $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$, $R = 287\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$:
$$\dfrac{g}{\beta R} = \dfrac{9.80665}{0.0065 \times 287} \approx 5.256$$

So the practical form is:
$$P(h) = 1013.25 \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.0065 \cdot h}{288.15} \right)^{5.256}\ \text{hPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation that an aneroid altimeter uses *implicitly* — the mechanical scale on its dial is calibrated directly to invert this relation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: FM-CW Radio Altimeter Altitude

### Step 1: Transmitted Signal
A voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) produces a linearly frequency-modulated carrier:
$$f_T(t) = f_0 + \dot{f} \cdot t, \quad \text{where}\ \dot{f} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m}$$

### Step 2: Received Signal
The signal travels to ground and back, arriving with a delay $\tau = \dfrac{2h}{c}$:
$$f_R(t) = f_0 + \dot{f} \cdot (t - \tau)$$

### Step 3: Mixer Output
The mixer multiplies the two signals, producing sum and difference frequencies. A low-pass filter retains only the **difference (beat) frequency**:
$$f_b = f_T(t) - f_R(t) = \dot{f} \cdot \tau = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot \dfrac{2h}{c}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Altitude
$$h = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b \cdot T_m}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
Given: $\Delta f = 100\ \text{MHz}$, $T_m = 10\ \text{ms}$, measured $f_b = 667\ \text{Hz}$:
$$h = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \times 667 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 100 \times 10^6}$$
$$h = \dfrac{2.001 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^8} = 10.005\ \text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **speed of light cancels precision errors only mildly** — radio altimeters must compensate for atmospheric refractive index variations (which change effective $c$) for high-precision autoland use.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Magnetic Compass Deviation Correction

### The Compass Error Chain (KTU High-Yield)

The compass reading passes through a chain of corrections:

$$\text{True Heading} = \text{Compass Heading} \pm \text{Deviation} \pm \text{Variation}$$

**Mnemonic (using the rhyming rule):**

> *"Can Dead Men Vote Twice — at Elections"*
> 
> **C**ompass → **D**eviation → **M**agnetic → **V**ariation → **T**rue (going **C → D → M → V → T**)

### Worked Example
An aircraft is flying with:
- **Compass Heading (CH):** 085°
- **Deviation (from card):** 2° W
- **Variation (from chart):** 5° E

**Step 1:** Apply deviation (East is positive):
$$\text{MH} = 085^\circ + (-2^\circ) = 083^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Apply variation:
$$\text{TH} = 083^\circ + 5^\circ = 088^\circ$$

**True Heading = 088°** ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Flux Valve Output Voltage

### Principle
The flux valve is a three-legged transformer with an AC-excited primary. In the absence of an external field, the two secondaries have equal and opposite induced EMFs. When an external DC field $H_E$ is applied along one axis, the core saturates asymmetrically.

### Step-by-Step

**Primary excitation:** $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$

**Phase 1 — Without external field:** Equal saturation in both legs → secondary EMFs cancel: $V_{s1} = -V_{s2}$.

**Phase 2 — With external field $H_E$ along one axis:** Asymmetric saturation occurs. The net secondary voltage is:

$$V_{\text{out}} = k \cdot H_E \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

The phase $\phi$ depends on the *direction* of $H_E$, and the amplitude is proportional to its *magnitude*. The two secondary windings (orthogonal) yield:

$$V_x = K \cdot H_E \cdot \cos\theta$$
$$V_y = K \cdot H_E \cdot \sin\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the aircraft heading relative to magnetic north.

### Heading Computation
$$\theta = \arctan\left( \dfrac{V_y}{V_x} \right)$$

This is the angle transmitted to the cockpit repeater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** The $400\ \text{Hz}$ excitation frequency is chosen to be far from the $50/60\ \text{Hz}$ power line harmonics to prevent interference from aircraft electrical systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Aneroid Altimeter | Radio Altimeter | Magnetic Compass |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | Static pressure | Time delay of RF echo | Magnetic field direction |
| **Output Type** | Pressure altitude | Absolute height AGL | Magnetic heading |
| **Range** | $-1000$ to $50{,}000$ ft | $0$ to $2500$ ft | $0^\circ$ to $360^\circ$ |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 50$ to $100$ ft | $\pm 2$ ft or $2\%$ | $\pm 2^\circ$ to $5^\circ$ |
| **Datum** | Sea level (or pressure setting) | Terrain directly below | Magnetic North |
| **Key Error** | Barometric setting | Beam footprint / sea reflection | Variation + Deviation + Dip |
| **Power Required** | None (passive) | Yes (active RF) | None (passive) |
| **Primary Use** | Cruising, ATC separation | Landing, GPWS, autoland | Heading reference |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Explain the working principle of an aneroid altimeter with a neat sketch. State any two sources of error.**

#### Model Answer (Concise)
An aneroid altimeter measures altitude by correlating it with the local atmospheric static pressure using the ISA relationship. **Working principle:**

1. **Static port** on the fuselage supplies ambient pressure to the instrument case.
2. One or more **evacuated aneroid capsules** expand as ambient pressure decreases (altitude increases).
3. The minute mechanical deflection is amplified by a **lever-and-gear train** to rotate a calibrated dial.
4. A **barometric setting knob (Kollsman window)** allows the pilot to set the local sea-level pressure (QNH/QFE), shifting the entire altitude indication.

**Two sources of error:**
- **Barometric setting error:** Incorrect QNH input causes large altitude errors (hundreds of feet).
- **Temperature error:** Aneroid elasticity varies with temperature; the bimetallic compensator partially corrects this, but residual error is $\sim 4\ \text{ft/°C}$ deviation from ISA.

(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for capsule mechanism, 1 for error sources.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is a radio altimeter? Explain its working with the FM-CW technique.**

#### Model Answer (Concise)
A **radio altimeter** is an airborne instrument that measures the **absolute height of the aircraft above the terrain directly below** by transmitting radio waves and measuring the time delay of the reflected echo.

**FM-CW technique:**
1. The transmitter frequency is **swept linearly** over a band $\Delta f$ with period $T_m$.
2. The reflected signal arrives with delay $\tau = 2h/c$.
3. Mixing the transmitted and received signals produces a **beat frequency** $f_b$.
4. Altitude is computed as: $h = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b \cdot T_m}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$.

**Advantage:** Operates accurately down to $0\ \text{ft}$ AGL — essential for **autoland** and **GPWS**.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for FM-CW principle, 1 for formula/advantage.)

---

### Question 3 [KTU University Exam — Sample]
**Distinguish between variation and deviation in a magnetic compass.**

#### Model Answer (Concise)

| Parameter | Variation | Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Angle between True North and Magnetic North | Angle between Magnetic North and Compass North |
| **Cause** | Earth's magnetic pole misalignment | Aircraft's own magnetic fields (steel, wiring) |
| **Location-dependent** | Yes (varies with geographic position) | Yes (varies with aircraft heading) |
| **Correction** | Read from aeronautical chart (isogonic lines) | Read from compass deviation card; compensated by magnets |
| **Magnitude** | 0° to 30° typical | $\pm 10°$ typical |

(3 marks.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]
**(a)** Derive the **ISA pressure-altitude equation** for the troposphere starting from the hydrostatic equation and the ideal gas law. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft cruises at **FL 350** (Flight Level 350 = 35,000 ft) with an altimeter set to **1013.25 hPa**. If the temperature at this altitude is **ISA − 10°C** (10°C colder than standard), calculate:
   (i) The pressure altitude. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The density altitude. **(5 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]
**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the working of an **FM-CW radio altimeter**. Derive the expression for altitude in terms of beat frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **flux valve** and **slaved gyrocompass** system. Explain how the slaving system corrects the gyroscope drift. **(7 marks)**

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a): Derivation of ISA Pressure–Altitude Equation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Troposphere ($0 \le h \le 11\ \text{km}$)
2. Constant temperature lapse rate $\beta = 0.0065\ \text{K/m}$
3. Dry air behaves as an ideal gas
4. $g$ is constant = $9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$
5. Hydrostatic equilibrium: $dP = -\rho g\ dh$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Hydrostatic equation
$$\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\rho g$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Ideal gas law substitution: $\rho = P/(RT)$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Variable separation:
$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{g}{R}\cdot\dfrac{dh}{T_0 - \beta h}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integration from $0$ to $h$:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{g}{\beta R}\cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{T_0}{T_0 - \beta h}\right)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Final expression:
$$\boxed{P = P_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{\beta h}{T_0} \right)^{\tfrac{g}{\beta R}}}$$

with $g/(\beta R) \approx 5.256$.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** FL 350 = 35,000 ft = $10{,}668\ \text{m}$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert to meters:
$$h = 35{,}000 \times 0.3048 = 10{,}668\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Standard temperature at FL 350:
$$T_{\text{ISA}} = 288.15 - 0.0065 \times 10{,}668 = 288.15 - 69.34 = 218.81\ \text{K} = -54.34^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Actual temperature:
$$T_{\text{actual}} = 218.81 - 10 = 208.81\ \text{K}$$

**Part (i) — Pressure Altitude (2 marks):**
With altimeter set to 1013.25 hPa, **pressure altitude = indicated altitude = 35,000 ft** (by definition of FL).

$$P = 1013.25 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{0.0065 \times 10{,}668}{288.15}\right)^{5.256}$$
$$P = 1013.25 \times (0.7518)^{5.256} \approx 238.4\ \text{hPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Density Altitude (5 marks):**
$$\rho = \dfrac{P}{R T} = \dfrac{23{,}840}{287 \times 208.81} = \dfrac{23{,}840}{59{,}929} \approx 0.3978\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

ISA density at this altitude:
$$\rho_{\text{ISA}} = \dfrac{P}{R T_{\text{ISA}}} = \dfrac{23{,}840}{287 \times 218.81} = \dfrac{23{,}840}{62{,}798} \approx 0.3796\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Density Altitude:** Find the altitude where ISA density equals $0.3978\ \text{kg/m}^3$:
$$\rho(h) = 0.3796 \times \left(\dfrac{288.15 - 0.0065 h}{288.15}\right)^{4.256}$$

Solving numerically: $h_{\text{density}} \approx 9{,}500\ \text{m} \approx \text{FL 311}$

$$\boxed{\text{Density Altitude} \approx \text{FL 311}\ (31{,}100\ \text{ft})}$$

**Interpretation:** Aircraft performs as if it is at a *lower* altitude due to colder (denser) air.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a): FM-CW Radio Altimeter (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** As shown in Section 4.3 — VCO → TX antenna → ground reflection → RX antenna → mixer → LPF → counter → display.

**Principle (2 marks):** The transmitter frequency is linearly swept; the round-trip delay $\tau = 2h/c$ creates a frequency difference at the mixer output.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2:
$$h = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b \cdot T_m}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$

---

#### Part (b): Flux Valve and Slaved Gyrocompass (7 marks)

**Flux valve (3 marks):**
- A three-spoke saturable inductor with one primary and two secondary windings
- Primary excited at $400\ \text{Hz}$; secondaries produce voltages proportional to the horizontal components of the Earth's field
- Phase and amplitude ratio yield heading: $\theta = \arctan(V_y/V_x)$

**Slaved gyrocompass (3 marks):**
- Directional gyroscope provides stable heading reference
- A **latitude nut** on the inner gimbal applies precession torque $= \omega_E \sin\phi$ to counter Earth rotation
- The slaving amplifier compares flux valve heading with gyro heading and applies a corrective torque to the gyro
- This continuously compensates for **apparent drift** due to Earth rotation and transport

**Slaving system diagram (1 mark):** As in Section 4.4.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Compass & Altimeter Problems**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** When applying variation/deviation, students often add East and subtract West inconsistently. **Always state your sign convention first.**
> 2. **FL confusion:** Flight Level is *always* pressure altitude referenced to **1013.25 hPa** — never actual altitude. Many students mix up FL with indicated altitude when altimeter is set to local QNH.
> 3. **FM-CW formula:** Confusing $T_m$ (sweep period) with pulse width. Remember $T_m$ is the **modulation period** of the frequency sweep.
> 4. **Density vs Pressure altitude:** Density altitude requires **temperature** input; pressure altitude is *only* a function of pressure. Mixing them up costs 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Aneroid capsule trap:** The aneroid is a *pressure sensor*, not an altitude sensor. The altitude scale is a *calibrated mapping* based on ISA. Students who write "altimeter measures altitude directly" lose marks.
> 6. **ANDs rule (acceleration):** Forgetting that *only* N/S headings are affected by dip-induced acceleration error — East/West are not.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ✅ Must-Know Concepts

- [ ] Aneroid altimeter = **pressure-to-altitude transducer** using ISA
- [ ] ISA constants: $P_0 = 1013.25\ \text{hPa}$, $T_0 = 288.15\ \text{K}$, $\beta = 0.0065\ \text{K/m}$
- [ ] Radio altimeter measures **absolute altitude (AGL)** via time-of-flight
- [ ] Pulsed radar formula: $h = ct/2$
- [ ] FM-CW formula: $h = cf_b T_m / (2\Delta f)$
- [ ] C-band allocation: $4.2$ to $4.4\ \text{GHz}$
- [ ] Magnetic compass heading chain: **C → D → M → V → T** (Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True)
- [ ] East is **positive (+)**, West is **negative (−)** in correction
- [ ] Dip causes **ANDs** (Acceleration on N/S → Deviation towards S) and **UNOS** (Undershoot North, Overshoot South in turns)
- [ ] Flux valve operates on **magnetic saturation principle** with $400\ \text{Hz}$ excitation
- [ ] Gyrocompass precession rate: $\omega_p = \omega_E \sin\phi$
- [ ] GPWS, autoland, and Category III ILS all depend on radio altimeter

### ⚠️ High-Yield Mnemonics

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **C D M V T** | Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True |
| **CDMV TV MDC** | Reverse chain (True → Compass) — same letters backwards |
| **ANDs** | Acceleration on North/South → deviation South |
| **UNOS** | Undershoot North, Overshoot South during turns |
| **East is least** | East variations/deviations are added (positive) |
| **West is best** | West variations/deviations are subtracted (negative) |

### 🎯 Common Numerical Questions

1. Compute ISA pressure at a given altitude (use $\beta$, $g$, $R$).
2. Compute altitude from FM-CW beat frequency.
3. Compute true heading given compass, deviation, and variation.
4. Convert FL to meters; convert QNH setting to pressure altitude.
5. Estimate flux valve output phase for a given heading.

### 📌 Definitions to Memorize Verbatim

- **Aneroid:** "An evacuated, sealed metallic capsule used to detect changes in atmospheric pressure."
- **Pressure Altitude:** "Altitude above the standard pressure datum of 1013.25 hPa."
- **Density Altitude:** "Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature."
- **Absolute Altitude:** "Vertical distance from aircraft to terrain directly below."
- **Variation:** "Angle between True North and Magnetic North."
- **Deviation:** "Angle between Magnetic North and Compass North, caused by aircraft's magnetic fields."
- **Dip:** "Vertical component of Earth's magnetic field causing the compass needle to incline from horizontal."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
