# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Proportional Navigation (PN) Guidance Law

We derive the proportional navigation command from first principles. This is a frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU Module 4.

### 3.1.1 Geometry and Definitions

Let the missile be at position $M(x, y)$ and the target at $T(x_T, y_T)$ in an inertial 2-D plane.

**Step 1 — Define the line-of-sight (LOS) angle $\lambda$:**

$$
\lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y_T - y}{x_T - x}\right)
$$

The line-of-sight *rate* $\dot{\lambda}$ is the rate at which this angle changes as both the missile and target move.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\lambda$ with respect to time:**

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{d}{dt}\arctan\!\left(\frac{y_T - y}{x_T - x}\right)
$$

Applying the chain rule with $R_{los}^2 = (x_T - x)^2 + (y_T - y)^2$:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{(x_T - x)(\dot{y}_T - \dot{y}) - (y_T - y)(\dot{x}_T - \dot{x})}{R_{los}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce relative velocity components:**

Define the relative velocity vector $V_{rel} = V_T - V_M$. Its components are:

$$
V_{rel,x} = \dot{x}_T - \dot{x}, \qquad V_{rel,y} = \dot{y}_T - \dot{y}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $\dot{\lambda}$ in terms of closing velocity and LOS geometry:**

Decompose the relative velocity into components *along* and *perpendicular* to the LOS. The perpendicular component is $V_{rel,\perp} = V_{rel} \sin(\eta)$, where $\eta$ is the angle between the relative velocity and the LOS. Therefore:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{V_{rel} \sin(\eta)}{R_{los}} = \frac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R_{los}}
$$

Where $\eta_T$ and $\eta_M$ are the angles made by target and missile velocity vectors with the LOS respectively.

**Step 5 — Apply the small-angle / non-maneuvering-target assumption (pure pursuit baseline):**

If the target is non-maneuvering and the missile is on a collision course such that $V_T \sin(\eta_T) = V_M \sin(\eta_M)$, then $\dot{\lambda} \to 0$. The PN law breaks this singularity by *commanding* an acceleration proportional to $\dot{\lambda}$.

**Step 6 — State the Proportional Navigation law:**

The commanded lateral acceleration perpendicular to the missile's velocity vector is:

$$
a_{cmd} = N \cdot V_c \cdot \dot{\lambda}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = navigation constant (dimensionless), typically $3 \le N \le 5$
- $V_c$ = closing velocity $= -\dot{R}_{los}$
- $\dot{\lambda}$ = LOS rate in rad/s

**Step 7 — Substituting the closing-velocity form:**

$$
a_{cmd} = -N \cdot \dot{R}_{los} \cdot \dot{\lambda}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** PN "punishes" any rotation of the LOS. The faster the LOS rotates (i.e., the target tries to dodge), the harder the missile turns. The factor $N$ is the *gain* — higher $N$ means tighter tracking but more sensitivity to noise.

## 3.2 Derivation: INS Position Error Growth (1-D Vertical Channel)

This derivation shows *why* a pure INS error grows with time — a classic 7-mark part-(a) question.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Assume a 1-D vertical channel with a single accelerometer measuring specific force $f$. The platform is stationary on the ground, so *true* acceleration $a = 0$, and $f = -g$ (accelerometer reads the reaction to gravity).

**Step 1 — Accelerometer output (true):**

$$
f_{true} = a - g = 0 - g = -g
$$

**Step 2 — Accelerometer output (measured, with bias $b_f$):**

$$
f_{meas} = f_{true} + b_f = -g + b_f
$$

**Step 3 — Naive (incorrect) acceleration estimate:**

A naive navigator would interpret $f_{meas}$ as $\hat{a}$:

$$
\hat{a} = f_{meas} = -g + b_f
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity after time $t$ (integrating acceleration):**

$$
\hat{v}(t) = \int_0^t \hat{a}\, d\tau = (-g + b_f)\, t
$$

**Step 5 — Position after time $t$ (integrating velocity):**

$$
\hat{r}(t) = \int_0^t \hat{v}(\tau)\, d\tau = \frac{1}{2}(-g + b_f)\, t^2
$$

**Step 6 — Position error $\delta r = \hat{r} - r_{true}$:**

Since $r_{true} = 0$ (stationary):

$$
\delta r(t) = \frac{1}{2}\, b_f\, t^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A *constant* accelerometer bias of just $b_f = 10^{-4}$ g ($\approx 10^{-3}$ m/s²) produces a position error of $\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times (3600)^2 \approx 6480$ m after **1 hour**. This quadratic-in-time growth is the fundamental reason INS is almost always integrated with GPS in modern systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Navigation Techniques

| Parameter | Celestial | LORAN-C | VOR/DME | INS | GPS | INS/GPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coverage** | Global | Regional | Line-of-sight | Global | Global | Global |
| **Accuracy** | 1–2 nm | 0.25 nm | ±90 m, ±0.5 nm | 0.1–1 nm/h | 5–10 m | 1–3 m |
| **Update rate** | Manual | 10 Hz | 1 Hz | 100–200 Hz | 1–10 Hz | 100 Hz |
| **Error growth** | N/A | Bounded | Bounded | Unbounded ($\sim t^2$) | Bounded | Bounded |
| **Weather dep.** | Yes (sky) | No | No | No | Slight (ionosphere) | No |
| **Jamming vuln.** | None | Low | Low | Immune | High | Low |
| **Cost** | Low | High ground | Medium ground | High | Low (receiver) | Medium |
| **KTU weight** | 2 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 5 |

## 3.4 Quantitative Worked Example (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A missile travels at $V_M = 300$ m/s. The target is at range $R_{los} = 5$ km and moves perpendicularly at $V_T = 50$ m/s. The relative velocity angle to LOS is $\eta = 90°$. Using proportional navigation with $N = 4$, find the commanded acceleration.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute LOS rate:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{V_{rel} \sin(\eta)}{R_{los}} = \frac{50 \times \sin(90°)}{5000} = \frac{50}{5000} = 0.01 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 2 — Closing velocity (assume $V_c = V_M$ for head-on approximation):

$$
V_c \approx V_M = 300 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Apply PN law:

$$
a_{cmd} = N \cdot V_c \cdot \dot{\lambda} = 4 \times 300 \times 0.01 = 12 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

Step 4 — Express in g-units:

$$
a_{cmd} = \frac{12}{9.81} \approx 1.22\, g
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *low* g-command — typical of cruise missiles. Air-to-air missiles with $N=5$ and $V_M=600$ m/s, $V_T=400$ m/s, can demand 30+ g. If $a_{cmd}$ exceeds the missile's structural g-limit, guidance is **g-saturated** and PN fails.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Navigation, Guidance, and Control in the context of an aerospace vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

Navigation, Guidance, and Control (NGC) are three sequential subsystems in any aerospace vehicle. **Navigation** is the process of determining the vehicle's current position, velocity, and attitude (PVA) using onboard sensors such as GPS receivers, inertial measurement units (IMUs), or altimeters. It answers the question *“Where am I?”*. **Guidance** is the function of computing the desired trajectory from the present state to the target state, using algorithms such as waypoint navigation, pursuit guidance, or proportional navigation. It answers *“Where should I go?”*. **Control** is the function of generating actuator commands (e.g., aileron, elevator, throttle) to make the actual vehicle motion follow the guidance-referenced trajectory. It answers *“How do I get there?”*. The three subsystems are arranged in cascade: Navigation output feeds Guidance, Guidance output feeds Control, and the resulting vehicle motion is sensed again by Navigation, forming a closed loop.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by “specific force” as measured by an accelerometer? Why is gravity compensation needed in inertial navigation?**

**Model Answer:**

A *specific force* $f$ is the non-gravitational force per unit mass experienced by a proof mass inside an accelerometer. By Newton's second law, it equals the difference between the true kinematic acceleration $a$ of the vehicle and the gravitational acceleration $g$:

$$
f = a - g
$$

In inertial navigation, the accelerometer measures $f$, **not** the true acceleration $a$. To obtain $a$ (which can be integrated to get velocity and then position), the gravity vector $g$ must be added back to the accelerometer output. Without this *gravity compensation*, integrating the raw specific force would yield a velocity that drifts at approximately $9.81$ m/s per second — an unacceptable error within seconds. Hence, an accurate local gravity model (e.g., the WGS-84 gravity anomaly model) is essential for any INS.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an Inertial Navigation System (INS). Derive the mechanization equation that relates specific force to velocity in the ECEF frame.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft is fitted with a navigation-grade INS whose accelerometer bias is $b_f = 50\,\mu g$ and gyro drift is $0.005\,°/\text{h}$. After 2 hours of unaided INS operation, estimate:
(i) the position error due to the accelerometer bias, and
(ii) the attitude error due to the gyro drift. Comment on the suitability of unaided INS for a 5-hour oceanic flight.

#### Model Solution to A(a)

A *strapdown INS* consists of an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) containing three gyroscopes and three accelerometers rigidly mounted to the airframe, a navigation computer performing the mechanization, and output interfaces. The functional flow is:

1. Gyros measure the body angular rates $\omega_{ib}^b$.
2. The navigation computer integrates these (using quaternions or a Direction Cosine Matrix) to maintain the attitude quaternion $q_b^n$, which transforms vectors from body to navigation frame.
3. Accelerometers measure the specific force $f^b$.
4. This is rotated to the navigation frame: $f^n = C_b^n f^b$.
5. The gravity vector $g^n$ is added to obtain $\dot{v}^n$.
6. Velocity and position are integrated: $v^n = \int \dot{v}^n\, dt$, $r^n = \int v^n\, dt$.
7. Coriolis and Earth-rate corrections are applied.

**Derivation of the ECEF mechanization equation:**

The position vector in the inertial frame satisfies:

$$
\ddot{r}^i = a^i
$$

Transforming to the Earth-fixed (ECEF) frame, with $\Omega_{ie}$ being the Earth's rotation vector:

$$
\ddot{r}^e = \ddot{r}^i - 2\Omega_{ie}^e \dot{r}^e - \Omega_{ie}^e \Omega_{ie}^e r^e
$$

The accelerometer measures the specific force, which in the ECEF frame is:

$$
f^e = \ddot{r}^e - g^e
$$

Combining the two:

$$
f^e = a^e - g^e = \ddot{r}^e - g^e
$$

$$
\dot{v}^e = f^e + g^e - 2\Omega_{ie}^e v^e - \Omega_{ie}^e \Omega_{ie}^e r^e
$$

Where $v^e = \dot{r}^e$. This is the standard ECEF mechanization equation. *(7 marks: 3 for block diagram explanation, 4 for derivation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**(i) Position error from accelerometer bias:**

Converting bias: $b_f = 50\,\mu g = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s².

Time of operation: $t = 2\,\text{h} = 7200$ s.

Position error:

$$
\delta r = \frac{1}{2} b_f t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times (7200)^2
$$

$$
\delta r = 0.5 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.184 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\delta r \approx 12{,}712 \text{ m} \approx 12.7 \text{ km}
$$

**(ii) Attitude error from gyro drift:**

Gyro drift rate: $0.005\,°/\text{h}$. Over 2 hours, attitude error:

$$
\delta \theta = 0.005 \times 2 = 0.01\,° \approx 36 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

**Comment on oceanic flight (5 hours):**

A 5-hour flight would give $\delta r \approx 0.5 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times (18000)^2 \approx 79{,}450$ m $\approx 80$ km. This far exceeds the Required Navigation Performance (RNP) for oceanic flight (RNP-4 → ±7.4 km). Therefore, **unaided INS is unsuitable** for oceanic routes; an integrated INS/GPS system is mandatory to bound the errors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Proportional Navigation (PN) guidance. Derive the expression for the commanded lateral acceleration in a 2-D engagement geometry. State the significance of the navigation constant $N$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A surface-to-air missile uses PN with $N=4$. The target is at range $R=8$ km and is moving perpendicular to the LOS at $V_T=200$ m/s. The missile velocity is $V_M=600$ m/s.
(i) Compute the LOS rate.
(ii) Compute the commanded acceleration.
(iii) What happens if the target performs a 5 g evasive maneuver perpendicular to the LOS?

#### Model Solution to B(a)

Proportional Navigation (PN) is a homing guidance law in which the missile's commanded lateral acceleration is made proportional to the line-of-sight (LOS) rate $\dot{\lambda}$. The rationale is that a rotating LOS indicates a maneuvering target, and the missile must turn *more* as the LOS rotates *faster*.

**Derivation (compact form):**

From the geometry in Section 3.1, the LOS rate is:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}
$$

The PN law commands an acceleration perpendicular to the missile's velocity:

$$
a_{cmd} = N \cdot V_c \cdot \dot{\lambda}
$$

Where $V_c$ is the closing velocity. Substituting:

$$
a_{cmd} = N \cdot V_c \cdot \frac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}
$$

**Significance of $N$:** The *navigation constant* $N$ is the gain of the guidance loop. A higher $N$ yields tighter tracking and smaller miss distance, but at the cost of higher sensitivity to seeker noise and increased g-demand. Theory shows that for non-maneuvering targets, any $N \ge 2$ guarantees interception. For maneuvering targets, $N \ge 3$ is typical, and $N=4$–$5$ is common in tactical missiles. Practically, $N$ is chosen such that the *maximum* demanded $a_{cmd}$ does not exceed the airframe's g-limit.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**(i) LOS rate:**

The target moves perpendicular to the LOS, so the perpendicular relative velocity is $V_{T,\perp} = V_T = 200$ m/s.

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \frac{V_{T,\perp}}{R} = \frac{200}{8000} = 0.025 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Commanded acceleration:**

Closing velocity (approximate): $V_c \approx V_M = 600$ m/s.

$$
a_{cmd} = N \cdot V_c \cdot \dot{\lambda} = 4 \times 600 \times 0.025 = 60 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

In g-units:

$$
a_{cmd} = \frac{60}{9.81} \approx 6.12\,g
$$

**(iii) Effect of 5 g target maneuver:**

The target's lateral acceleration is $a_T = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05$ m/s². This adds an extra term to the LOS rate as the LOS begins to rotate due to the maneuver itself. The *additional* LOS rate induced is approximately:

$$
\Delta \dot{\lambda} \approx \frac{a_T \cdot t_{go}}{R}
$$

With time-to-go $t_{go} \approx R/V_c = 8000/600 \approx 13.3$ s:

$$
\Delta \dot{\lambda} \approx \frac{49.05 \times 13.3}{8000} \approx 0.0815 \text{ rad/s}
$$

The new commanded acceleration would be:

$$
a_{cmd}' \approx 4 \times 600 \times 0.0815 = 195.6 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 19.94\, g
$$

This exceeds typical airframe limits (15–20 g for SAMs), leading to **g-saturation** and a probable miss. Increasing $N$ further would worsen the noise problem; the missile is said to be **maneuver-limited**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the centripetal term** $\Omega_{ie}^e \Omega_{ie}^e r^e$ in the INS mechanization equation. Examiners routinely allocate 1 mark for this term. If you write only $f^e + g^e - 2\Omega_{ie}^e v^e$, you lose that mark.
> 2. **Confusing "closing velocity"** with "missile velocity." $V_c$ is the *rate of change of LOS range* $V_c = -\dot{R}_{los}$, not the missile's airspeed.
> 3. **Wrong sign on PN command.** In the convention $a_{cmd} = N V_c \dot{\lambda}$, the sign must match your chosen positive LOS-rotation sense. State the convention explicitly to avoid sign-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing up "accuracy" and "precision."** Accuracy = closeness to truth; precision = repeatability. Many students swap these in definitions.
> 5. **Neglecting to mention Kalman filter** in any "integrated navigation" 7-mark question. INS/GPS without Kalman fusion is just two independent systems — not *integrated*.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in NGC architecture answers. Even if your prose is perfect, a missing diagram costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Unit errors on PN.** $a_{cmd}$ must be in m/s² (or g). Students often forget the units of $N$ (dimensionless), $V_c$ (m/s), and $\dot{\lambda}$ (rad/s).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Introduction to Navigation & Guidance Instrumentation**

- **NGC trilogy** — Navigation (where am I?), Guidance (where should I go?), Control (how to get there?). Always cite the cascade order.
- **Navigation** delivers PVA = **Position, Velocity, Attitude**.
- **Three navigation classes:** *Position-fix* (absolute), *Dead-reckoning* (relative, error grows with time), and *Integrated* (Kalman-fused).
- **INS error growth** is **quadratic in time** $\delta r = \frac{1}{2} b_f t^2$ — the *raison d'être* for GPS aiding.
- **Specific force** $f = a - g$. Accelerometers measure $f$, not $a$. Gravity must be added back.
- **INS mechanization equation** (ECEF):
  $\dot{v}^e = f^e + g^e - 2\Omega_{ie}^e v^e - \Omega_{ie}^e \Omega_{ie}^e r^e$. Memorize the four terms: *specific force, gravity, Coriolis, centripetal*.
- **Earth's rotation rate** $\Omega_e = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s — appears in Coriolis and centripetal terms.
- **Coordinate frames** to know cold: *ECI, ECEF, NED, Body, Geographic (lat/lon/h)*.
- **Proportional Navigation** is the workhorse missile guidance law: $a_{cmd} = N V_c \dot{\lambda}$, with $N = 3$–$5$ typical.
- **Pursuit guidance** = point at target (simple, inefficient). **PN** = counter LOS rotation (efficient, dominant).
- **g-saturation** occurs when $a_{cmd}$ exceeds airframe limit; missile cannot intercept maneuvering targets beyond its g-capability.
- **Performance metrics** — accuracy, precision, update rate, availability, continuity, integrity. Define each precisely.
- **GPS** is the modern *position-fix* workhorse; **INS/GPS** is the modern *integrated* workhorse.
- **Kalman filter** is the *fusion algorithm* of choice in modern integrated navigation — mention it explicitly in any integrated-system answer.
- **Accuracy tier of gyros:** Strategic (0.0001 °/h) → Navigation (0.01 °/h) → Tactical (1 °/h) → Commercial (10 °/h+).
- **DGPS/WAAS/LAAS** improve GPS accuracy from ~5 m to ~1 m or better — recognize augmentation systems.
- **Coordinate transformations** between frames use Direction Cosine Matrices (DCM) or quaternions; quaternions avoid gimbal lock.
- **The navigation constant $N$ is dimensionless** but the *effective navigation ratio* is $N'$ = actual $a_{cmd}$ divided by $V_c \dot{\lambda}$ — must equal chosen $N$ for optimum homing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle, Construction and Application of Inertial Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle, Construction and Application of Inertial Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Inertial Sensors** are a class of transducers that detect and measure physical quantities related to the inertial state of a body — specifically, **linear acceleration** and **angular rate (rotation)** — without requiring any external reference frame. They form the fundamental sensing nucleus of every **Inertial Navigation System (INS)**, **Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS)**, and **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context, inertial sensors are categorised into two primary families:

| Family | Measured Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accelerometer** | Specific force / linear acceleration | m/s² (or g) | Voltage / digital count |
| **Gyroscope** | Angular rate / rotation | rad/s (or °/hr) | Voltage / digital count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4, students are expected to master the *operating principle, mechanical/electrical construction, and aerospace/navigation application* of the following canonical inertial sensors:
> 1. **Pendulous (Force-Balance) Accelerometer**
> 2. **Vibrating Beam / Resonant Beam Accelerometer**
> 3. **Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)**
> 4. **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)**
> 5. **MEMS Gyroscope (Coriolis Vibratory Gyro)**

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Human Body Analogy

Imagine you are sitting inside a **completely darkened, windowless aircraft cabin** in smooth, level flight. You cannot see the horizon, feel the wind, or hear the engines. Despite this total isolation, you can still:

* Feel whether the aircraft is **speeding up, slowing down, or banking into a turn** (this is your body's **accelerometer** — the fluid in your inner ear vestibular otolith organs).
* Detect whether you are **rotating or spinning** in place (this is your body's **gyroscope** — the three semicircular canals of the inner ear).

**Inertial sensors do exactly the same job — but with engineering precision.** They allow a vehicle (aircraft, spacecraft, missile, ship, submarine, or autonomous car) to "feel" its own motion relative to inertial space, completely independent of GPS, stars, or magnetic north.

## 1.3 The Two Foundational Physical Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physics Used by Inertial Sensors**
> 1. **Newton's Second Law (F = ma)** — the basis for all accelerometers. A known mass experiencing acceleration develops an inertial force, which is transduced into an electrical signal.
> 2. **Sagnac Effect / Coriolis Effect** — the basis for all optical and vibratory gyroscopes. A rotating frame splits a light beam (or vibrating mass) into two paths, and the resulting phase/path difference is directly proportional to the rotation rate.

---

## 1.4 Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INERTIAL SENSORS]:::root --> B[ACCELEROMETERS]:::acc
    A[C[GYROSCOPES]:::gyro

    B --> B1[Pendulous Force Balance]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Servo / Torque Rebalance]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Vibrating Beam Resonant]:::leaf
    B --> B4[MEMS Capacitive]:::leaf
    B --> B5[Strain Gauge / Piezoelectric]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Mechanical Rotor Gyro]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Ring Laser Gyro RLG]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Fiber Optic Gyro FOG]:::leaf
    C --> C4[MEMS Coriolis Vibratory]:::leaf
    C --> C5[Hemispherical Resonator Gyro HRG]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f2a44,stroke-width:2px
    classDef acc fill:#2b6cb0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2b6cb0
    classDef gyro fill:#9b2c2c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9b2c2c
    classDef leaf fill:#f7fafc,color:#1a202c,stroke:#cbd5e0
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pendulous (Force-Balance) Accelerometer

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A **proof mass** is suspended from a hinged pendulous arm inside a sealed, gas-damped case. When the case accelerates, the inertia of the proof mass causes a tiny angular displacement of the pendulous arm. A **capacitive pick-off** detects this displacement, and a **torque motor (forcer)** applies a rebalancing torque to keep the mass centred. The current required to rebalance the mass is directly proportional to the applied acceleration.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic
* **Step 1 — Inertial Reaction:** The aircraft accelerates with specific force `a`. The proof mass `m` experiences an inertial reaction force `F = m·a`.
* **Step 2 — Torque Generation:** Because the mass is offset by a pendulous arm of length `l`, this force generates a torque about the hinge: `T = m·l·a`.
* **Step 3 — Pick-off Sensing:** The resulting micro-rotation `θ` is sensed by a differential capacitor.
* **Step 4 — Closed-Loop Re-balance:** A servo amplifier drives a current `i` through the torque coil, producing an electromagnetic counter-torque `T_c = K_t·i` that exactly cancels `T`.
* **Step 5 — Output:** The measured current `i` is the accelerometer output, and `a = (K_t/m·l)·i`.

### 2.1.3 Why It Works So Well
The closed-loop operation makes the sensor **insensitive to pick-off non-linearity**, achieving very high scale-factor stability (often better than **10 ppm**) — essential for long-duration inertial navigation in civil aviation and submarine INS.

## 2.2 The Vibrating Beam Accelerometer

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
A tiny **quartz crystal beam** is forced into mechanical resonance at its natural frequency `f_0`. When subjected to acceleration, the induced axial compressive/tensile stress changes the beam's stiffness, and hence its **resonant frequency shifts**. The frequency difference is the output.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic
* **Step 1 — Drive Oscillator:** Electronics maintain the beam in vibration at `f_0`.
* **Step 2 — Stress Coupling:** Acceleration `a` creates axial force `F = m·a` on the beam.
* **Step 3 — Frequency Modulation:** Beam stiffness `k` changes, so `f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m_eff}` shifts to `f_0 + \Delta f`.
* **Step 4 — Output:** `a = K·\Delta f`, where `K` is the scale factor.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Vibrating beam accelerometers are the **gold standard for space-grade navigation** (e.g., used in the Space Shuttle IMU and most satellite GNC systems) because quartz is inherently **radiation-hard** and the frequency output is intrinsically digital — perfect for high-reliability, radiation-tolerant space computers.

## 2.3 The Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)

### 2.3.1 The Sagnac Effect
When a closed optical path rotates, light travelling **with** the rotation must traverse a longer path than light travelling **against** the rotation. This path-length difference manifests as a **frequency difference (beat frequency)** between the two counter-propagating laser beams in a closed triangular or square cavity.

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic
* **Step 1 — Helium-Neon Plasma Discharge:** A high-voltage discharge excites He-Ne gas inside a precisely-machined **closed optical cavity** (usually a block of Zerodur glass with three bores forming a triangle).
* **Step 2 — Bidirectional Lasing:** Two coherent laser beams propagate in opposite directions around the ring.
* **Step 3 — Path Difference:** When the block rotates at angular rate `\Omega`, the two beams experience different effective path lengths (Sagnac effect).
* **Step 4 — Beat Frequency Detection:** A photodetector at the corner measures the interference, producing a beat frequency `\Delta f` proportional to `\Omega`.
* **Step 5 — Output:** Angular rate `\Omega = K·\Delta f`.

### 2.3.3 The Lock-In Problem
At very low rotation rates (`< 100 °/hr`), the two beams **lock into the same frequency** due to back-scatter from the mirrors. This is solved by mechanically **dithering** the entire RLG block at a few hundred Hz using a piezo-electric actuator, ensuring the gyro is never at zero input long enough to lock.

## 2.4 The Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle (Sagnac Effect in Fiber)
Instead of a free-space laser cavity, light from a **broadband SLD (Superluminescent Diode)** is split and sent in both directions through a long coil (typically 200 m to 5 km) of single-mode optical fiber. Rotation produces a **phase shift** between the two beams, which is detected interferometrically.

### 2.4.2 Operational Logic
* **Step 1 — Light Source:** An SLD emits low-coherence light to minimise back-scatter noise.
* **Step 2 — Beam Splitting:** An integrated optics Y-junction splits the beam into CW and CCW paths.
* **Step 3 — Fiber Coil Transit:** Each beam travels `N` turns of a coil of radius `R`.
* **Step 4 — Sagnac Phase Shift:** Rotation `\Omega` induces a non-reciprocal phase difference `\Delta\varphi`.
* **Step 5 — Interferometric Readout:** The recombined beams produce a sinusoidal intensity variation at the detector.
* **Step 6 — Output:** `\Omega = (\lambda c)/(4\pi R^2 N)·\Delta\varphi`.

## 2.5 The MEMS Coriolis Vibratory Gyroscope

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle (Coriolis Effect)
A microscopic proof mass is driven into **primary linear oscillation** at frequency `f_drive`. When the sensor rotates, the **Coriolis force** acts perpendicular to both the velocity and rotation vectors, inducing a **secondary (sense) oscillation** whose amplitude is proportional to the angular rate.

### 2.5.2 Operational Logic
* **Step 1 — Drive Mode:** Comb-finger electrostatic actuators oscillate the mass laterally at `f_drive` with velocity `v`.
* **Step 2 — Coriolis Coupling:** Rotation `\Omega` induces force `F_c = 2m(v \times \Omega)`.
* **Step 3 — Sense Mode:** `F_c` drives the mass into a perpendicular out-of-plane vibration.
* **Step 4 — Capacitive Pick-off:** Differential comb fingers sense the displacement.
* **Step 5 — Output:** `\Omega = K·V_{sense}/V_{drive}`.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it contains 100% of the equations needed for KTU Module 4 numericals and theory questions.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Variables / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd Law — basis of all accelerometers | $m$ = kg, $a$ = m/s² |
| 2 | $T = m \cdot l \cdot a$ | Torque on pendulous arm | $l$ = m, $T$ = N·m |
| 3 | $a = \dfrac{K_t}{m \cdot l} \cdot i$ | Pendulous accelerometer output (closed-loop) | $i$ = A, $K_t$ = N·m/A |
| 4 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | Resonant frequency of vibrating beam | $k$ = N/m, $f_0$ = Hz |
| 5 | $a = K \cdot \Delta f$ | Vibrating beam output (frequency-domain) | $K$ = (m/s²)/Hz |
| 6 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c^{2}}$ | Sagnac transit-time difference (RLG/FOG) | $A$ = m², $c$ = 3×10⁸ m/s |
| 7 | $\Delta f_{RLG} = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{P \lambda}$ | RLG beat frequency | $P$ = perimeter (m), $\lambda$ = m |
| 8 | $\Delta\varphi_{FOG} = \dfrac{8 \pi A \Omega N}{c \lambda}$ | FOG Sagnac phase shift | $N$ = turns, $\lambda$ = m |
| 9 | $\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c}{4 \pi R^{2} N} \cdot \Delta\varphi$ | FOG output | $R$ = coil radius (m) |
| 10 | $F_c = 2 m (v \times \Omega)$ | Coriolis force in MEMS gyro | $v$ = m/s, $\Omega$ = rad/s |
| 11 | $V_{out} = K \cdot \dfrac{V_{sense}}{V_{drive}}$ | MEMS gyro rate output | $K$ = V/(°/s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> * Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> * Standard gravity: $g = 9.80665$ m/s²
> * He-Ne laser wavelength: $\lambda = 632.8$ nm
> * 1 radian = $\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ degrees

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Civil Aviation INS** (e.g., Honeywell Laseref IV): uses RLG + pendulous accelerometers → **< 0.01 °/hr drift**.
* **Commercial Drones & Smartphones**: use MEMS IMUs (BMI088, MPU-9250) for attitude stabilisation.
* **Strategic Missile Guidance**: uses RLG/FOG INS for **autonomous mid-course navigation without GPS**.
* **Spacecraft GNC**: uses hemispherical resonator gyros + vibrating beam accelerometers (e.g., **NASA Cassini, James Webb Telescope**).
* **Autonomous Vehicles**: MEMS IMUs provide dead-reckoning backup when GPS is denied.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pendulous Accelerometer Output Equation

**Starting Point:** A proof mass `m` is offset by distance `l` from a pivot. The case containing the assembly accelerates with specific force `a`. The pendulous arm is deflected, and a torque motor provides counter-torque.

**Step 1 — Identify the inertial force on the mass**

When the case accelerates at `a`, in the case frame the mass experiences a fictitious inertial force:

$$F_{inertial} = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2 — Convert linear force to torque about the pivot**

Because the mass is offset by the pendulous arm length `l`, this force produces a torque about the pivot:

$$T_{inertial} = F_{inertial} \cdot l = m \cdot l \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium under servo rebalance**

The torque motor supplies a counter-torque `T_c` that balances `T_{inertial}` (since the pick-off keeps the arm at null):

$$T_{c} = T_{inertial}$$

$$K_{t} \cdot i = m \cdot l \cdot a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the output current `i`**

Rearranging the equation to express the measured current in terms of the applied acceleration:

$$i = \frac{m \cdot l}{K_{t}} \cdot a$$

Or, equivalently, expressing the acceleration as a function of the measured current:

$$a = \frac{K_{t}}{m \cdot l} \cdot i$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$a = K_a \cdot i, \quad \text{where} \quad K_a = \frac{K_{t}}{m \cdot l}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The scale factor $K_a$ is set by the *physical geometry* and *torquer constant*. This is why pendulous accelerometers are so stable — the scale factor is governed by **macroscopic, mechanically stable quantities** (mass, lever arm, coil turns), not by exotic electronic constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sagnac Phase Shift in a Fiber Optic Gyroscope

**Starting Point:** Two coherent light beams, of wavelength `\lambda`, are injected into a fiber coil of radius `R` and `N` turns, one travelling clockwise (CW) and one counter-clockwise (CCW). The coil rotates about its axis at angular rate `\Omega`.

**Step 1 — Define the effective area enclosed**

A single circular turn of fiber encloses area:

$$A_{1} = \pi R^{2}$$

For `N` turns, the total effective area is:

$$A_{total} = N \pi R^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Sagnac transit-time difference (general expression)**

According to the Sagnac effect, the time difference between the two counter-propagating beams is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{4 A_{total} \, \Omega}{c^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert time difference to phase difference**

A phase shift `\Delta\varphi` relates to a transit-time difference `\Delta t` at optical frequency `f_{opt} = c/\lambda` as:

$$\Delta\varphi = 2 \pi f_{opt} \cdot \Delta t = \frac{2 \pi c}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Substitute `\Delta t`**

$$\Delta\varphi = \frac{2 \pi c}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4 A_{total} \, \Omega}{c^{2}} = \frac{8 \pi A_{total} \, \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute `A_{total} = N\pi R^{2}`**

$$\Delta\varphi = \frac{8 \pi \cdot (N \pi R^{2}) \cdot \Omega}{\lambda c} = \frac{8 \pi^{2} N R^{2} \, \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\Delta\varphi = \frac{8 \pi^{2} N R^{2}}{\lambda c} \cdot \Omega}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the angular rate (the sensor output equation)**

Rearranging:

$$\Omega = \frac{\lambda c}{8 \pi^{2} N R^{2}} \cdot \Delta\varphi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A FOG has a coil radius $R = 0.05$ m, $N = 1000$ turns, operating at $\lambda = 1550$ nm. The measured phase shift is $\Delta\varphi = 0.01$ rad.
>
> Using $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:
>
> $$\Omega = \frac{(1550 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{8})}{8 \pi^{2} \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.05)^{2}} \cdot 0.01$$
>
> $$\Omega = \frac{4.65 \times 10^{-1}}{8 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.0025} \cdot 0.01$$
>
> $$\Omega = \frac{0.465}{197.39} \cdot 0.01 = 2.355 \times 10^{-2} \cdot 0.01$$
>
> $$\Omega \approx 2.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.0135 \text{ °/s} \approx 48.5 \text{ °/hr}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RLG Beat Frequency

**Starting Point:** A ring laser cavity has perimeter `P` enclosing area `A`. Two counter-propagating He-Ne beams at wavelength `\lambda` lase simultaneously. The block rotates at rate `\Omega`.

**Step 1 — Optical path-length difference**

Due to the Sagnac effect, the two beams effectively traverse different path lengths:

$$\Delta L = \frac{4 A \Omega}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Wavelength to frequency conversion**

The frequency of each beam is `f = c/\lambda`. A small path-length change `\Delta L` produces a frequency shift:

$$\Delta f = \frac{c}{\lambda^{2}} \cdot \Delta L = \frac{c}{\lambda^{2}} \cdot \frac{4 A \Omega}{c} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Account for the cavity perimeter**

For a closed loop, the standing-wave condition depends on the perimeter `P`. The more rigorous RLG beat-frequency expression is:

$$\Delta f_{RLG} = \frac{4 A \Omega}{P \lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the input rate**

$$\Omega = \frac{P \lambda}{4 A} \cdot \Delta f_{RLG}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering note:** For a square RLG of side $a$, $A = a^{2}$ and $P = 4a$. So $\Delta f = (a \cdot \Omega)/\lambda$. This shows that **a larger RLG block produces a larger, more easily-measured beat frequency** — explaining why aircraft-grade RLGs (e.g., Honeywell GG1320) are physically large (~ 15 cm per side).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coriolis Force in MEMS Vibratory Gyro

**Starting Point:** A proof mass `m` oscillates linearly with velocity `v` in the `x`-direction. The sensor (and mass) rotate about the `z`-axis at rate `\Omega`.

**Step 1 — Vector definition**

$$\vec{v} = v \, \hat{x}, \quad \vec{\Omega} = \Omega \, \hat{z}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Coriolis acceleration formula**

In a rotating frame, the Coriolis acceleration is:

$$\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2 \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} = 2 (\Omega \, \hat{z}) \times (v \, \hat{x}) = 2 \Omega v \, \hat{y}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Coriolis force**

$$\vec{F}_{c} = m \vec{a}_{Cor} = 2 m \Omega v \, \hat{y}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of Coriolis force**

$$F_{c} = 2 m \Omega v$$

**Step 5 — Relate to drive-oscillation velocity**

The drive velocity amplitude is `v = \omega_d A_d`, where `\omega_d` is the drive frequency and `A_d` is the drive amplitude.

$$F_{c} = 2 m \omega_{d} A_{d} \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Sense-mode displacement**

The Coriolis force drives the sense mode as a second-order system with stiffness `k_s` and damping `c_s`. The peak displacement at resonance is:

$$y_{peak} = \frac{F_{c}}{k_{s}} = \frac{2 m \omega_{d} A_{d}}{k_{s}} \cdot \Omega$$

**Final Output Equation:**

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{k_{s}}{2 m \omega_{d} A_{d}} \cdot y_{sense}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — At a Glance

| Parameter | Pendulous Accel. | Vibrating Beam Accel. | RLG | FOG | MEMS Gyro |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Measured quantity** | Linear accel. | Linear accel. | Angular rate | Angular rate | Angular rate |
| **Underlying physics** | $F=ma$ | Stress → freq. shift | Sagnac (f) | Sagnac ($\varphi$) | Coriolis |
| **Typical bias stability** | < 50 $\mu g$ | < 10 $\mu g$ | < 0.001 °/hr | < 0.01 °/hr | ~ 10 °/hr |
| **Scale-factor type** | Voltage | Frequency | Frequency | Phase | Voltage |
| **Output type** | Analog | Digital-ready | Digital (pulse) | Digital | Analog / I²C |
| **Aero. application** | AHRS, INS | Spacecraft GNC | Civil aviation INS | Tactical missiles | Drones, IMU |
| **Cost class** | High | Very high | Very high | Medium | Very low |
| **Key limitation** | Temperature | Temperature | Lock-in at low $\Omega$ | Shupe / thermal | Bias drift |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Inertial Sensor (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[INPUT MOTION\nLINEAR OR ANGULAR]:::inp --> A1[INERTIAL ELEMENT\nPROOF MASS OR COIL]:::elem
    A1 --> A2[TRANSDUCER\nPICKOFF OR PHOTODETECTOR]:::trans
    A2 --> A3[SIGNAL CONDITIONER\nDEMOD AMPLIFIER]:::cond
    A3 --> A4{DECISION\nNULL OR OPEN LOOP}:::dec
    A4 --> A5[OUTPUT\nVOLTAGE OR FREQUENCY]:::out
    A4 --> A6[REBALANCE LOOP\nSERVO AMPLIFIER]:::loop
    A6 --> A7[TORQUE MOTOR\nOR PHASE MODULATOR]:::act
    A7 --> A1

    classDef inp fill:#fef3c7,color:#1a202c,stroke:#d69e2e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef elem fill:#e6fffa,color:#1a202c,stroke:#319795,stroke-width:2px
    classDef trans fill:#bee3f8,color:#1a202c,stroke:#3182ce,stroke-width:2px
```
