# Ring Laser Gyroscope — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Sagnac Effect and explain its role in a Ring Laser Gyroscope.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sagnac Effect describes the phenomenon in which two coherent light beams, propagating in opposite directions along a closed optical path, experience different optical path lengths when the path is rotating. In an RLG, this path-length asymmetry causes the CW and CCW lasing modes to oscillate at different frequencies. The difference (beat) frequency is directly proportional to the input rotation rate, allowing the RLG to function as an absolute angular rate sensor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often state that the path-length difference arises because "the light speed changes in a rotating frame." This is wrong — the speed of light is *invariant*. The correct explanation is that **the cavity walls move** relative to the inertial lab frame, so the *round-trip times* (and hence path lengths) of the two beams differ. Marks will be deducted for an incorrect physical statement.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is lock-in in a Ring Laser Gyroscope? List any two techniques used to overcome it.**

**Model Answer:**
Lock-in is the phenomenon in which, at very low rotation rates, the two counter-propagating laser modes are coupled by backscattered light from the cavity mirrors and lose their frequency separation, producing zero beat output. Two techniques to overcome it are:
1. **Mechanical dithering** — oscillating the entire gyro body about its sensing axis so the system spends most of the time outside the lock-in band.
2. **Magneto-optical (Faraday) bias** — applying an axial magnetic field with a non-planar crystal (e.g., quartz rotator) to introduce a non-reciprocal frequency bias.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Ring Laser Gyroscope. Discuss the role of the Sagnac Effect. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An equilateral-triangular RLG has a side length of 15 cm and operates at $\lambda = 632.8$ nm. The cavity is rotated at $30^{\circ}/\text{s}$ about an axis perpendicular to the cavity plane. Calculate:
   1. The scale factor $K$ in Hz/(rad/s).
   2. The beat frequency $\Delta f$.
   3. The total angle rotated in 1 minute, expressed in terms of beat counts. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working:**
A He-Ne RLG consists of:
- A **gas discharge tube** filled with He-Ne mixture, energized by an RF source to produce population inversion.
- **Three high-reflectivity mirrors** arranged at the corners of an equilateral triangle, one of which is partially transmissive to allow beam extraction.
- A **photodetector** that mixes the two extracted beams to produce a beat signal.
- A **frequency counter / signal processor**.

**Working:** In a stationary cavity, both beams lase at the same frequency. Under rotation, the Sagnac Effect creates an optical path difference $\Delta L = 4A\Omega \cos\theta / c$, which detunes the resonant frequencies of the two beams. The photodetector produces a heterodyne beat at $\Delta f = 4A\Omega \cos\theta / (\lambda L)$, which is proportional to the rotation rate.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
$L = 3s = 3 \times 0.15 = 0.45$ m
$A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} (0.15)^{2} = 0.00974$ m²

**Step 2 — Scale factor.**
$$K = \frac{4 A}{\lambda L} = \frac{4 \times 0.00974}{632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.45}$$

$$K = \frac{0.03896}{2.848 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.368 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz/(rad/s)}$$

**Step 3 — Beat frequency.**
$\Omega = 30^{\circ}/\text{s} = \pi / 6 \approx 0.5236$ rad/s
$$\Delta f = K \cdot \Omega = 1.368 \times 10^{5} \times 0.5236 = 7.164 \times 10^{4} \text{ Hz} \approx 71.64 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Total beat counts in 60 seconds.**
$$N = \Delta f \times T = 7.164 \times 10^{4} \times 60 = 4.298 \times 10^{6} \text{ counts}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Do not forget to convert degrees per second to **radians per second** before multiplying by the scale factor (which is defined in SI). A common mistake is to directly multiply Hz/(rad/s) by °/s, which yields a wrong answer by a factor of ~57.3. **(Loses 2 marks if missed.)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the beat frequency of a Ring Laser Gyroscope in terms of the cavity area, perimeter, wavelength, and input rotation rate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the lock-in phenomenon in RLGs. Describe in detail the mechanical dithering technique and the four-frequency RLG technique used to overcome lock-in. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
Refer to **Section 3.2** above. The key steps are:
1. Establish Sagnac path difference: $\Delta L = 4A\Omega \cos\theta / c$.
2. Apply resonance condition to rotating cavity: $\lambda_{\text{CW}} = L_{\text{CW}} / n$ and similarly for CCW.
3. Compute $\Delta f = f_{\text{CCW}} - f_{\text{CW}}$ using first-order binomial expansion.
4. Obtain final boxed result: $\Delta f = 4 A \Omega \cos\theta / (\lambda L)$.

**(b) Lock-in & Mitigation:**

**Lock-in phenomenon:** Backscattering from cavity mirrors couples the CW and CCW modes, forcing them to merge to a single frequency whenever the rotation-induced frequency splitting is below a critical threshold $\Omega_L$. Within this dead-band, the RLG produces *no output*, which is unacceptable for inertial navigation.

**Mechanical Dithering:** The entire RLG body is mounted on a **piezoelectric actuator** that oscillates the cavity sinusoidally about the input axis, typically at $f_d \approx 400$ Hz with peak amplitude $\pm 100^{\circ}/\text{hr}$. The dithering motion adds a large, time-varying bias that carries the instantaneous operating point *outside* the lock-in band for most of the cycle. The dither motion is sensed by a separate accelerometer and subtracted from the output, leaving only the true inertial rotation.

**Four-Frequency RLG:** This design uses **two isotopic species of Neon** ($^{20}$Ne and $^{22}$Ne) inside the same cavity, producing **four** lasing modes (CW and CCW for each isotope). The two isotopes have slightly different lasing frequencies, so even at zero rotation, each pair has a non-zero splitting. By taking the *difference* of the two beat frequencies (one for each isotope), common-mode errors such as magnetic-field drift and lock-in bias cancel, while the Sagnac-induced splitting doubles. This technique is used in high-precision navigation RLGs (e.g., Honeywell GG1320).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A common mistake is describing dithering as "rotating the gyro continuously so the average output equals the true rotation." This is incorrect. Dithering does **not** produce a net bias; it merely ensures the gyro *passes through* the linear regime where its output is unambiguous. The dither signal is rejected, not averaged. **(Loses 1–2 marks for wrong physical intuition.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- [x] **Sagnac Effect** — path-length difference $\Delta L = 4A\Omega \cos\theta / c$ arises because cavity walls move in the lab frame; light speed is invariant.
- [x] **Beat Frequency Equation** — $\Delta f = 4A\Omega \cos\theta / (\lambda L)$, central to every numerical problem.
- [x] **Scale Factor** — $K = 4A/(\lambda L)$; maximize by enlarging $A/L$ ratio (triangular cavity is optimal).
- [x] **Equilateral Triangle Geometry** — $L = 3s$, $A = \sqrt{3}s^{2}/4$, $A/L = \sqrt{3}s/12$.
- [x] **Lock-In Phenomenon** — backscatter coupling forces $\Delta f = 0$ below critical $\Omega_L$.
- [x] **Dithering Solution** — piezo oscillation about sensing axis; dither signal is subtracted from output.
- [x] **Four-Frequency RLG** — uses $^{20}$Ne and $^{22}$Ne; differential output cancels common-mode lock-in.
- [x] **Magneto-Optical Bias** — Faraday rotator introduces non-reciprocal frequency offset.
- [x] **He-Ne Wavelength** — standard laser line is $\lambda = 632.8$ nm (red).
- [x] **Output Interpretation** — beat count = rotation angle (with proper scale factor and unit conversion).
- [x] **Real-World RLGs** — Honeywell GG1320 (strategic), Sperry SLIC-7 (tactical), Sandia UGRL (large-area geodesic).
- [x] **Performance Metric** — bias stability of strategic-grade RLG: $< 0.001^{\circ}/\text{hr}$.
- [x] **Lock-In Threshold Formula** — $\Omega_L \propto \lambda^{2}\sigma / (A L)$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibre Optic Gyroscope, MEMS Gyroscopes and Accelerometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fibre Optic Gyroscope, MEMS Gyroscopes and Accelerometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Fibre Optic Gyroscope, MEMS Gyroscopes and Accelerometers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fibre Optic Gyroscope (FOG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibre Optic Gyroscope (FOG) — Formal Definition**
> A Fibre Optic Gyroscope is a solid-state, strap-down rotational rate sensor that measures angular velocity (or absolute rotation) by exploiting the **Sagnac Effect** — the relative phase shift induced between two counter-propagating light beams travelling through a coil of optical fibre when the coil experiences rotation about its axis.

**Sagnac Effect (the underlying physical phenomenon):**
When a beam of light is split into two equal halves and sent in opposite directions around a rotating closed loop (an optical fibre coil), the beam travelling *with* the rotation has to cover a slightly longer optical path than the beam travelling *against* the rotation. This path-length difference produces a measurable phase shift $\Delta\phi$, which is directly proportional to the angular rotation rate $\Omega$ of the coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Rotating Walking Track"**
> Imagine two runners on a circular track who start at the same point but run in opposite directions. If the track itself begins to rotate clockwise, the runner going clockwise must cover slightly more ground to come back to the start line than the runner running counter-clockwise. The *time difference* (or in optics, the *phase difference*) between their arrivals is exactly the Sagnac signal. The faster the track rotates, the larger the time/phase difference. A FOG does this with *photons* instead of runners, and uses kilometres of wound optical fibre to amplify the tiny phase shift into a usable signal.

**Key Features:**
- **No moving parts** (all-solid-state) → extremely high reliability, MTBF > 100,000 hours.
- High sensitivity: can detect rotation rates as low as **0.001°/hr** (navigation-grade).
- Wavelength of operation: typically **1550 nm** (telecom C-band) or **1310 nm**.
- Fibre coil length: from **100 m** (tactical grade) up to **5 km** (strategic/navigation grade).

---

### 1.2 MEMS Gyroscope

> [!NOTE]
> **MEMS Gyroscope — Formal Definition**
> A Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) gyroscope is a microfabricated vibrating-structure angular rate sensor that measures rotation by detecting the **Coriolis force** induced on a proof mass that is driven into resonant oscillation when the device is subjected to angular velocity about a sensitive axis.

**Core Operating Principle:**
A tiny suspended silicon structure is electrostatically driven to oscillate at a known primary resonant frequency $f_d$. When the chip is rotated about the sensitive axis, the moving proof mass experiences a Coriolis pseudo-force perpendicular to its velocity. This Coriolis force drives a secondary (sense) mode, whose amplitude is proportional to the applied angular rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Hula-Hoop on a Turntable"**
> Hold a vibrating tuning fork on a slowly rotating turntable. You will notice the prongs of the fork begin to *sway sideways* — perpendicular to their original vibration — the faster the turntable rotates. This sideways sway is exactly the Coriolis effect. A MEMS gyroscope builds a microscopic, silicon version of this tuning fork using electrostatic comb drives. The sideways motion is sensed capacitively and converted into a voltage proportional to the rotation rate.

**Key Features:**
- Size: typically **5 mm × 5 mm × 1 mm** chip (whole sensor board ~1 cm³).
- Bias stability: 1°/hr (tactical) to 10°/hr (consumer).
- Bandwidth: 50 Hz – 1 kHz.
- Mass-producible using standard CMOS-compatible silicon processes.

---

### 1.3 MEMS Accelerometer

> [!NOTE]
> **MEMS Accelerometer — Formal Definition**
> A MEMS accelerometer is a microfabricated inertial sensor that measures linear acceleration (proper acceleration) by detecting the **displacement of a proof mass** suspended by compliant flexures relative to a fixed reference frame, with the displacement transduced into an electrical signal proportional to the applied acceleration via **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Mass on a Spring"**
> Imagine a small mass attached to a horizontal spring anchored to a wall. When you push the wall forward (i.e., accelerate the assembly), the inertia of the mass causes it to *lag behind*, stretching (or compressing) the spring. The displacement of the mass is directly proportional to the acceleration of the wall. A MEMS accelerometer does precisely this on a silicon chip, with the "spring" being a micromachined flexure and the displacement read out as a change in capacitance between interdigitated comb fingers.

**Key Features:**
- Ranges: ±1 g (inclinometer) to ±100 g (high-g shock).
- Noise floor: down to **µg/√Hz** (seismic grade).
- Used for tilt, vibration, shock, navigation, airbag deployment.

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Devices Matter in Aerospace

> [!TIP]
> **Aerospace Context (KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping → CO4)**
> - **FOG** → Strap-down inertial navigation of commercial aircraft, spacecraft, ships, missiles (replaces mechanical spinning-mass gyros).
> - **MEMS Gyro** → AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference Systems) in UAVs, drones, small satellites, flight control computers of micro-aerial vehicles.
> - **MEMS Accelerometer** → Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs), flight-data recorders, airbag triggers in ejection seats, gravity-gradient attitude determination in CubeSats.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fibre Optic Gyroscope — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Sagnac Phase Shift (Core Equation)

When a fibre coil of area $A$, wound with $N$ turns, rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about its symmetry axis, the optical path difference between the CW (clockwise) and CCW (counter-clockwise) beams produces a phase shift:

$$
\Delta\phi_{Sagnac} = \frac{8\pi N A}{\lambda c} \cdot \Omega
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of turns of the fibre coil
- $A$ = enclosed area of one turn (m²)
- $\lambda$ = optical wavelength in vacuum (m)
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\Omega$ = input angular velocity (rad/s)

### 2.1.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Beam splitting:** A broadband superluminescent diode (SLD) source emits light at $\lambda \approx 1550$ nm, which is split into two orthogonal polarisation states by a polariser and then into CW and CCW beams by a coupler.
2. **Counter-propagation:** Both beams are launched into the same multi-turn fibre coil from opposite ends.
3. **Non-reciprocal path difference:** When the coil rotates at $\Omega$, the CW beam traverses a slightly longer geometric path than the CCW beam (or vice-versa), the difference being $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 A N \Omega}{c}$.
4. **Phase accumulation:** This path difference creates a non-reciprocal Sagnac phase shift $\Delta\phi_{Sagnac}$.
5. **Re-combination & interference:** The two beams recombine at the detector. The intensity $I$ depends on $\Delta\phi$ as $I = I_0 [1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)]$.
6. **Signal extraction:** Because $\Delta\phi$ is extremely small ($\sim 10^{-7}$ rad for Earth rate = 15°/hr), a **closed-loop** configuration with an integrated-optic phase modulator (Lissajous / Serrodyne modulation) is used to convert the small AC signal into a linear output.

### 2.1.3 Reciprocity & Bias Error Sources

- **Reciprocity theorem:** The configuration must be perfectly reciprocal so that the only non-reciprocal phase is due to rotation. Any non-reciprocity from stray birefringence, polarisation crosstalk, or back-reflection produces bias drift.
- **Shupe effect:** Time-varying temperature gradients along the fibre coil cause time-dependent index-of-refraction changes, mimicking rotation. Mitigated by winding the coil in a *symmetrical* (e.g., quadrupolar) pattern.

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility

| Application | Required Grade | Typical Drift |
|---|---|---|
| Tactical missiles | Tactical FOG | 1°/hr |
| Commercial aircraft INS | Navigation FOG | 0.01°/hr |
| Strategic submarines | Strategic FOG | 0.001°/hr |
| Spacecraft AOCS | Space-grade FOG | < 0.01°/hr |

---

## 2.2 MEMS Gyroscope — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Coriolis Acceleration

The fundamental force balance:

$$
\vec{F}_{Coriolis} = -2 \, m \, (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})
$$

where $\vec{v}$ is the velocity of the proof mass and $\vec{\Omega}$ is the applied angular rate.

For a primary drive-mode velocity $v_d = \hat{j} \, v_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$ and an input rotation $\vec{\Omega} = \hat{k} \, \Omega$:

$$
\vec{F}_{Cor} = -2m\Omega \, v_0 \cos(\omega_d t) \, \hat{i}
$$

This produces a sense-mode displacement $y(t)$ whose amplitude is directly proportional to $\Omega$.

### 2.2.2 Drive Mode & Sense Mode (Two-DOF Resonator)

The MEMS gyroscope is a **2-DOF** (two degree-of-freedom) resonator:

| Mode | Equation of Motion | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Drive (x) | $\ddot{x} + \dfrac{\omega_d}{Q_d}\dot{x} + \omega_d^2 x = \dfrac{F_d}{m}\sin(\omega_d t)$ | Maintains constant amplitude oscillation |
| Sense (y) | $\ddot{y} + \dfrac{\omega_s}{Q_s}\dot{y} + \omega_s^2 y = -2\Omega\,\dot{x}$ | Response proportional to $\Omega$ |

In *rate-mode* operation, the drive and sense resonant frequencies are **matched** ($\omega_d = \omega_s = \omega_0$), yielding maximum mechanical sensitivity.

### 2.2.3 Capacitive Readout

The sense-mode displacement $\Delta y$ changes the gap between interdigitated comb fingers from $g_0$ to $g_0 - \Delta y$, changing capacitance:

$$
C = \frac{n \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A_f}{g_0 - \Delta y}
$$

The differential sense output is:

$$
\Delta C = 2 C_0 \frac{\Delta y}{g_0}
$$

which is converted to a voltage via a charge amplifier or switched-capacitor ASIC.

### 2.2.4 MEMS Gyroscope Performance Limitations

- **Bias instability** dominated by electronics flicker noise (1/f).
- **Bias drift vs. temperature:** Mitigated with on-chip temperature sensors and polynomial compensation.
- **Scale-factor non-linearity:** typically 0.1 % – 1 % over the full-scale range.
- **g-sensitivity:** Coriolis signal contaminated by linear-acceleration cross-coupling.

---

## 2.3 MEMS Accelerometer — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Force Balance Equation

For a proof mass $m$ suspended by stiffness $k$ with damping $b$, the equation of motion under external acceleration $a$ is:

$$
m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m a
$$

In *steady state* ($\ddot{x} = \dot{x} = 0$):

$$
x_{ss} = -\frac{m a}{k}
$$

The proof-mass displacement $x_{ss}$ is linearly proportional to input acceleration.

### 2.3.2 Sensitivity

$$
S = \frac{\partial x_{ss}}{\partial a} = \frac{m}{k}
$$

To *increase* sensitivity: increase mass $m$ or decrease stiffness $k$. However, lowering $k$ reduces the natural frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \quad f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi}
$$

There is a classic **trade-off** between sensitivity and bandwidth.

### 2.3.3 Capacitive Sensing Equation

For a parallel-plate differential capacitor with $n$ fingers, area $A_f$, initial gap $g_0$:

$$
C_1 = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 A_f}{g_0 + x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 A_f}{g_0 - x}
$$

Differential output:

$$
\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, x}{g_0^2 - x^2} \approx \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, x}{g_0^2} \;\; (\text{for } x \ll g_0)
$$

### 2.3.4 Noise Performance — Brownian Noise Floor

The fundamental thermomechanical noise limit is:

$$
a_{noise} = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T \, \omega_n}{m \, Q}}
$$

where $k_B = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}\ \text{J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $Q$ is the quality factor. This sets the **noise floor** — no MEMS design can beat it.

---

## 2.4 ★ KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet ★

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarises every equation you MUST memorise for KTU University Exam questions from this module. Replace any `|` symbol with `\vert` if you write it in LaTeX context.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sagnac phase shift | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | N turns, A area, $\lambda$ wavelength | rad |
| 2 | Sagnac path difference | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 N A \Omega}{c}$ | Geometric path difference | m |
| 3 | Coriolis acceleration | $a_c = 2 \Omega v \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between $\vec{\Omega}$ and $\vec{v}$ | m/s² |
| 4 | Coriolis force on proof mass | $F_c = 2 m \Omega v_d$ | Mass, rate, drive velocity | N |
| 5 | Drive-mode displacement amplitude | $x_0 = \dfrac{F_d}{m\omega_d^2}$ | Drive force amplitude | m |
| 6 | Sense-mode displacement | $y(t) \approx \dfrac{2 \Omega x_0 Q_s}{\omega_0}$ | Proportional to $\Omega$ | m |
| 7 | Accelerometer static deflection | $x_{ss} = \dfrac{m a}{k}$ | $a$ = applied acceleration | m |
| 8 | Resonant frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ flexure stiffness, $m$ proof mass | rad/s |
| 9 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{b}$ | $b$ damping coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | Differential capacitance change | $\Delta C = \dfrac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f x}{g_0^2}$ | $n$ fingers, $A_f$ finger area | F |
| 11 | Brownian noise floor | $a_{noise} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}}$ | Thermomechanical limit | m/s²/√Hz |
| 12 | Angular rate to voltage scale-factor | $SF = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{\Omega_{FS}}$ | $V_{FS}$ full-scale output | V/(°/s) |
| 13 | Earth rotation rate | $\Omega_E = 15.041°/\text{hr} = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s | Reference for gyro testing | rad/s |
| 14 | Shupe drift rate | $\dot{\phi}_{shupe} \propto \dfrac{N}{L} \dfrac{dn}{dT} \dot{T}$ | Temperature-gradient bias | rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sagnac Phase Shift in a Rotating Fibre Coil

We derive the fundamental FOG equation **from first principles** so that it is fully reproducible in a 14-mark university exam answer.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a circular fibre loop of radius $R$, wound with $N$ turns, lying in a plane and rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane (the *sensitive axis*).

In the *rotating frame*, the loop has tangential velocity at radius $R$:

$$
v_{tang} = \Omega R
$$

### 3.1.2 Travel Time Difference

Let the loop circumference (one turn) be $C = 2\pi R$.

In the *lab (inertial) frame*, the beam travelling **with** the rotation must travel a path that is *stretched* by $v_{tang} \cdot t_{cw}$, and the beam travelling **against** the rotation is *compressed* by the same amount.

For the CW beam (co-moving), the effective speed in the lab frame is $(c/n - \Omega R)$ when the rotation tends to drag it backward (using relativistic addition in the limit $v \ll c$):

$$
t_{cw} = \frac{N \cdot 2\pi R}{\dfrac{c}{n} - \Omega R}
$$

For the CCW beam:

$$
t_{ccw} = \frac{N \cdot 2\pi R}{\dfrac{c}{n} + \Omega R}
$$

### 3.1.3 Time Difference

Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{1-x} \approx 1 + x + x^2 + \dots$ for $x = \dfrac{n \Omega R}{c} \ll 1$:

$$
t_{cw} = \frac{2\pi R N \, n}{c}\left(1 + \frac{n\Omega R}{c} + \dots\right)
$$

$$
t_{ccw} = \frac{2\pi R N \, n}{c}\left(1 - \frac{n\Omega R}{c} + \dots\right)
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\Delta t = t_{cw} - t_{ccw} = \frac{4\pi R^2 N \, n^2 \, \Omega}{c^2}
$$

### 3.1.4 Path Difference

The optical path difference (in terms of *vacuum-equivalent length*) is:

$$
\Delta L = c \cdot \Delta t = \frac{4\pi R^2 N \, n^2 \, \Omega}{c}
$$

Since the area of one loop is $A = \pi R^2$ and the index of the fibre is $n$:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta L = \frac{4 N A \, n^2 \, \Omega}{c}\;}
$$

If we work in vacuum ($n=1$) — valid since the phase is referenced to the vacuum wavelength:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{4 N A \Omega}{c}
$$

### 3.1.5 Phase Shift

The phase accumulated over an optical path $L$ is $\phi = \dfrac{2\pi L}{\lambda}$, so:

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \, \Delta L}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4 N A \Omega}{c}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\phi_{Sagnac} = \frac{8\pi N A}{\lambda c}\,\Omega\;}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Increasing $N$, $A$, or decreasing $\lambda$ directly increases sensitivity. This is why navigation-grade FOGs use *kilometres* of fibre (huge $N$) and small-wavelength telecom lasers (1550 nm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coriolis-Induced Sense Displacement in MEMS Gyroscope

### 3.2.1 Primary Drive Motion

The proof mass is driven by an electrostatic comb drive along the x-axis at its mechanical resonance $\omega_d$:

$$
x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_d t)
$$

So the velocity is:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)
$$

### 3.2.2 Coriolis Force on the Proof Mass

With input angular rate $\Omega$ applied about the z-axis:

$$
F_{Cor}(t) = -2 m \, \Omega \, \dot{x}(t) = 2 m \, \Omega \, x_0 \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)
$$

This force drives the sense-mode along the y-axis.

### 3.2.3 Sense-Mode Equation of Motion

The sense-mode resonator obeys:

$$
m\ddot{y} + b\dot{y} + k y = F_{Cor}(t)
$$

Define the natural frequency $\omega_s = \sqrt{k/m}$ and quality factor $Q_s = \dfrac{m \omega_s}{b}$. The steady-state response amplitude (driven at $\omega_d$ with $\omega_d = \omega_s$) is:

$$
y_0 = \frac{F_{Cor,0}}{m \omega_s^2 / Q_s} = \frac{F_{Cor,0} \, Q_s}{k}
$$

Substituting $F_{Cor,0} = 2 m \Omega x_0 \omega_d$:

$$
y_0 = \frac{2 m \Omega x_0 \omega_d \, Q_s}{m \omega_s^2} = \frac{2 \Omega x_0 \omega_d \, Q_s}{\omega_s^2}
$$

Since $\omega_d = \omega_s = \omega_0$ in matched mode:

$$
\boxed{\;y_0 = \frac{2 \Omega \, x_0 \, Q_s}{\omega_0}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sense displacement is proportional to (a) input rate $\Omega$, (b) drive amplitude $x_0$, and (c) sense-mode quality factor $Q_s$; and inversely proportional to resonant frequency $\omega_0$. This is the **engineering design equation** of every MEMS gyroscope.

### 3.2.4 Capacitive Readout Voltage

The capacitance change for $n$ sensing fingers is:

$$
\Delta C = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, y_0}{g_0^2}
$$

For a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_{fb}$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{\Delta C \cdot V_{ref}}{C_{fb}} = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, V_{ref}}{g_0^2 \, C_{fb}} \cdot y_0
$$

Combining with $y_0$:

$$
\boxed{\;V_{out} = \frac{4 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, V_{ref} \, Q_s \, x_0}{g_0^2 \, C_{fb} \, \omega_0}\,\Omega\;}
$$

This gives the **scale factor** (V per rad/s) of the MEMS gyroscope.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop FOG Scale Factor

In a **closed-loop (reciprocal) FOG**, an integrated-optic Y-branch phase modulator applies a non-reciprocal feedback phase $\phi_{fb}$ to null the Sagnac signal. Setting $\phi_{fb} = -\Delta\phi_{Sagnac}$:

$$
\phi_{fb} = -\frac{8\pi N A}{\lambda c}\,\Omega
$$

The applied feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ through a linearised modulator with $V_\pi$ half-wave voltage gives:

$$
V_{fb} = \frac{V_\pi}{\pi}\,\phi_{fb} = -\frac{8 N A \, V_\pi}{\lambda c}\,\Omega
$$

Therefore, the closed-loop scale factor is:

$$
\boxed{\;SF_{FOG} = \frac{V_{fb}}{\Omega} = \frac{8 N A \, V_\pi}{\lambda c}\;}
$$

The output is **digital** (a counter of $2\pi$ resets), which makes the closed-loop FOG scale factor **stable** and **linear** regardless of source power or detector gain — a key advantage over open-loop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MEMS Accelerometer Sensitivity

For a proof mass $m$ with suspension stiffness $k$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, the static deflection under acceleration $a$ is:

$$
x_{ss} = \frac{m a}{k} = \frac{a}{\omega_n^2}
$$

The full-scale acceleration $a_{FS}$ corresponds to a maximum allowed deflection $x_{max}$ (set by the mechanical stop gap $g_0$):

$$
a_{FS} = \omega_n^2 \, x_{max}
$$

**Design example (typical values):**
- $m = 1\,\mu$g = $10^{-9}$ kg
- $k = 1$ N/m
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{10^9} = 31{,}623$ rad/s ≈ 5 kHz
- $x_{max} = 1\,\mu$m = $10^{-6}$ m
- $a_{FS} = (31{,}623)^2 \cdot 10^{-6} \approx 1000$ m/s² ≈ **100 g**

The capacitive output sensitivity at full scale:

$$
\Delta C_{FS} = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, x_{max}}{g_0^2}
$$

For $n = 100$ fingers, $A_f = 2000\,\mu\text{m}^2$, $g_0 = 2\,\mu$m:

$$
\Delta C_{FS} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 8.854\times10^{-12} \times 2\times10^{-9}}{(2\times10^{-6})^2} \approx 8.85 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{F} \approx 0.885\,\text{fF}
$$

A very small signal — read out via low-noise ASIC.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example for KTU Exam Practice

> **Q:** A FOG has $N = 1000$ turns of fibre, coil radius $R = 0.05$ m, operating wavelength $\lambda = 1550$ nm. Calculate the Sagnac phase shift for an input rotation rate of 100°/hr.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
\Omega = 100°/\text{hr} = \frac{100 \pi}{180 \times 3600}\,\text{rad/s} = 4.848 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute coil area:**

$$
A = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Sagnac equation:**

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}
$$

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{8\pi \times 1000 \times 7.854\times10^{-3} \times 4.848\times10^{-4}}{1550\times10^{-9} \times 3\times10^{8}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
8\pi \times 1000 \times 7.854\times10^{-3} \times 4.848\times10^{-4} = 9.566 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Denominator:

$$
1550\times10^{-9} \times 3\times10^{8} = 0.465
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\phi \approx 0.2057\,\text{rad} \approx 11.79°\;}
$$

This is a comfortably detectable phase shift — illustrating why navigation-grade FOGs use very long fibre coils to amplify the Sagnac signal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fibre Optic Gyroscope — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Superluminescent Diode SLD Source 1550nm] --> B[Polariser]
    B --> C[3 dB Coupler Splitter]
    C --> D[Integrated Optic Phase Modulator Y Branch]
    D -->|CW Beam| E[Fibre Coil N turns area A]
    D -->|CCW Beam| E
    E --> D
    D --> F[Photodetector]
    F --> G[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    G --> H[Signal Processing DSP FPGA]
    H -->|Feedback Voltage| D
    H --> I[Digital Angular Rate Output deg per hr]

    classDef source fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef optical fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef sensing fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef electronics fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    class A source
    class B,C,D optical
    class E sensing
    class F,G,H,I electronics
```

**Architecture Walk-through:**
- The **SLD** (broadband source) suppresses Rayleigh-backscatter-induced coherent noise that would plague a narrow-linewidth laser.
- The **polariser** ensures single-polarisation operation (the reciprocal condition).
- The **integrated-optic Y-branch** acts as both splitter/combiner *and* the phase modulator.
- The **fibre coil** is the rotation-sensitive element.
- The **photodetector** converts the interference intensity into a current.
- The **DSP** closes the loop by applying a counter-phase to null the Sagnac signal.

---

## 4.2 MEMS Gyroscope — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensing_Element [MEMS Proof Mass Resonator]
        A1[Drive Mode X axis Resonant Oscillator f0] -->|Velocity v_x| A2[Coriolis Coupling Block]
        K[Angular Rate Input Omega z axis] --> A2
        A2 --> A3[Sense Mode Y axis Capacitive Pickoff]
    end

    A3 --> B1[Charge Amplifier ASIC]
    B1 --> B2[Synchronous Demodulator at f0]
    B2 --> B3[Low Pass Filter and Offset Trim]
    B3 --> B4[Temperature Compensation Polynomial]
    B4 --> C1[Digital SPI Output Angular Rate deg per s]

    A1 -.Drive Loop PLL.-> D1[PLL Auto Gain Control]
    D1 --> A1

    classDef mems fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef asic fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3 mems
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 asic
    class C1 output
    class D1,K control
```

**Architecture Walk-through:**
- The **drive loop (PLL)** keeps the proof mass oscillating at constant amplitude $x_0$ even as $Q_d$ drifts with temperature.
- **Coriolis coupling** converts in-plane velocity $\dot{x}$ and rotation $\Omega$ into an out-of-plane force.
- **Synchronous demodulation** at $f_0$ extracts the in-phase and quadrature components; the in-phase component is proportional to $\Omega$.
- **Temperature compensation** removes polynomial bias drift ($T$, $T^2$, $T^3$ terms).

---

## 4.3 MEMS Accelerometer — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Linear Acceleration a m per s2] --> B[Proof Mass m suspended by Flexure stiffness k]
    B -->|Displacement x = ma per k| C[Differential Capacitor n fingers gap g0]
    C -->|Delta C proportional to x| D[Low Noise Charge Amplifier]
    D --> E[Demodulator and Gain Stage]
    E --> F[Sigma Delta ADC 16 to 20 bit]
    F --> G[Digital Filter Decimation]
    G --> H[Output Acceleration g or m per s2 SPI I2C]

    subgraph Self_Test [Built in Self Test BIST]
        I[Electrostatic Actuator] -->|Force pulse| B
    end

    classDef input fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef mems fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef asic fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    class A input
    class B,C mems
    class D,E,F,G,H asic
    class I test
```

**Architecture Walk-through:**
- The **proof mass** is the only mechanically moving element.
- The **differential capacitor** rejects common-mode signals (e.g., temperature drift).
- The **BIST (Built-In Self-Test)** actuator applies a known electrostatic force to verify end-to-end functionality without external motion.
- The **ΣΔ ADC** provides high resolution (16-bit effective) within a small silicon area.

---

## 4.4 Comparative System-Level Trade-off Table (Board-Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SP[Solid State Fibre Optic Gyro FOG]
        SP1[High accuracy 0.001 deg per hr]
        SP2[Long coil 100m to 5km]
        SP3[Heavy and expensive]
    end

    subgraph MM[MEMS Gyro]
        MM1[Medium accuracy 1 to 10 deg per hr]
        MM2[Sub cm3 silicon die]
        MM3[Cheap CMOS compatible]
    end

    subgraph RG[Ring Laser Gyro RLG]
        RG1[Very high accuracy]
        RG2[Contains plasma and mirrors]
        RG3[Lock in problem]
    end

    FOG_DEC{Choose FOG when} -->|high accuracy and no moving parts| SP
    MEMS_DEC{Choose MEMS when} -->|low cost mass volume| MM
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Q2(a)]**
*Define the Sagnac effect and write the expression for the Sagnac phase shift in a fibre optic gyroscope. Identify two design parameters that increase the sensitivity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Sagnac effect is the phenomenon whereby two counter-propagating light beams traversing a closed rotating optical loop experience a *non-reciprocal* optical path difference, leading to a measurable phase shift proportional to the angular velocity of the loop.

$$
\Delta\phi_{Sagnac} = \frac{8\pi N A}{\lambda c}\,\Omega
$$

Two design parameters that increase sensitivity:
1. **Number of turns $N$** — increasing the winding count multiplies the enclosed area effectively; navigation-grade FOGs use $N$ up to several thousand (fibre length 1–5 km).
2. **Operating wavelength $\lambda$** — using a smaller wavelength increases phase per unit path difference; 1550 nm telecom-grade components are standard.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the design parameters.)*

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2(b)]**
*Explain the operating principle of a MEMS gyroscope. What physical effect does it exploit?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A MEMS gyroscope is a vibrating-structure angular rate sensor. A proof mass is electrostatically driven into resonant oscillation along a primary (drive) axis at a fixed frequency $f_d$. When the device experiences an angular rotation $\Omega$ about its sensitive axis, the proof mass — because of its linear momentum in the drive direction — experiences a **Coriolis pseudo-force** perpendicular to its velocity:

$$
\vec{F}_{Cor} = -2m\,(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})
$$

This force deflects the proof mass along a secondary (sense) axis. The sense displacement is proportional to $\Omega$ and is detected capacitively using interdigitated comb fingers. The MEMS device thus converts *angular rate* into a *capacitive (voltage) signal*.

*(Award 1 mark each for: drive-mode description, Coriolis principle, capacitive sense readout.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Many students write "FOG uses Sagnac effect" but **omit the formula** for $\Delta\phi$ — this costs 3 marks immediately.
> 2. Students frequently confuse **MEMS accelerometer** (uses $F = ma$, senses linear acceleration) with **MEMS gyroscope** (uses Coriolis force, senses angular rate). Marks are deducted for swapped definitions.
> 3. In derivations, students write the Sagnac phase shift but **do not show the time-difference step**. Always derive $\Delta t$ first, then convert to $\Delta L$, then to $\Delta\phi$.
> 4. For MEMS gyros, students forget to mention that the drive-mode and sense-mode are designed to have **matched resonant frequencies** for maximum sensitivity.
> 5. For accelerometers, students often forget to mention the **Brownian noise floor** which sets the fundamental performance limit — frequently asked in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ★ Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q11(a), Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Fibre Optic Gyroscope (FOG)**. Derive the expression for the Sagnac phase shift. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **reciprocity requirement** and explain the **Shupe effect**. How is the Shupe effect mitigated in practical FOG coils? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-A(a):

**Construction (block diagram – refer Section 4.1):**
A FOG consists of:
1. **Broadband light source (SLD)** at 1550 nm or 1310 nm — chosen for low temporal coherence to suppress backscatter noise.
2. **Polariser** — enforces single-polarisation to maintain reciprocity.
3. **3 dB coupler / Y-branch splitter** — divides source light into CW and CCW beams.
4. **Integrated-optic phase modulator** — applies serrodyne modulation for closed-loop nulling.
5. **Fibre sensing coil** — wound with $N$ turns of single-mode fibre enclosing area $A$.
6. **Photodetector** + **transimpedance amplifier** — converts interference into voltage.
7. **DSP / signal processor** — implements closed-loop algorithm and outputs angular rate.

**Working:**
- Light is split into two beams launched in opposite directions into the coil.
- A rotating coil introduces path-length difference; beams recombine at the coupler.
- The detector senses the interference intensity, which carries the Sagnac phase shift.
- The DSP drives the phase modulator with a feedback voltage that *nulls* the detected AC signal.
- The applied feedback voltage (or, equivalently, the digital phase count) is the **gyro output**.

**Derivation (refer Section 3.1):** For a coil of radius $R$, $N$ turns rotating at $\Omega$:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\phi_{Sagnac} = \frac{8\pi N A}{\lambda c}\,\Omega,\quad A = \pi R^2\;}
$$

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks working, 3 marks derivation.)*

#### Model Solution to B-A(b):

**Reciprocity Requirement:**
A *reciprocal* optical system gives identical transmission characteristics for light travelling in opposite directions — *in the absence of rotation*. The Sagnac phase is the only allowed non-reciprocal contribution. Any non-reciprocity from polarisation crosstalk, birefringence, or back-reflection appears as a **bias error** indistinguishable from rotation. Hence the FOG must be designed for strict reciprocity through:
- Single-polarisation operation (one polariser + polarisation-maintaining fibre).
- Symmetric splitter/combiner (Y-junction or 50:50 coupler).
- No Faraday rotators or time-varying elements in the loop.

**Shupe Effect:**
Time-varying temperature gradients along the fibre coil produce time-dependent index-of-refraction changes. Since the CW and CCW beams traverse the coil at slightly different times, they experience different indices, producing a spurious phase shift that mimics rotation:

$$
\Delta\phi_{Shupe}(t) \approx \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\frac{dn}{dT}\int_0^L \left[T(z,t) - T(z,t - \tau)\right]\,dz
$$

where $\tau$ is the loop transit time. This is the **dominant bias error** in high-precision FOGs.

**Mitigation strategies:**
1. **Symmetric winding patterns:** Dipole or quadrupole winding so that temporally-adjacent fibre lengths are placed at the same *spatial* location, making temperature changes equally affect CW and CCW beams.
2. **Thermal insulation / oven control:** Maintain the coil at constant temperature using a heater and proportional controller.
3. **Active temperature compensation:** Use multiple thermistors along the coil and apply a polynomial correction in the DSP.
4. **Selection of low $dn/dT$ fibre:** Use pure-silica-core fibre (e.g., SMF-28).

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks reciprocity concept, 2 marks Shupe effect equation/explanation, 3 marks mitigation strategies.)*

---

### ★ Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q11(b), Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of a MEMS gyroscope**. Derive the equation for the sense-mode displacement in terms of the input angular rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **MEMS capacitive accelerometer**. Derive the expression for static proof-mass deflection and explain the Brownian noise limit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-B(a):

**Principle (refer Section 2.2):**
A MEMS gyroscope is a 2-DOF vibrating structure. The proof mass is driven into resonant oscillation along the drive axis (x). When rotated, the Coriolis force deflects it along the sense axis (y). The sense displacement is read out capacitively.

**Derivation (refer Section 3.2):**
Drive motion:

$$
x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_d t) \implies \dot{x}(t) = -x_0 \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)
$$

Coriolis force:

$$
F_{Cor}(t) = 2 m \Omega x_0 \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)
$$

For matched resonant frequencies $\omega_d = \omega_s = \omega_0$, the steady-state sense amplitude is:

$$
\boxed{\;y_0 = \frac{2 \Omega x_0 Q_s}{\omega_0}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental MEMS gyro design equation**. Engineering levers:
- Increase $x_0$ (drive amplitude).
- Increase $Q_s$ (sense-mode quality factor, achieved by operating in vacuum).
- Decrease $\omega_0$ (lower stiffness).

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.2):** The drive loop (PLL), Coriolis coupling, and sense-mode ASIC chain are clearly described.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks principle, 4 marks derivation, 1 mark block diagram.)*

#### Model Solution to B-B(b):

**Construction (refer Section 4.3):**
A MEMS accelerometer consists of:
- A **silicon proof mass** suspended by compliant flexures (folded-beam or crab-leg design).
- **Interdigitated comb fingers** (fixed + movable) forming a differential capacitor.
- An **ASIC** containing charge amplifier, demodulator, and ADC.
- **BIST (Built-In Self-Test)** electrodes for in-situ functional verification.

**Working:**
An external acceleration $a$ produces an inertial force $-ma$ on the proof mass, deflecting it against the spring force. The deflection $x$ is detected capacitively. In steady state:

$$
\boxed{\;x_{ss} = \frac{m a}{k} = \frac{a}{\omega_n^2},\quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}\;}
$$

The differential capacitance change is:

$$
\Delta C = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 A_f \, x}{g_0^2}
$$

which is converted to a voltage by the ASIC and digitised.

**Brownian Noise Floor (refer Section 2.3.4):**
The fundamental thermomechanical limit of a MEMS accelerometer is:

$$
a_{noise} = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}}
$$

This arises from the equipartition of thermal energy $\tfrac{1}{2}k_B T$ in the proof-mass resonator. It cannot be reduced by better electronics — only by:
1. Increasing the **proof mass $m$**.
2. Increasing the **quality factor $Q$** (vacuum packaging).
3. Lowering the **resonant frequency $\omega_n$** — but this conflicts with bandwidth.

*Design Trade-off:* A high-bandwidth (high $\omega_n$) accelerometer has low noise floor penalty only if $m$ and $Q$ are simultaneously maximised — this is the central engineering challenge in MEMS accelerometer design.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks construction + block diagram, 2 marks working + deflection derivation, 3 marks noise floor + design trade-off.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Memory Anchor Points)**

### A. Fibre Optic Gyroscope (FOG)
- [ ] **Underlying physics:** Sagnac effect (1913).
- [ ] **Core equation:** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$.
- [ ] **Key design levers:** $N$ (turns), $A$ (area), $\lambda$ (wavelength), $c$ (constant).
- [ ] **Light source:** Superluminescent diode (SLD), 1550 nm preferred.
- [ ] **Operating modes:** Open-loop (analog) vs. closed-loop (digital, serrodyne nulling).
- [ ] **Main error source:** Shupe effect (temperature-gradient-induced phase drift).
- [ ] **Mitigation:** Symmetric (quadrupole) winding, thermal control, low $dn/dT$ fibre.
- [ ] **Reciprocity is mandatory** — single-polarisation operation.
- [ ] **Grades:** Tactical (1°/hr), Navigation (0.01°/hr), Strategic (0.001°/hr).
- [ ] **Application:** Aircraft INS, ship & submarine navigation, spacecraft AOCS.

### B. MEMS Gyroscope
- [ ] **Underlying physics:** Coriolis effect ($F = 2m\,\Omega\,v$).
- [ ] **Architecture:** 2-DOF resonator — drive mode (x) + sense mode (y).
- [ ] **Key equation:** $y_0 = \dfrac{2 \Omega x_0 Q_s}{\omega_0}$.
- [ ] **Operating modes:** Rate-mode (matched $\omega$) vs. whole-angle mode.
- [ ] **Readout:** Capacitive comb fingers → charge amplifier → synchronous demodulator.
- [ ] **Vacuum packaging essential** to maintain high $Q$.
- [ ] **Bias errors:** Bias instability (1/f noise), g-sensitivity, temperature drift.
- [ ] **Application:** UAV AHRS, smartphone IMU, micro-satellite ADCS.

### C. MEMS Accelerometer
- [ ] **Underlying physics:** Newton's 2nd law + spring-mass-damper.
- [ ] **Static deflection:** $x_{ss} = \dfrac{ma}{k} = \dfrac{a}{\omega_n^2}$.
- [ ] **Resonant frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.
- [ ] **Sensitivity:** $S = m/k$.
- [ ] **Readout:** Differential capacitive comb → ΣΔ ADC → digital output.
- [ ] **Brownian noise floor:** $a_{noise} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}}$.
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Sensitivity ↑ when $m$ ↑ and $k$ ↓; but bandwidth ↑ requires $k$ ↑.
- [ ] **BIST** = Built-In Self-Test using electrostatic actuator.
- [ ] **Application:** IMUs, airbag triggers, tilt sensing, vibration monitoring, gravity-gradient attitude determination.

### D. Key Constants to Memorise
- [ ] $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- [ ] $\lambda_{FOG} = 1550$ nm or 1310 nm
- [ ] $\Omega_{Earth} = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s = 15.041°/hr
- [ ] $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- [ ] $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

### E. Frequently-Tested Comparison Points (Likely 3-Mark Question)
- [ ] FOG vs. RLG vs. MEMS gyro — accuracy, size, cost, moving parts.
- [ ] MEMS gyro vs. MEMS accelerometer — physical effect (Coriolis vs. inertia), output (rate vs. acceleration), equation ($y_0 \propto \Omega$ vs. $x_{ss} \propto a$).
- [ ] Open-loop vs. closed-loop FOG — output linearity, dynamic range, scale-factor stability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Doppler Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT751 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING AND NAVIGATION INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pulse Doppler Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse Doppler Radar (PDR):** A coherent radar system that transmits a coherent train of RF pulses and uses **Doppler frequency shift** information extracted from the moving targets to detect them, while simultaneously rejecting echoes from stationary objects (clutter). It is a hybrid architecture combining the **range-measuring capability of pulse radar** with the **velocity-measuring capability of Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler radar**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context (PEICT751 – Aerospace Engineering and Navigation Instrumentation), Pulse Doppler Radar is classified as a **coherent MTI (Moving Target Indication) radar** that retains full **Doppler information** at the expense of accepting some range ambiguities, or vice versa, depending on the chosen Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) regime.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echolocation Traffic Police"

Imagine standing at a railway crossing with a flashlight (the radar's transmitted pulse) and a stopwatch.

- You flash the light at the train (transmit a pulse).
- The light bounces off the train and returns to you (echo return).
- The time taken tells you **how far the train is** (range measurement — pulse radar principle).
- Now, here is the clever part: the colour (frequency) of the reflected light has shifted slightly because the train is moving towards you. This is the **Doppler effect**.
- A stationary tree nearby also reflects light, but its colour does **not** shift — this lets you **ignore the tree and only see the train** (clutter rejection — CW Doppler principle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Doppler Radar = Pulse Radar (for range) + CW Doppler Radar (for velocity/clutter rejection), implemented coherently.**

A truly stationary object (clutter) reflects the signal with **zero Doppler shift**, while a moving target reflects it with a Doppler shift $f_d$ proportional to its radial velocity $v_r$. The receiver exploits this $f_d$ to suppress clutter.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following parameters are standard for any Pulse Doppler analysis in KTU examinations:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Operating frequency:** $f_0$ (typically **2–12 GHz** for surveillance, up to **94 GHz** for mmW aerospace seekers)
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f_0$
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** $f_r$ or PRF (Hz)
- **Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI):** $T_r = 1/\text{PRF}$ (seconds)
- **Duty cycle:** $\tau / T_r$ where $\tau$ is pulse width
- **Radial velocity range of interest:** typically **$\pm 5$ m/s to $\pm 1500$ m/s** for aerospace targets

## 1.4 Why Pulse Doppler Radar is Indispensable in Aerospace

In an aerospace scenario, the radar platform (fighter aircraft, AEW&C, missile seeker) is itself moving, and the targets of interest (other aircraft, missiles) are also moving. The background (ground, buildings) is stationary relative to the radar only if we consider ground-clutter.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Modern fighter radars (e.g., AN/APG-68, AN/APG-81) are predominantly Pulse Doppler radars because they can detect small moving targets (a 1 m² RCS missile) against a massive ground-clutter background (which may be 60–80 dB stronger).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The Pulse Doppler Radar operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Coherent Oscillator Generation (COHO):** A highly stable reference oscillator at IF frequency $f_{IF}$ is generated. This signal is the **phase reference** for all transmitted and received signals.
2. **Stable Local Oscillator (STALO):** A second oscillator at RF frequency $f_0$ is mixed with the COHO output to produce the **transmitted RF pulse** at carrier frequency $f_0 + f_{IF}$. The phase of every transmitted pulse is therefore **deterministically related** to the COHO — this is what makes the radar *coherent*.
3. **Pulse Transmission:** Pulses of width $\tau$ and repetition interval $T_r$ are radiated via the duplexer and antenna.
4. **Echo Reception:** Echoes from targets, ground, and clutter return to the antenna after a round-trip time $t_R = 2R/c$.
5. **Coherent Detection:** The received RF echo is mixed with the STALO and then with the COHO, producing an **I-channel (in-phase)** and **Q-channel (quadrature)** baseband video. This two-channel scheme is critical because it preserves the **sign of the Doppler shift**, which tells whether the target is approaching or receding.
6. **Doppler Filtering:** A bank of narrowband filters (FFT bank) separates targets by Doppler frequency. Stationary clutter appears at $f_d = 0$ and is rejected by an MTI high-pass filter.
7. **Detection & Display:** Targets exceeding the detection threshold are displayed on a **range-Doppler map** (intensity vs. range vs. velocity).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Coherent Detection

A non-coherent (magnitudes only) pulse radar cannot distinguish a target approaching at $+v_r$ from one receding at $-v_r$ — both give identical echo amplitudes. By retaining the I and Q components relative to a stable COHO, the receiver can measure the **phase change from pulse to pulse**, which is directly proportional to Doppler:

$$\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_d T_r = \frac{4\pi v_r T_r}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign of Doppler = Direction of radial motion.** This is the entire reason for I/Q processing in Pulse Doppler radars. KTU examiners love asking this conceptual question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every** equation you must memorize for Pulse Doppler problems. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Range of a target | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$ | metres; $t_R$ is round-trip time |
| 2 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | metres; depends on PRF only |
| 3 | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Hz; $v_r$ is radial velocity (m/s) |
| 4 | Phase change per PRI | $\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_d T_r$ | radians |
| 5 | Blind speed | $v_{blind} = \dfrac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}$ | m/s; $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 6 | First blind speed | $v_{b1} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}$ | m/s; occurs at $f_d = \text{PRF}/2$ |
| 7 | Maximum unambiguous Doppler (Nyquist) | $f_{d,max} = \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{2}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Maximum unambiguous velocity | $v_{r,max} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{4}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Single-delay-line canceller response | $H(f) = \vert 1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r} \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\pi f T_r) \vert$ | Dimensionless; MTI filter |
| 10 | Two-pulse MTI improvement factor | $I = 2 \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)_{input}$ | ratio (linear) |
| 11 | Clutter attenuation (single canceller) | $\text{CA} = \dfrac{1}{2 \sin^2(\pi f_d T_r)}$ | For $f_d = 0$, CA $\to \infty$ |
| 12 | PRF for unambiguous range $R_u$ | $\text{PRF} \le \dfrac{c}{2 R_u}$ | Hz |
| 13 | Staggered PRF ratio (for blind speed) | $f_{b,staggered} = f_{b1} \cdot \dfrac{\text{PRF}_{actual}}{\text{PRF}_{nominal}}$ | Extended via stagger |
| 14 | Range-Doppler coupling | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 15 | Time-bandwidth product | $\text{TBP} = B \cdot \tau$ | Dimensionless; pulse compression |

## 2.4 Three PRF Regimes — The Critical Engineering Trade-off

Pulse Doppler radars are classified by their PRF selection, and this is one of the most frequently tested KTU concepts:

| Regime | PRF Condition | Range Ambiguity | Velocity Ambiguity | Typical Use |
|--------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Low PRF (LPRF)** | PRF $\le 2 f_{d,max}$ | Unambiguous | Ambiguous (blind speeds) | Long-range surveillance, ground mapping |
| **Medium PRF (MPRF)** | $2 f_{d,max} <$ PRF $< c/(2R_{max})$ | Some ambiguity | Some ambiguity | Modern multi-mode fighter radars |
| **High PRF (HPRF)** | PRF $\ge c/(2R_{max})$ | Highly ambiguous | Unambiguous | Look-down/shoot-down against small targets in clutter |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Favourite:** "Why is a **high-PRF Pulse Doppler radar** preferred for a fighter aircraft's look-down mode?" — Answer: HPRF gives **unambiguous Doppler (velocity)** and places clutter at the **PRF line and its harmonics**, leaving the central Doppler region (where target velocities lie) clutter-free, achieving excellent **sub-clutter visibility (SCV)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airborne Intercept (AI) radars:** AN/APG-77 (F-22), AN/APG-81 (F-35), Rafal RBE2, Eurofighter CAPTOR-E.
- **AEW&C systems:** Beriev A-50, Boeing E-3 Sentry AWACS.
- **Missile seekers:** AIM-120 AMRAAM, Astra, MICA.
- **Weather radars:** NEXRAD (WSR-88D) uses Pulse Doppler to measure precipitation velocity.
- **Air-traffic control:** ASR-9, ASR-11 use MTI/PDR for primary surveillance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Doppler Frequency for a Moving Target

### Given:
- Radar transmits at carrier frequency $f_0$ (Hz)
- Target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ (m/s), positive when moving away
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

### Assumptions:
- Non-relativistic target ($v_r \ll c$)
- Far-field plane wave approximation

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** The target sees the incoming wave Doppler-shifted to frequency $f'$:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2:** The target re-radiates (scatters) the wave at $f'$. The radar, now being the receiver, sees a second Doppler shift on the return path:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Take the differential. The total Doppler shift is:

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \left[ \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right)^2 - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Expand using the binomial approximation valid for $v_r \ll c$:

$$\left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right)^2 = \left( 1 \pm \frac{v_r}{c} \right)^2 \approx 1 \pm \frac{2 v_r}{c}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract:

$$\Delta f = f_0 \left( 1 \pm \frac{2 v_r}{c} - 1 \right) = \pm \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c}$$

**Step 6:** Define the Doppler frequency (magnitude) using wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}}$

The factor of **2** appears because the Doppler effect happens **twice**: once on the way to the target, once on the way back.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Unambiguous Range from PRF

### Given:
- Two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$ produce echoes that arrive at times $t_1 = 2R_1/c$ and $t_2 = 2R_2/c$ relative to the most recent transmitted pulse.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** The radar can only unambiguously assign an echo to a specific transmitted pulse if the echo arrives **before the next pulse** is transmitted. The maximum allowable round-trip time is the PRI:

$$t_R^{max} = T_r = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the range equation $R = c t_R / 2$:

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c \cdot T_r}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}}$

An echo arriving later than $T_r$ will be interpreted as belonging to a later pulse, creating a **range ambiguity** (second-time-around echo).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blind Speed

### Given:
- A single-delay-line canceller (MTI filter) with response $H(f) = 1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r}$
- The canceller output is zero when $H(f) = 0$

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** Set the magnitude response to zero:

$$1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r} = 0 \implies e^{-j 2\pi f T_r} = 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply Euler's identity. The exponential equals 1 when its argument is a multiple of $2\pi$:

$$2\pi f T_r = 2\pi n \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the Doppler frequency:

$$f = \frac{n}{T_r} = n \cdot \text{PRF}$$

**Step 4:** A moving target with Doppler $f_d$ is "blind" when $f_d = n \cdot \text{PRF}$. But Nyquist sampling limits the visible Doppler to $\pm \text{PRF}/2$. The first blind speed corresponds to $f_d = \text{PRF}/2$:

$$f_{d,blind} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{2} \implies \frac{2 v_{blind}}{\lambda} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $v_{blind}$:

$$v_{blind} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{v_{blind} = \dfrac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}}$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

The first blind speed is obtained at $n = 1$. To avoid blind speeds, **staggered PRF** is used in practice.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Staggered PRF for Blind-Speed Elimination

### Given:
- Pulse repetition intervals $T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots$ varying around a nominal $T_0$

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** With nominal PRF $f_r = 1/T_0$, the first blind speed is $v_{b1} = \lambda f_r / 2$.

**Step 2:** With a stagger ratio $k_i = T_i / T_0$ (slightly different from 1), the canceller response becomes:

$$H(f) = 1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_i}$$

**Step 3:** A target moving at $v$ is blind when $f_d = n / T_i$ for some $n$, i.e., when:

$$v = \frac{n \lambda}{2 T_i}$$

**Step 4:** For the staggered system to be blind at this $v$, the condition must hold **simultaneously** for all $T_i$:

$$v = \frac{n_1 \lambda}{2 T_1} = \frac{n_2 \lambda}{2 T_2} = \frac{n_3 \lambda}{2 T_3}$$

**Step 5:** For non-trivial solutions, the integers $n_1, n_2, n_3$ must differ. The first common blind speed is pushed out to:

$$v_{b,staggered} = \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \text{lcm}\left(\frac{1}{T_1}, \frac{1}{T_2}, \frac{1}{T_3}\right)$$

**Step 6:** With three staggers in a ratio of **5:6:7** (a classic KTU/aerospace example), the first common blind speed is pushed out by a factor of about 30, effectively eliminating the blind-speed problem for typical aircraft velocities.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Staggered PRF is a high-yield topic. Always quote the trade-off: staggering eliminates blind speeds but **slightly degrades MTI improvement factor** and complicates the signal processor.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An X-band Pulse Doppler radar operates at $f_0 = 10$ GHz with PRF = 10 kHz. Find (a) the wavelength, (b) the Doppler shift for a target moving at $v_r = 300$ m/s, (c) the maximum unambiguous range, (d) the first blind speed, and (e) the maximum unambiguous velocity.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Part (b) — Doppler shift:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 300}{0.03} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10{,}000} = 15{,}000 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ km}$$

**Part (d) — First blind speed:**

$$v_{b1} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2} = \frac{0.03 \times 10{,}000}{2} = 150 \text{ m/s}$$

Since the target is moving at 300 m/s, and $300 = 2 \times 150$, this target is moving at the **second blind speed**! It will appear stationary to the single-delay-line canceller.

**Part (e) — Maximum unambiguous velocity:**

$$v_{r,max} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{4} = \frac{0.03 \times 10{,}000}{4} = 75 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The 300 m/s target is at the second blind speed, and is also beyond the maximum unambiguous velocity. This radar is operating in the **HPRF regime** — it has excellent velocity resolution but poor range unambiguity. To detect this target correctly, **staggered PRF or a PRF change** is mandatory.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Coherent Pulse Doppler Radar

The figure below shows the signal-flow architecture of a typical single-channel coherent Pulse Doppler radar. The COHO provides the phase reference, the STALO provides the RF carrier, and the I/Q demodulator preserves Doppler sign.

        A2 --> A4[ Duplexer\nTR Switch] --> A5[Antenna\nRadiating Element]

        B3 --> B4[Mixer 2\nIn-Phase Branch]

## 4.2 Doppler Filter Bank — Functional Layout

The FFT-based Doppler filter bank is the heart of the Pulse Doppler processor. Each filter isolates targets within a narrow velocity bin.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["INPUT I AND Q"]
        InI[In-Phase Samples\nI_n] --> InQ[Quadrature Samples\nQ_n]
    end

    Input --> FFT[Complex FFT\nBlock Size M Pulses]

    FFT --> F0[Filter 0\nfd = 0 Hz\nClutter Bin]
    FFT --> F1[Filter 1\nfd = PRF per M]
    FFT --> F2[Filter 2\nfd = 2 PRF per M]
    FFT --> FN[Filter N-1\nfd = M-1 PRF per M]

    F0 --> D[Detection\nLogic]
    F1 --> D
    F2 --> D
    FN --> D

    D --> RDM[Range Doppler Map\nOutput to Display]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q1.** Define Pulse Doppler Radar. Why is **coherent detection** essential in a Pulse Doppler radar but not strictly required in a conventional pulse radar used only for range measurement?

**Model Answer:**

> A Pulse Doppler Radar is a coherent radar system that transmits a coherent train of RF pulses and uses the **Doppler frequency shift** of moving targets for detection and clutter rejection, in addition to measuring range from pulse time delay.

> Coherent detection is essential because the receiver must compare the **phase** of successive echoes with a stable local reference (the COHO) to extract the small Doppler frequency shift. This phase comparison is impossible without a phase reference preserved from pulse to pulse.

> In a conventional non-coherent pulse radar (e.g., a simple range-only radar), only the **envelope** of the echo is detected, which gives range via time delay but discards all phase/Doppler information. Hence, no COHO is needed for range-only measurement.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition, 1 mark coherent explanation, 1 mark non-coherent comparison]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q2.** What is **blind speed** in the context of a Pulse Doppler radar with a single-delay-line MTI canceller? Write the expression for the first blind speed and explain how **staggered PRF** mitigates the problem.

**Model Answer:**

> **Blind speed:** A radial velocity of a target at which the MTI canceller output becomes zero, making the target invisible to the radar even though it is physically present.

> **Expression for the first blind speed:**

$$v_{b1} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}$$

> **Staggered PRF mitigation:** By varying the PRI in a pre-set pattern (e.g., 5:6:7 ratio), the blind speeds for each PRI are different. A target is therefore blind only when it is simultaneously blind at **all** staggered PRFs, which pushes the first common blind speed to a much higher (often unreachable) value, effectively eliminating the problem for the velocity range of interest.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark stagger explanation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry **14 marks** each, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives below follow this exact pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q.A(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **coherent Pulse Doppler radar**. Clearly bring out the role of the COHO, STALO, and the I/Q demodulator. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A(b)** An S-band Pulse Doppler radar operates at $f_0 = 3$ GHz with a PRF of 1 kHz. A target is closing at a radial velocity of 200 m/s. Calculate: (i) the wavelength, (ii) the Doppler frequency, (iii) the maximum unambiguous range, and (iv) whether the target lies within the maximum unambiguous velocity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a):

**Block diagram** — refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the full schematic.

**Operation explanation (7 marks):**

1. **STALO** generates a stable RF signal at $f_0$. It feeds both the transmitter (via the pulsed amplifier) and the receiver mixer. **(1 mark)**
2. **COHO** generates a stable IF reference at $f_{IF}$. The transmitted pulse is derived by mixing STALO + COHO, so the phase of every transmitted pulse is **coherently related** to the COHO. **(1 mark)**
3. The pulsed RF signal is routed through the **duplexer (TR switch)** to the antenna, which radiates it. **(1 mark)**
4. Echoes return via the same antenna, are amplified by the **LNA**, and mixed with the STALO to bring the echo down to IF. **(1 mark)**
5. The IF signal is then split into **two parallel branches**: one mixed directly with the COHO (giving the **I channel**) and one mixed with the COHO shifted by **90°** (giving the **Q channel**). **(1 mark)**
6. The I/Q samples are integrated over the pulse width (integrate-and-dump), producing complex baseband samples that retain the sign of the Doppler shift. **(1 mark)**
7. The complex samples pass through the **MTI canceller** (clutter rejection) and the **FFT filter bank** (Doppler separation) and are presented on a **range–Doppler map**. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b):

**Given:** $f_0 = 3$ GHz, PRF = 1 kHz, $v_r = 200$ m/s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^9} = 0.1 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Doppler frequency:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 200}{0.1} = 4{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1{,}000} = 150 \text{ km}$$

**(iv) Maximum unambiguous velocity:**

$$v_{r,max} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{4} = \frac{0.1 \times 1{,}000}{4} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

Since $v_r = 200$ m/s is **8 times** $v_{r,max} = 25$ m/s, the target is **highly ambiguous in velocity** and will be folded multiple times. The radar is operating in the **LPRF (Low PRF) regime**, suitable for unambiguous range but poor for velocity measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.A(b)):** Many students forget that the maximum unambiguous **Doppler frequency** is **PRF/2** (Nyquist), not PRF. A common mistake is writing $v_{r,max} = \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}/2$, which is actually the **first blind speed**, not the unambiguous velocity. This will cost **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q.B(a)** Derive the expression for the **Doppler frequency shift** in a monostatic radar. What is the significance of the **factor of 2**? **(7 marks)**

**Q.B(b)** With reference to a Pulse Doppler radar, explain the three **PRF regimes** (LPRF, MPRF, HPRF) and their relative merits for airborne radar applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a):

**Derivation** — refer to SECTION 3.1 above for the full 6-step derivation.

**Significance of the factor of 2:**

In a monostatic radar (transmitter and receiver co-located), the Doppler shift occurs **twice**:

- First, on the **outward journey**, the moving target sees the carrier frequency shifted by $\pm v_r f_0 / c$.
- Second, on the **return journey**, the target re-radiates (or scatters) at the already-shifted frequency, and the **stationary** radar sees a second shift of $\pm v_r f_0 / c$.

The two shifts add because the relative motion is the same on both legs, giving the total Doppler shift:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

If the radar were **bistatic** (transmitter and receiver separated), the factor of 2 would be replaced by $(cos\theta_1 + cos\theta_2)$, where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the angles between the target velocity vector and the transmitter–target / target–receiver lines, respectively. **(1 mark for bistatic mention)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b):

The three PRF regimes are classified by comparing the **Doppler unambiguous limit** ($\text{PRF}/2$) and the **range unambiguous limit** ($c/(2 \text{PRF})$) with the maximum expected Doppler $f_{d,max}$ and maximum range $R_{max}$:

| Regime | Condition | Range | Doppler | Aerospace Application |
|--------|-----------|-------|---------|------------------------|
| **LPRF** | $\text{PRF} \le 2 f_{d,max}$ | Unambiguous | Ambiguous (blind speeds) | Long-range surveillance, AWACS, ground mapping |
| **MPRF** | $2 f_{d,max} < \text{PRF} < c/(2R_{max})$ | Slightly ambiguous | Slightly ambiguous | Modern fighter radars (F-16, Rafale) — flexible multi-mode operation |
| **HPRF** | $\text{PRF} \ge c/(2R_{max})$ | Highly ambiguous | Unambiguous | Look-down/shoot-down against small targets in heavy ground clutter (e.g., AN/APG-77) |

**Detailed explanation:**

1. **LPRF (Low PRF):** Long maximum unambiguous range. Suitable when the radar platform is stationary or when the target is at very long range. However, blind speeds appear and clutter return is significant, making it poor against small moving targets in clutter. **(2 marks)**

2. **MPRF (Medium PRF):** A compromise — both range and velocity are partially ambiguous. Modern fighters use multiple PRFs in a **staggered burst pattern** to resolve the residual ambiguities. MPRF is the most flexible, supporting both air-to-air and air-to-ground modes. **(2.5 marks)**

3. **HPRF (High PRF):** Excellent **sub-clutter visibility** because clutter is folded into narrow bands at multiples of the PRF, while the central Doppler region (where target velocities lie) is clutter-free. This is critical for **look-down** scenarios where the radar is flying above the ground. The penalty is severe range ambiguity. **(2.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B(b)):** Students often confuse **range ambiguity** with **velocity ambiguity**. Remember:
> - **High PRF** → unambiguous **velocity**, ambiguous **range**.
> - **Low PRF** → unambiguous **range**, ambiguous **velocity**.
> - This is the reverse of what many students guess! The mnemonic is: **"High PRF measures the speed of light's travel time imperfectly (range ambiguous), but measures the Doppler well."**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Pulse Doppler Radar (KTU 2024 PEICT751 Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Coherent radar combining pulse ranging + CW Doppler velocity measurement.
- **Key building blocks:** COHO (phase reference), STALO (RF carrier), Duplexer, LNA, I/Q demodulator, MTI canceller, FFT Doppler bank, CFAR detector.
- **Doppler equation:** $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = 2 v_r f_0 / c$. The factor of 2 is due to the round-trip in monostatic radar.
- **Unambiguous range:** $R_{unamb} = c / (2 \text{PRF})$. Inversely proportional to PRF.
- **Unambiguous velocity:** $v_{r,max} = \lambda \text{PRF} / 4$. Proportional to PRF.
- **Blind speed:** $v_{b,n} = n \lambda \text{PRF} / 2$. Eliminate via **staggered PRF** (e.g., 5:6:7 ratio).
- **Single-delay-line canceller response:** $\vert H(f) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\pi f T_r) \vert$. Zero at $f = n \cdot \text{PRF}$.
- **Three PRF regimes:** LPRF (range unambiguous, blind speeds), MPRF (compromise), HPRF (velocity unambiguous, range ambiguous, look-down friendly).
- **I/Q processing** is mandatory to determine **sign of Doppler** (approaching vs. receding).
- **Staggered PRF trade-off:** Eliminates blind speeds, but slightly degrades MTI improvement factor and increases signal-processing complexity.
- **Modern aerospace use:** F-22 APG-77, F-35 APG-81, Rafale RBE2, Eurofighter CAPTOR-E, AWACS, AMRAAM seeker.
- **Range–Doppler map:** The standard display; intensity vs. range (fast time) vs. Doppler (slow time / FFT bin).
- **Clutter rejection** comes from the MTI high-pass filter, which has nulls at $f_d = 0$ and at multiples of the PRF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
